title: Are Multimodal LLMs Ready for Clinical Dermatology? A Real-World Evaluation in Dermatology
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated promise on publicly available dermatology benchmarks. However, benchmark performance may not generalize to real-world dermatologic decision-making. To quantify this benchmark-to-bedside gap, we evaluated four open-weight MLLMs (InternVL-Chat v1.5, LLaVA-Med v1.5, SkinGPT4 and MedGemma-4B-Instruct) and one commercial MLLM (GPT-4.1) across three publicly available dermatology datasets and a retrospective multi-site hospital-based dermatology consultation cohort comprising 5,811 cases and 46,405 clinical images. Models were evaluated on two clinically relevant tasks: differential diagnosis generation and severity-based triage. Diagnostic performance was modest on public datasets and declined substantially in the real-world cohort. On public benchmarks, top-3 diagnostic accuracy reached 26.55% for the best open-weight model and 42.25% for GPT-4.1. On real-world consultation cases using images alone, top-3 diagnostic accuracy fell to 1.50%-13.35% among open-weight models and 24.65% for GPT-4.1. Incorporating clinical context improved performance across all models, increasing top-3 diagnostic accuracy up to 28.75% among open-weight models and 38.93% for GPT-4.1. However, model outputs were highly sensitive to incomplete or erroneous consultation context. For severity-based triage, models achieved moderate sensitivity (above 60%), suggesting potential utility for screening but insufficient reliability for clinical deployment. These findings demonstrate that benchmark performance substantially overestimates the real-world clinical capability of current dermatology MLLMs.

AI.tldr: Multimodal LLMs perform much worse on real-world dermatology cases than on public benchmarks, with top-3 diagnostic accuracy dropping from ~42% (GPT-4.1) on benchmarks to ~25% in hospital settings, and open-weight models falling below 14% with images alone.
AI.motivation: To quantify the gap between benchmark performance and real-world clinical utility of MLLMs in dermatology, given that prior evaluations may overestimate actual clinical capability.
AI.method: Evaluated 5 MLLMs (4 open-weight, 1 commercial) on 3 public datasets + a multi-site hospital cohort of 5,811 cases with 46,405 images, testing differential diagnosis generation and severity-based triage performance.
AI.result: Benchmark top-3 accuracy: best open-weight 26.55%, GPT-4.1 42.25%. Real-world with images only: open-weight 1.50%-13.35%, GPT-4.1 24.65%. Adding clinical context improved to 28.75% (open-weight) and 38.93% (GPT-4.1), but models were sensitive to incomplete/erroneous context. Triage sensitivity >60% but insufficient for deployment.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark performance substantially overestimates real-world clinical capability of current dermatology MLLMs; models are far from reliable for clinical deployment despite moderate triage sensitivity.

title: MuCALD-SplitFed: Causal-Latent Diffusion for Privacy-Preserving Multi-Task Split-Federated Medical Image Segmentation
comment: Accepted to oral presentation and conference proceedings at IEEE International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP 2026), Finland
summary: Federated Learning enables decentralized training by aggregating model updates across clients without sharing raw data, while Split Federated Learning further partitions the model between clients and a server to reduce computation and communication at the client side. However, decentralized medical institutions rarely operate on a single shared task, making standard Federated and SplitFed collaborations poorly aligned with real clinical workflows. Multi-task FL extends these frameworks by allowing clients to handle different tasks, but often introduces instability and privacy vulnerabilities. This study proposes \textbf{MuCALD-SplitFed}, a multi-task SplitFed framework that integrates causal representation learning and latent diffusion. Experiments show MuCALD-SplitFed consistently improves segmentation, while baseline SplitFed fails to converge. The proposed approach further reduces information leakage at split points, mitigating reconstruction-based and membership inference attacks. Additionally, MuCALD SplitFed outperforms state-of-the-art personalized FL and multi-task FL approaches. The code repository is: https://github.com/ChamaniS/MuCALD_SplitFed.

AI.tldr: MuCALD-SplitFed is a multi-task Split Federated Learning framework using causal representation learning and latent diffusion to improve medical image segmentation, convergence stability, and privacy protection against reconstruction and membership inference attacks.
AI.motivation: Medical institutions rarely share a single task, making standard Federated Learning and Split Federated Learning misaligned with real clinical workflows. Multi-task FL can handle different tasks but suffers from instability and privacy vulnerabilities.
AI.method: Proposes MuCALD-SplitFed, a multi-task SplitFed framework that integrates causal representation learning and latent diffusion to stabilize training and enhance privacy.
AI.result: MuCALD-SplitFed consistently improves segmentation performance where baseline SplitFed fails to converge. It reduces information leakage at split points, mitigating reconstruction-based and membership inference attacks, and outperforms state-of-the-art personalized FL and multi-task FL approaches.
AI.conclusion: Causal representation learning combined with latent diffusion effectively addresses multi-task instability and privacy leakage in Split Federated Learning for decentralized medical imaging.

title: Topology-Constrained Quantized nnUNet for Efficient and Anatomically Accurate 3D Tooth Segmentation
summary: We propose a topology-constrained quantized nnUNet framework for efficient and anatomically accurate 3D tooth segmentation, addressing the challenges of spatial distortion introduced by quantization in deep learning models. The proposed method integrates a novel tooth-specific topological loss into quantization-aware training, preserving critical anatomical structures such as tooth count, adjacency relationships, and cavity integrity while maintaining computational efficiency. The system employs an 8-bit quantized nnUNet backbone, where weights and activations are dynamically calibrated to minimize precision loss during inference. Furthermore, the topological loss combines connected-component analysis, adjacency consistency, and hole detection penalties, ensuring anatomical fidelity without modifying the underlying network architecture. The joint optimization objective harmonizes cross-entropy loss, quantization regularization, and topological constraints, enabling end-to-end training with gradient approximations for persistent homology terms. Experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly reduces topological errors compared to conventional quantized models, achieving clinically plausible segmentations on dental CBCT scans. The method retains the hardware efficiency of integer-only inference, making it suitable for deployment in resource-constrained clinical environments. This work bridges the gap between computational efficiency and anatomical precision in medical image segmentation, offering a practical solution for real-world dental applications.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a topology-constrained quantized nnUNet framework for 3D tooth segmentation that preserves anatomical structures (tooth count, adjacency, cavity integrity) using an 8-bit quantized backbone with a novel tooth-specific topological loss, achieving clinically plausible segmentations on CBCT scans while maintaining hardware efficiency for resource-constrained environments.
AI.motivation: To address spatial distortion introduced by quantization in deep learning models for 3D tooth segmentation, bridging the gap between computational efficiency and anatomical precision in medical image segmentation for real-world dental applications.
AI.method: Integrates a tooth-specific topological loss (combining connected-component analysis, adjacency consistency, and hole detection penalties) into quantization-aware training with an 8-bit quantized nnUNet backbone, where weights/activations are dynamically calibrated. Joint optimization uses cross-entropy loss, quantization regularization, and topological constraints with gradient approximations for persistent homology.
AI.result: Significantly reduces topological errors compared to conventional quantized models, achieving clinically plausible segmentations on dental CBCT scans while retaining integer-only inference efficiency for deployment.
AI.conclusion: The topology-constrained quantized nnUNet offers a practical solution balancing computational efficiency and anatomical accuracy, suitable for resource-constrained clinical environments.

title: Anatomy of a failure: When, how, and why deep vision fails in scientific domains
summary: Mirroring its ubiquity in popular media and all human activities, the use of deep learning (DL) is rapidly growing in scientific imaging modalities. However, unlike everyday RGB pictures, pixels encode precise physicochemical properties in scientific imaging across potentially thousands of channels. While DL is well validated on human-centric RGB perceptual tasks, its effectiveness for scientific imaging remains uncertain. Here, we show that the naive application of DL frameworks to scientific images can lead to critical failures. We evaluate the use of DL for pathology, comparing RGB images of stained tissue with the quantitative and information-rich biochemical signatures of infrared (IR) imaging. Despite this informational advantage, DL models trained on IR data paradoxically underperform. We investigate this discrepancy to find that IR data priors interact poorly with the simplicity bias of DL, causing models to collapse to one-dimensional predictions. This constitutes a catastrophic DL failure because the model's representational capacity remains largely unused, while furthermore raising AI safety concerns and undermining the advantages of such scientific modalities. Notably, this problem persists even with state-of-the-art DL robustification strategies, which are primarily designed and validated for RGB imagery and thus inherit the same prior-bias mismatch. This work establishes a framework for understanding the limitations of generic DL in science and advocates for the study of modality-specific failure modes to guide the development of specialized, safe AI algorithms.

AI.tldr: The naive application of deep learning to scientific imaging (e.g., infrared tissue data) can paradoxically underperform compared to standard RGB-based models, due to a mismatch between data priors and DL's simplicity bias, causing model collapse to one-dimensional predictions even with robustification strategies.
AI.motivation: DL is widely used in scientific imaging, but its effectiveness is uncertain since scientific images encode precise physicochemical properties across many channels, unlike human-centric RGB images. The authors aim to investigate whether standard DL frameworks work reliably for scientific modalities.
AI.method: The authors compare DL models trained on RGB images of stained tissue versus quantitative infrared (IR) imaging data for pathology tasks. They analyze why IR-based models underperform despite having richer information, and test state-of-the-art robustification strategies.
AI.result: IR data priors interact poorly with DL's simplicity bias, causing models to collapse to one-dimensional predictions, leaving most representational capacity unused. This problem persists even with robustification strategies designed for RGB imagery.
AI.conclusion: Generic DL frameworks can catastrophically fail on scientific imaging due to prior-bias mismatch. The authors advocate for studying modality-specific failure modes to develop specialized, safe AI algorithms for scientific applications.

title: Hyperspectral Anomaly Detection Using Einstein Fuzzy Computing and Quantum Neural Network
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
summary: In the remote sensing (RS) field, hyperspectral imagery provides rich spectral information and facilitates numerous critical applications, such as material identification. Among these applications, hyperspectral anomaly detection (HAD) aims to detect substances whose spectral characteristics deviate from background spectra, which are termed anomalies. However, many widely used HAD algorithms in the RS community identify anomalies by relying on a ``background reconstruction'' strategy. Furthermore, the lack of prior target hyperspectrum and real-world limitations collectively reduces the spectral discrepancy between anomaly and background, limiting the performance of mainstream detections. By exploring the widely applicable fuzzy theory in the RS field, this study develops an unsupervised hybrid quantum-fuzzy multi-criteria decision framework (HyFuHAD) to detect anomalies from multiple perspectives. In our HyFuHAD, each pixel is first fuzzified using multiple HAD-based membership functions (MFs), including morphological, geometrical, and statistical MFs, to obtain various types of fuzzy degrees. Then, a multi-fuzzy-rule system, empowered by Einstein fuzzy computing, infers the classical fuzzy detection from these fuzzy degrees with sub-second-level computing. The Einstein sum and product provide significantly smoother transitions compared to typical min-max-based fuzzy ``OR'' and ``AND'' during the fuzzy matching and inference steps, thereby enabling effective detections. Moreover, a lightweight quantum defuzzifier obtains the quantum fuzzy detection from fuzzy features derived from the proposed fuzzy feature aggregation network. Experiments demonstrate that our HyFuHAD algorithm achieves state-of-the-art performance by fusing the information from the quantum and classical detectors. The demo code will be publicly available at https://github.com/IHCLab/HyFuHAD.

AI.tldr: HyFuHAD is an unsupervised hybrid quantum-fuzzy multi-criteria framework for hyperspectral anomaly detection that fuzzifies pixels using multiple membership functions, applies Einstein fuzzy computing for smoother inference, and combines classical and quantum fuzzy detection to achieve state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing HAD methods rely on background reconstruction and suffer from reduced spectral discrepancy between anomalies and background due to lack of prior target spectra and real-world limitations, which limits detection performance.
AI.method: HyFuHAD fuzzifies each pixel using morphological, geometrical, and statistical membership functions; applies a multi-fuzzy-rule system with Einstein fuzzy computing (Einstein sum/product for smoother transitions); and uses a lightweight quantum defuzzifier from a fuzzy feature aggregation network to combine classical and quantum detection outputs.
AI.result: HyFuHAD achieves state-of-the-art performance by fusing information from quantum and classical detectors, with sub-second-level computing time.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hybrid quantum-fuzzy framework effectively addresses limitations of mainstream HAD methods and achieves superior anomaly detection through multi-perspective fuzzy fusion.

title: From Language to Logic: A Theoretical Architecture for VLM-Grounded Safe Navigation
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, to be published in ICUAS 2026 conference proceedings
summary: We propose an architecture for integrating high-level, human-provided safety rules and operator-aligned semantic preferences into autonomous robot navigation in unstructured outdoor environments. In our approach, natural-language rules are translated into Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications that guide planning and navigation during runtime. Persistent, environment-centric rules and terrain preferences are grounded into a 2D cost map, while temporally dynamic requirements are expressed as STL specifications to be monitored during runtime. We hypothesize the use of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for zero-shot scene understanding, enabling mapping between human instructions, semantic features, and environmental constraints. Within this framework, we construct an illustrative navigation model that is designed to satisfy a set of STL-encoded specifications and soft operator preferences through formal satisfaction metrics embedded into environmental properties and runtime monitoring.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework that integrates natural-language safety rules and semantic preferences into autonomous robot navigation in outdoor environments by translating human instructions into Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications, grounding persistent rules into cost maps, and using Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for zero-shot scene understanding.
AI.motivation: To enable autonomous robots to safely navigate unstructured outdoor environments while adhering to high-level human-provided safety rules and operator-aligned semantic preferences, bridging the gap between natural-language instructions and formal navigation constraints.
AI.method: The approach translates natural-language rules into STL specifications; persistent rules are grounded into a 2D cost map, while dynamic requirements are monitored at runtime. VLMs are used for zero-shot scene understanding to map human instructions to semantic features and environmental constraints. Formal satisfaction metrics are embedded into environmental properties and runtime monitoring.
AI.result: The paper constructs an illustrative navigation model designed to satisfy STL-encoded specifications and soft operator preferences using formal satisfaction metrics, though no empirical results are presented in this abstract.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture effectively integrates human-provided rules and preferences into autonomous navigation by combining STL-based formal methods with VLM-driven scene understanding, enabling safe and semantically aligned robot behavior in unstructured outdoor environments.

title: Endogenous Regime Switching Driven by Scalar-Irreducible Learning Dynamics
summary: Achieving endogenous regime switching is crucial for the emergence of autonomous intelligence, yet remains a central challenge for existing machine learning frameworks, where such transitions are typically externally imposed. In this work, we introduce a classification that distinguishes scalar-reducible dynamics, which can be expressed as gradient flows driven by a scalar objective, from scalar-irreducible dynamics that cannot be reduced to such a form. While most existing machine learning systems operate within the scalar-reducible class, we demonstrate that scalar-irreducible dynamics naturally enable internally generated regime switching through feedback between fast dynamical variables and slow structural adaptation. Using a minimal dynamical model, we illustrate how this mechanism produces sustained endogenous regime transitions without external scheduling. Our results suggest a new dynamical paradigm for regime exploration and provide a potential route toward autonomous learning systems whose adaptive behavior is organized internally rather than externally prescribed.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a classification of dynamics into scalar-reducible (gradient-driven) and scalar-irreducible types, showing that scalar-irreducible dynamics enable endogenous regime switching without external scheduling, offering a path toward autonomous learning systems.
AI.motivation: Achieving endogenous regime switching is crucial for autonomous intelligence, but existing ML systems rely on externally imposed transitions, which limits autonomy.
AI.method: The authors propose a classification of dynamics (scalar-reducible vs. scalar-irreducible) and use a minimal dynamical model to demonstrate how feedback between fast variables and slow structural adaptation enables internally generated regime switching.
AI.result: Scalar-irreducible dynamics naturally produce sustained endogenous regime transitions without external scheduling, unlike scalar-reducible systems (e.g., gradient descent) that dominate current ML.
AI.conclusion: This work suggests a new dynamical paradigm for regime exploration and a potential route toward autonomous learning systems whose behavior is internally organized rather than externally prescribed.

title: LCM: Lossless Context Management
summary:
We introduce Lossless Context Management (LCM), a deterministic architecture for LLM memory that outperforms Claude Code on long-context tasks. When benchmarked using Opus 4.6, our LCM-augmented coding agent, Volt, achieves higher scores than Claude Code on the OOLONG long-context eval, including at every context length between 32K and 1M tokens.
  LCM may be considered both a vindication and extension of the recursive paradigm pioneered by Recursive Language Models (RLMs). Our results demonstrate that recursive context manipulation can outperform not just conventional LLMs, but frontier coding agents with native file-system access.
  LCM departs from RLM by decomposing symbolic recursion into two deterministic, engine-managed mechanisms: recursive context compression, in which a hierarchical summary DAG automatically compacts older messages while retaining lossless pointers to every original; and recursive task partitioning, in which engine-managed parallel primitives like LLM-Map replace model-written loops. This trade-off, analogous to the move from GOTO to structured control flow in program-ming language design, sacrifices maximal flexibility for termination guarantees, zero-cost continuity on short tasks, and lossless retrievability of all prior state.

AI.tldr: LCM introduces a deterministic architecture for LLM memory that improves long-context performance through recursive context compression (with lossless pointers) and recursive task partitioning (engine-managed parallel primitives), outperforming Claude Code on long-context benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of conventional LLMs and frontier coding agents in handling long-context tasks, by providing a more structured and deterministic approach to context management.
AI.method: LCM decomposes symbolic recursion into two mechanisms: (1) recursive context compression using a hierarchical summary DAG with lossless pointers, and (2) recursive task partitioning using engine-managed parallel primitives like LLM-Map.
AI.result: The LCM-augmented agent (Volt) achieves higher scores than Claude Code on the OOLONG long-context eval across all context lengths from 32K to 1M tokens.
AI.conclusion: Recursive context manipulation can outperform both conventional LLMs and frontier coding agents; the structured control flow approach (analogous to GOTO-to-structured-programming transition) sacrifices flexibility for termination guarantees, zero-cost continuity on short tasks, and lossless retrievability.

title: Disentangled Learning Improves Implicit Neural Representations for Medical Reconstruction
summary: Implicit neural representations (INRs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for medical imaging via physics-informed unsupervised learning. Classical INRs optimize an entire network from scratch for each subject, leading to inefficient training and suboptimal imaging quality. Recent initialization-based approaches attempt to inject population priors into pre-trained networks, yet they rely on high-quality images and often suffer from catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning. We present DisINR, a novel INR framework that explicitly disentangles shared and subject-specific representations. DisINR introduces a shared encoder-decoder pair and subject-specific encoders, whose features are jointly decoded for image reconstruction. By integrating differentiable forward models, it pre-trains the shared modules directly from limited raw measurements, removing the need for pre-acquired high-quality images. During test-time adaptation, only the subject-specific encoder is optimized, while the shared pair remains frozen, effectively preserving learned priors. Extensive evaluations on three representative medical imaging tasks show that DisINR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art INRs in both reconstruction accuracy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: DisINR disentangles shared and subject-specific representations for implicit neural representations in medical imaging, enabling efficient pre-training from raw measurements and test-time adaptation with frozen shared modules, outperforming prior methods.
AI.motivation: Classical INRs optimize from scratch per subject (inefficient), and initialization-based approaches rely on high-quality images and suffer catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning.
AI.method: DisINR uses a shared encoder-decoder pair plus subject-specific encoders, pre-trains shared modules from raw measurements via differentiable forward models, and at test-time only optimizes the subject-specific encoder while freezing shared modules.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art INRs in both reconstruction accuracy and efficiency across three medical imaging tasks.
AI.conclusion: DisINR effectively preserves learned priors via frozen shared modules, removes reliance on high-quality pre-acquired images, and achieves superior performance.

title: External Validation of Deep Learning Models for BI-RADS Breast Density Prediction from Ultrasound Images
comment: Accepted at the 18th International Workshop on Breast Imaging (IWBI 2026)
summary: We externally validated three deep learning models (DenseNet121, ViT-B/32, and ResNet50) for predicting mammographic breast density from breast ultrasound exams on an independent cohort. The external validation set comprised 2,000 ultrasound exams, including 500 cancer cases defined by an initial negative exam (BI-RADS 1 or 2) followed by a cancer diagnosis within 6 months to 10 years, and 1,500 negative controls matched by manufacturer and study year. Performance was measured using patient-level AUROC across four density categories: A (fatty), B (scattered), C (heterogeneous), and D (extremely dense). As a downstream assessment, we also evaluated 10-year risk prediction by incorporating age and AI-derived density into the Tyrer-Cuzick model and comparing performance against a reference model using age and mammography-reported density. All three models performed best in extremely dense breasts (AUROC 0.868-0.899), with strong performance in fatty (0.814-0.838) and scattered density (0.764-0.799), and lower performance in heterogeneously dense breasts (0.699-0.729). DenseNet121 achieved the highest overall performance (micro-averaged AUROC 0.885), and performance across categories was comparable between internal and external testing. For risk modeling, age combined with AI-derived density yielded a lower AUROC than age combined with mammography-reported density (0.541 vs. 0.570; p = 0.23), with no statistically significant difference. These findings indicate that deep learning models generalize well to external data with different racial composition for breast density assessment. While performance is strongest in extremely dense breasts, heterogeneously dense remains more challenging, highlighting the need for targeted optimization.

AI.tldr: Three deep learning models (DenseNet121, ViT-B/32, ResNet50) were externally validated for predicting mammographic breast density from ultrasound exams on 2,000 cases. DenseNet121 achieved the highest overall performance (AUROC 0.885). Models performed best on extremely dense breasts and worst on heterogeneously dense breasts. AI-derived density combined with age showed no significant improvement over mammography-reported density for 10-year risk prediction.
AI.motivation: To externally validate deep learning models for breast density assessment from ultrasound on an independent cohort, and evaluate whether AI-derived density can improve 10-year breast cancer risk prediction when combined with age.
AI.method: Three deep learning models (DenseNet121, ViT-B/32, ResNet50) were validated on 2,000 ultrasound exams (500 cancer cases, 1,500 matched controls). Performance was measured by patient-level AUROC across four density categories (A-D). Risk prediction used age and AI-derived density in the Tyrer-Cuzick model, compared against age with mammography-reported density.
AI.result: Best performance in extremely dense breasts (AUROC 0.868-0.899), lowest in heterogeneously dense (0.699-0.729). DenseNet121 best overall (micro-averaged AUROC 0.885). AI+age risk prediction (0.541) not significantly different from mammography+age (0.570, p=0.23).
AI.conclusion: Deep learning models generalize well across different racial compositions for breast density assessment. Heterogeneously dense breasts remain challenging, requiring targeted optimization. AI-derived density did not significantly outperform mammography-reported density for risk prediction.

title: Conditional Flow-VAE for Safety-Critical Traffic Scenario Generation
summary: Safety-critical scenarios are essential for the development of autonomous vehicles (AVs) but are rare in real-world driving data. While simulation offers a way to generate such scenarios, manually designed test cases lack scalability, and adversarial optimization often produces unrealistic behaviors. In this work, we introduce a conditional latent flow matching approach for scalable and realistic safety-critical scenario generation. Our method uses distribution matching to transform nominal scenes into safety-critical rollouts. Furthermore, we demonstrate that incorporating both simulation and real-world data enables our framework to efficiently generate diverse, data-driven scenarios. Experimental results highlight that our approach is able to more consistently and realistically generate novel safety-critical scenarios, making it a valuable tool for training and benchmarking AV systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a conditional latent flow matching method for generating realistic safety-critical driving scenarios from both simulation and real-world data, outperforming prior approaches in consistency and realism.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical scenarios are essential for AV development but are rare in real-world data. Manual test cases lack scalability, and adversarial optimization often produces unrealistic behaviors.
AI.method: The authors introduce a conditional latent flow matching approach that uses distribution matching to transform nominal scenes into safety-critical rollouts, leveraging both simulation and real-world data.
AI.result: The approach generates diverse, novel safety-critical scenarios more consistently and realistically compared to existing methods, proving useful for AV training and benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is a valuable tool for scalable and realistic safety-critical scenario generation for autonomous vehicles.

title: A Self-Attentive Meta-Optimizer with Group-Adaptive Learning Rates and Weight Decay
summary: Adaptive optimizers like AdamW apply uniform hyperparameters across all parameter groups, ignoring heterogeneous optimization dynamics across layers and modules. We address this limitation by proposing MetaAdamW - a new optimizer that integrates a self-attention mechanism to dynamically modulate per-group learning rates and weight decay. The modulation factors are produced by a lightweight Transformer encoder that operates on statistical features (gradient norms, momentum norms, correlations) extracted from each parameter group. To train the attention module, we introduce a meta-learning objective that combines gradient alignment, loss decrease, and generalization gap. A key novel contribution is the extension of homoscedastic uncertainty weighting (HUW) with task-specific priorities that directly scale the regularization terms - enabling domain knowledge to guide automatic loss balancing. Extensive experiments on five diverse tasks-time series forecasting (ETT), language modeling (WikiText-2), machine translation (Multi30k), image classification (CIFAR-10), and sentiment analysis (IMDB) - demonstrate that MetaAdamW consistently outperforms the standard AdamW baseline in terms of validation loss, accuracy, or perplexity. Depending on the task, MetaAdamW either reduces overall training time (by up to 17.11%) or improves performance (by up to 11.08%) while introducing only moderate overhead; in some cases, it can also mitigate issues of insufficient convergence caused by premature early stopping. Ablation studies validate the effectiveness of each component, including feature versions, grouping strategies, and the proposed priority-injected uncertainty weighting.

AI.tldr: MetaAdamW is a new optimizer that uses a self-attention mechanism and meta-learning to dynamically adjust per-group learning rates and weight decay, outperforming standard AdamW across five diverse tasks with up to 17.11% faster training or 11.08% better performance.
AI.motivation: Standard adaptive optimizers like AdamW apply uniform hyperparameters across all parameter groups, failing to account for heterogeneous optimization dynamics across different layers and modules.
AI.method: Integrates a self-attention mechanism (lightweight Transformer encoder) to dynamically modulate per-group learning rates and weight decay based on statistical features. Introduces a meta-learning objective combining gradient alignment, loss decrease, and generalization gap. Extends homoscedastic uncertainty weighting with task-specific priorities for automatic loss balancing.
AI.result: Outperforms AdamW on five tasks: time series forecasting, language modeling, machine translation, image classification, and sentiment analysis. Reduces training time by up to 17.11% or improves performance by up to 11.08% with moderate overhead. Also mitigates insufficient convergence from early stopping.
AI.conclusion: MetaAdamW effectively addresses heterogeneous optimization dynamics across parameter groups and consistently beats AdamW, with ablation studies confirming the importance of each component.

title: Regularized Centered Emphatic Temporal Difference Learning
summary: Off-policy temporal-difference (TD) learning with function approximation faces a structural tradeoff among stability, projection geometry, and variance control. Emphatic TD (ETD) improves the off-policy projection geometry through follow-on emphasis, but the follow-on trace can have high variance. We revisit this tradeoff through Bellman-error centering. Although centering naturally removes a common drift term from TD errors, we show that a naive centered emphatic extension introduces an auxiliary coupling that can destroy the positive-definiteness of the ETD key matrix. We propose \emph{Regularized Emphatic Temporal-Difference Learning} (RETD), which preserves the follow-on trace and regularizes only the auxiliary centering recursion, corresponding to lifting the lower-right block of the coupled key matrix from \(1\) to \(1+c\). We derive the RETD core matrix, prove convergence under a conservative sufficient regularization condition, and evaluate the method on diagnostic linear off-policy prediction tasks. The experiments show that RETD avoids the instability of naive centered emphatic learning, preserves favorable emphatic geometry, and exhibits a robust intermediate regime for the regularization parameter \(c\) across the diagnostics.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Regularized Emphatic Temporal-Difference Learning (RETD), which improves off-policy TD learning by adding regularization to the centering recursion of emphatic TD, preserving stability and geometric benefits while avoiding variance and instability issues.
AI.motivation: Off-policy TD learning with function approximation suffers from a tradeoff among stability, projection geometry, and variance control. Emphatic TD (ETD) improves geometry via follow-on emphasis but introduces high variance, and naive centering can destroy the positive-definiteness of the ETD key matrix.
AI.method: The authors propose RETD, which preserves the follow-on trace of ETD and regularizes only the auxiliary centering recursion by lifting the lower-right block of the coupled key matrix from 1 to 1+c. They derive the RETD core matrix and prove convergence under a conservative sufficient regularization condition.
AI.result: Experiments on diagnostic linear off-policy prediction tasks show that RETD avoids instability of naive centered emphatic learning, preserves favorable emphatic geometry, and exhibits a robust intermediate regime for the regularization parameter c.
AI.conclusion: RETD effectively resolves the tradeoff between stability, geometry, and variance in off-policy TD learning through targeted regularization of the centering recursion, achieving robust performance across a range of tasks.

title: Densification and forecasting of Sentinel-2 time series from multimodal SAR and Optical satellite data using deep generative models
summary: Optical satellite image time series are extensively used in many Earth observation applications, including agriculture, climate monitoring, and land surface analysis. However, clouds and swath edges result in irregular sampling along the temporal dimension, limiting continuous monitoring. To address this issue, a growing body of work has focused on temporal densification and reconstruction of satellite image time series, with the objective of filling missing or cloud-contaminated observations within the temporal extent of the available data. While these approaches improve temporal continuity, they are inherently restricted to the reconstruction of the gaps within the observed time periods, and do not address the prediction of future observations. This work proposes a probabilistic deep learning framework for the densification and forecasting of Sentinel-2 time series by generating optical images at arbitrary past or future dates. The approach leverages multimodal satellite data by jointly exploiting Sentinel-2 optical and Sentinel-1 SAR observations. Unlike most existing works, we propose to focus on the uncertainty of the generated images. Experimental results demonstrate effective densification and forecasting, on sparse and temporally misaligned time series.

AI.tldr: A probabilistic deep learning framework that uses Sentinel-1 SAR data to densify and forecast Sentinel-2 optical time series at arbitrary dates, with a focus on quantifying prediction uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Irregular temporal sampling due to clouds and swath edges limits continuous monitoring from optical satellite time series. Existing methods only fill gaps within observed periods but cannot predict future observations.
AI.method: A probabilistic deep learning framework that leverages multimodal data (Sentinel-2 optical + Sentinel-1 SAR) to generate optical images at arbitrary past or future dates, explicitly modeling uncertainty of generated images.
AI.result: Effective densification and forecasting demonstrated on sparse and temporally misaligned time series.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework goes beyond gap-filling by enabling both temporal densification (past) and forecasting (future) with uncertainty quantification, offering more complete temporal monitoring.

title: CTseg: A Tool for Brain CT Segmentation, Spatial Normalisation, and Volumetrics
summary: This paper presents and validates CTseg, a freely available software for brain CT segmentation, spatial normalisation, and volumetrics. CTseg builds on the Multi-Brain generative modelling framework, providing a CT-specific pipeline that produces tissue maps, deformation fields, and brain volume estimates in the same format as SPM's unified segmentation, thereby extending SPM's established analysis chain from MRI to CT. CTseg is designed for routine hospital CT scans without requiring preprocessing or resampling in deployment. Although CTseg has been adopted in clinical research spanning, among other things, stroke, dementia, and brain morphometry, a systematic validation against an independent reference standard has been lacking. Using paired MR/CT head scans, we evaluate CTseg across four dimensions: segmentation accuracy against an MRI-derived silver standard; spatial normalisation consistency through group-average sharpness and voxelwise coefficient of variation; brain volume agreement via intraclass correlation and Bland-Altman analysis; and downstream sex classification performance from normalised tissue maps. As a baseline, we apply SPM's MRI-based unified segmentation directly to the CT images. CTseg significantly outperformed this baseline for segmentation and normalisation, showed stronger TBV agreement, and achieved comparable TIV agreement. CTseg is freely available at https://github.com/WCHN/CTseg, and all experiment code is included in the repository for full reproducibility.

AI.tldr: CTseg is a free software for brain CT segmentation, spatial normalisation, and volumetrics that extends SPM's MRI analysis chain to CT. Validated against paired MR/CT scans, it significantly outperforms directly applying MRI-based SPM segmentation to CT images across segmentation accuracy, normalisation consistency, and brain volume agreement.
AI.motivation: To systematically validate CTseg against an independent reference standard, which has been lacking despite its adoption in clinical research for stroke, dementia, and brain morphometry.
AI.method: Using paired MR/CT head scans, CTseg was evaluated across four dimensions: segmentation accuracy vs. MRI-derived silver standard, spatial normalisation consistency (group-average sharpness, voxelwise coefficient of variation), brain volume agreement (ICC, Bland-Altman), and downstream sex classification. Baseline comparison was SPM's MRI-based unified segmentation applied directly to CT images.
AI.result: CTseg significantly outperformed the MRI-based baseline for segmentation and normalisation, showed stronger total brain volume (TBV) agreement, and achieved comparable total intracranial volume (TIV) agreement.
AI.conclusion: CTseg is a validated, freely available tool that reliably extends SPM's analysis pipeline from MRI to routine hospital CT scans without preprocessing, with full reproducibility support.

title: Autonomous Laparoscope Control through Unified Mechanics-Based Representation of Multimodal Intraoperative Information
summary: Laparoscope-holding robots can provide surgeons with a stable laparoscopic field of view (FOV) and reduce the burden on human assistants. To maintain an ideal intraoperative FOV, the robot must continuously adjust the laparoscope pose according to intraoperative information. However, intraoperative multimodal signals, such as position, force/torque, and images, differ markedly in physical meaning and units, making it difficult to build a unified representation and to generate control commands that can be used directly for laparoscope control. To address this issue, we propose a laparoscope-holding robot control method based on unified mechanics modeling of multimodal information. First, we design mapping strategies for multiple intraoperative sources, including position, force/torque, and images, and unify them into an equivalent-wrench representation in the operational space. Then, using a task-priority scheme, we inject the wrenches into the task space and the null space, respectively, and synthesize laparoscope control commands via task-priority projection, thereby achieving consistent representation and coordinated fusion of multimodal information within a single framework. Finally, taking the intraoperative remote center of motion (RCM) position, force/torque sensor readings, and laparoscopic images as examples, we construct an RCM-constraint wrench to enforce the RCM geometric constraint and reduce the contact force at the trocar site, a laparoscope-manipulation wrench to enable compliant dragging, and an instrument-tracking wrench to achieve autonomous visual tracking of the instruments. Experiments on a surgical phantom and in vivo porcine trials demonstrate that the proposed method supports multi-task operation, including compliant laparoscope manipulation and autonomous instrument tracking, while maintaining the RCM constraint and reducing sustained trocar-site loading.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified mechanics-based control framework for laparoscope-holding robots that converts multimodal intraoperative signals (position, force/torque, images) into equivalent-wrench representations, enabling coordinated multi-task operation including RCM constraint maintenance, compliant dragging, and autonomous instrument tracking, validated on surgical phantoms and in vivo porcine trials.
AI.motivation: Intraoperative multimodal signals (position, force/torque, images) differ markedly in physical meaning and units, making it difficult to build a unified representation and generate direct control commands for laparoscope-holding robots.
AI.method: A unified mechanics modeling approach that maps multimodal signals into equivalent-wrench representations in operational space, then uses task-priority projection to inject wrenches into task and null spaces for coordinated control.
AI.result: Experiments on surgical phantom and in vivo porcine trials demonstrate multi-task operation including compliant laparoscope manipulation and autonomous instrument tracking while maintaining RCM constraint and reducing trocar-site loading.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unified wrench-based framework effectively addresses multimodal signal fusion for laparoscope-holding robots, enabling safe and coordinated multi-task performance in surgical settings.

title: Transformation Categorization Based on Group Decomposition Theory Using Parameter Division
summary: Representation learning seeks meaningful sensory representations without supervision and can model aspects of human development. Although many neural networks empirically learn useful features, a principled account of what makes a representation "good" remains elusive. We study unsupervised categorization of transformations between pairs of inputs under algebraic constraints. Classical disentanglement favors mutually independent factors and fails when factors are coupled. Our prior Galois-theoretic approach decomposes a group via normal subgroups by learning a product of two transformations with one factor constrained to a normal subgroup, covering both commutative and non-commutative cases. That method, however, relied on auxiliary assumptions (e.g., motion and isometry restrictions) not required by decomposition theory, and ablations did not separate theory-based from auxiliary effects. We propose parameter division for a single transformation: we split its parameter into components, impose homomorphism constraints mapping the full transformation to one component, and identify the normal subgroup as the set of transformations when that component is fixed to the identity. This formulation drops the previous auxiliary assumptions and applies more broadly. We evaluate on image pairs involving rotation, translation, and scale; ablations show that group-decomposition constraints drive appropriate categorization.

AI.tldr: A new parameter-division method for unsupervised categorization of transformations under algebraic constraints, dropping prior auxiliary assumptions like motion and isometry restrictions, and demonstrating that group-decomposition constraints drive appropriate categorization on rotation, translation, and scale tasks.
AI.motivation: Prior Galois-theoretic representation learning relied on auxiliary assumptions (motion/isometry restrictions) that decomposition theory does not require; ablations could not separate theory-based effects from auxiliary ones. The authors aim to drop these assumptions and generalize the approach.
AI.method: Parameter division for a single transformation: split the parameter into components, enforce homomorphism constraints mapping the full transformation to one component, and identify the normal subgroup as transformations where that component is identity.
AI.result: Evaluated on image pairs with rotation, translation, and scale transformations. Ablations confirm that group-decomposition constraints (not auxiliary assumptions) drive appropriate categorization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed parameter-division method extends the Galois-theoretic approach to unsupervised categorization without restrictive auxiliary assumptions, offering a more principled and general framework for representation learning under algebraic constraints.

title: Actionable Real-Time Modeling of Surgical Team Dynamics via Time-Expanded Interaction Graphs
comment: Accepted at Hybrid Human Artificial Intelligence (HHAI) 2026
summary: Surgical team performance arises from complex interactions between technical execution and non-technical skills, including communication and coordination dynamics. However, current surgical AI systems predominantly model visual workflow signals, lacking structured representations of intraoperative team interactions over time. We propose a real-time actionable approach for modeling surgical team dynamics using time-expanded interaction graphs, where team members are modeled as time-indexed nodes and communication exchanges define directed edges. This spatio-temporal expansion enables dynamic interaction modeling, while allowing efficient inference with a static graph neural network. The model predicts procedural efficiency as the deviation from the expected duration and supports real-time deployment. Beyond prediction, we perform a counterfactual analysis to identify minimal changes in communication structure and interpretable behavioral variables associated with improved predicted outcomes. Experiments on recorded surgical procedures show that structured modeling of team interactions improves early identification of prolonged interventions and provides coherent, actionable explanations. This work advances surgical AI toward real-time, team-aware, and actionable decision support in the operating room.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a real-time approach using time-expanded interaction graphs to model surgical team dynamics, predicting procedural efficiency and providing counterfactual explanations for improved team communication.
AI.motivation: Current surgical AI systems focus on visual workflow signals and lack structured representations of intraoperative team interactions (communication and coordination dynamics) over time.
AI.method: Time-expanded interaction graphs where team members are time-indexed nodes and communication exchanges are directed edges, enabling spatio-temporal dynamic modeling with a static graph neural network for efficient inference.
AI.result: Structured modeling of team interactions improves early identification of prolonged interventions and provides coherent, actionable explanations via counterfactual analysis.
AI.conclusion: This work advances surgical AI toward real-time, team-aware, and actionable decision support in the operating room.

title: Contextual Memory-Enhanced Source Coding for Low-SNR Communications
summary: While Separate Source-Channel Coding (SSCC) retains the practical benefits of modular system design, its effectiveness in noisy text transmission is fundamentally constrained by the fragility of autoregressive source decoding. In low-SNR regimes, even a small number of residual bit errors after channel decoding may derail the subsequent lossless reconstruction process, especially when Arithmetic Coding (AC) relies on Large Language Model (LLM)-based probability estimation. Existing remedies either strengthen channel decoding based solely on channel observations or introduce contextual information only at the receiver for post-hoc correction, yet neither fully addresses the fragility of source probability modeling under residual channel errors. To this end, this paper proposes a Memory-Augmented Source Coding (MASC) scheme for robust SSCC-based transmission. Rather than treating context as external side information, MASC internalizes contextual patterns into a source model shared by both the transmitter-side source encoder and the receiver-side source decoder. Specifically, MASC employs a shared Parameterized Contextual Memory (PCM) to encode multi-order $n$-gram patterns, and further introduces a Mixture-of-Memory-Experts Router (MMER) to perform sparse, hidden-state-dependent routing over memory experts during autoregressive source modeling. By adaptively activating only the most relevant memories at each coding step, MASC refines source probability estimation, shortens average codelength, and mitigates the sensitivity of source decoding to residual channel errors. Extensive experiments over Rayleigh fading and AWGN channels demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed scheme compared with state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Memory-Augmented Source Coding (MASC), a scheme that internalizes contextual patterns into a shared source model via Parameterized Contextual Memory (PCM) and Mixture-of-Memory-Experts Router (MMER) to improve robustness of Separate Source-Channel Coding (SSCC) in noisy text transmission, outperforming state-of-the-art methods over Rayleigh fading and AWGN channels.
AI.motivation: SSCC-based noisy text transmission suffers from fragile autoregressive source decoding in low-SNR regimes, where residual bit errors after channel decoding can derail lossless reconstruction, especially when Arithmetic Coding relies on LLM-based probability estimation. Existing solutions (strengthening channel decoding or post-hoc correction) fail to fully address the fragility of source probability modeling under residual errors.
AI.method: MASC internalizes contextual patterns into a source model shared by both encoder and decoder, using: (1) a shared Parameterized Contextual Memory (PCM) to encode multi-order n-gram patterns, and (2) a Mixture-of-Memory-Experts Router (MMER) for sparse, hidden-state-dependent routing over memory experts during autoregressive source modeling, adaptively activating only the most relevant memories at each coding step.
AI.result: Extensive experiments over Rayleigh fading and AWGN channels demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed MASC scheme compared with state-of-the-art methods, showing refined source probability estimation, shortened average codelength, and mitigated sensitivity of source decoding to residual channel errors.
AI.conclusion: By internalizing context into a shared source model rather than treating it as external side information, MASC significantly improves SSCC robustness against residual channel errors, offering a promising direction for reliable text transmission in noisy environments.

title: Near-field channel estimation via wavefront parameterization
summary: This paper deals with the estimation of multiantenna channels in the line-of-sight conditions that are prevalent in the near field. By expressing the curved wavefront as a polynomial via a power series expansion of a sphere, the estimation of the channel over the array can be formulated as a multidimensional polynomial phase estimation problem. The application of a newly developed polynomial phase estimator, able of handling arbitrary dimensions and polynomial degrees, yields a superior tradeoff between channel estimation accuracy and complexity.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method for estimating multiantenna channels in near-field line-of-sight conditions by modeling curved wavefronts as a polynomial phase and applying a multidimensional polynomial phase estimator, achieving better accuracy-complexity tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: To improve channel estimation in near-field line-of-sight multiantenna systems, where traditional far-field assumptions (planar wavefronts) are inaccurate due to curved wavefronts.
AI.method: The curved wavefront is expressed as a polynomial via power series expansion of a sphere, formulating channel estimation as a multidimensional polynomial phase estimation problem, then solved using a new polynomial phase estimator that handles arbitrary dimensions and polynomial degrees.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves a superior tradeoff between channel estimation accuracy and complexity compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The newly developed polynomial phase estimator is effective for near-field channel estimation, offering better performance tradeoffs.

title: Physics-Guided Regime Unmixing
summary: The Linear Mixing Model (LMM) dominates spectral unmixing for its simplicity, but fails under multiple scattering; existing nonlinear models compensate by applying a fixed regime uniformly across entire scenes. We propose Physics-Guided Regime Unmixing (PGRU), which estimates a pixel-wise scalar $ξ_i \in [0,1]$ from observable physical features to activate nonlinear mixing only where justified. Residuals from the Generalized Bilinear Model (GBM), the Post-Nonlinear Mixing Model (PPNM), and Hapke are combined via learned attention, yielding interpretable regime maps. Experiments on Samson, Jasper Ridge, and Urban show consistent improvements over baselines, with physical coherence $ρ> 0.90$.

AI.tldr: PGRU introduces pixel-wise nonlinear mixing regime selection via physical features, outperforming fixed-regime models on three benchmark datasets with high physical coherence.
AI.motivation: Linear Mixing Model fails under multiple scattering; existing nonlinear models apply a fixed regime uniformly across entire scenes, lacking flexibility.
AI.method: Estimates pixel-wise scalar ξ_i ∈ [0,1] from observable physical features to activate nonlinear mixing only where justified; combines residuals from GBM, PPNM, and Hapke via learned attention.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over baselines on Samson, Jasper Ridge, and Urban datasets; physical coherence ρ > 0.90.
AI.conclusion: Physics-guided adaptive regime selection improves spectral unmixing accuracy and interpretability compared to fixed-regime nonlinear models.

title: MRI-Eval: A Tiered Benchmark for Evaluating LLM Performance on MRI Physics and GE Scanner Operations Knowledge
summary: Background: Existing MRI LLM benchmarks rely mainly on review-book multiple-choice questions, where top proprietary models already score highly, limiting discrimination. No systematic benchmark has evaluated vendor-specific scanner operational knowledge central to research MRI practice. Purpose: We developed MRI-Eval, a tiered benchmark for relative model comparison on MRI physics and GE scanner operations knowledge using primary multiple-choice questions (MCQ), with stem-only and primed diagnostic conditions as complementary analyses. Methods: MRI-Eval includes 1365 scored items across nine categories and three difficulty tiers from textbooks, GE scanner manuals, programming course materials, and expert-generated questions. Five model families were evaluated (GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Llama 3.3 70B). MCQ was primary; stem-only removed options and used an independent LLM judge; primed stem-only tested responses to incorrect user claims. Results: Overall MCQ accuracy was 93.2% to 97.1%. GE scanner operations was the lowest category for every model (88.2% to 94.6%). In stem-only, frontier-model accuracy fell to 58.4% to 61.1%, and Llama 3.3 70B fell to 37.1%; GE scanner operations stem-only accuracy was 13.8% to 29.8%. Conclusion: High MCQ performance can mask weak free-text recall, especially for vendor-specific operational knowledge. MRI-Eval is most informative as a relative comparison benchmark rather than an absolute competency measure and supports caution in using raw LLM outputs for GE-specific protocol guidance.

AI.tldr: MRI-Eval is a new benchmark evaluating LLMs on MRI physics and GE scanner operations knowledge. While all models scored 93-97% on multiple-choice questions, performance dropped dramatically in open-ended recall (as low as 13.8% for GE-specific questions), revealing that MCQ accuracy masks weak free-text knowledge, especially for vendor-specific operational expertise.
AI.motivation: Existing MRI LLM benchmarks rely on review-book multiple-choice questions where top models already score highly, limiting discrimination. No systematic benchmark has evaluated vendor-specific scanner operational knowledge that is central to research MRI practice.
AI.method: Developed MRI-Eval with 1365 scored items across nine categories and three difficulty tiers from textbooks, GE scanner manuals, programming course materials, and expert-generated questions. Evaluated five model families (GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Llama 3.3 70B) using three conditions: standard MCQ, stem-only (no options, LLM judge), and primed stem-only (incorrect user claims).
AI.result: Overall MCQ accuracy was 93.2%-97.1%. GE scanner operations was the lowest category for every model (88.2%-94.6%). In stem-only, frontier-model accuracy fell to 58.4%-61.1%, Llama 3.3 70B fell to 37.1%, and GE scanner operations stem-only accuracy dropped to 13.8%-29.8%.
AI.conclusion: High MCQ performance can mask weak free-text recall, especially for vendor-specific operational knowledge. MRI-Eval is best used as a relative comparison benchmark rather than absolute competency measure. Results support caution in using raw LLM outputs for GE-specific protocol guidance.

title: Tightly-Coupled Estimation and Guidance for Robust Low-Thrust Rendezvous via Adaptive Homotopy
summary: Minimum-fuel low-thrust rendezvous guidance yields bang-bang control structures highly sensitive to estimation errors, sensor anomalies, and solver regularization, making aggressive closed-loop execution brittle for uncooperative proximity operations. This paper proposes a tightly-coupled estimation and guidance architecture where navigation confidence directly modulates the homotopy parameter of a receding-horizon indirect optimal control solver. Relative motion is modeled in the Clohessy-Wiltshire frame. The translational state is estimated via a linear Kalman filter augmented by a Multiple Tuning Factors (MTF) covariance inflation mechanism that suppresses suspicious innovation directions. A composite score from the normalized innovation and MTF activity is mapped online to the homotopy parameter, allowing the controller to relax toward a smoother, conservative regime when confidence degrades, and recover fuel-efficient bang-bang control as sensing improves. Numerical results under severe measurement degradation show fixed bang-bang guidance remains brittle; both plain-KF and MTF-KF fixed-epsilon controllers yield large terminal miss distances. Conversely, the proposed MTF-adaptive homotopy controller reduces terminal miss by roughly two orders of magnitude, from hundreds of meters to sub-meter levels, requiring only a moderate increase in control effort versus the open-loop fuel-optimal benchmark. A comparison indicates adaptive homotopy is the dominant robustness mechanism, while MTF provides additional accuracy and efficiency improvements. The receding-horizon implementation exhibits consistently fast and reliable solution times, supporting the practical online viability of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an adaptive homotopy control method that uses navigation confidence (via innovation and MTF covariance inflation) to modulate the homotopy parameter of a receding-horizon optimal controller for minimum-fuel low-thrust rendezvous, reducing terminal miss distance from hundreds of meters to sub-meter levels under severe measurement degradation.
AI.motivation: Minimum-fuel low-thrust rendezvous guidance using bang-bang control is highly sensitive to estimation errors, sensor anomalies, and solver regularization, making closed-loop execution brittle for uncooperative proximity operations.
AI.method: Tightly-coupled estimation and guidance: linear Kalman filter with MTF covariance inflation; a composite score from normalized innovation and MTF activity is mapped online to the homotopy parameter of a receding-horizon indirect optimal control solver, enabling smooth transition between conservative and aggressive control regimes.
AI.result: Under severe measurement degradation, adaptive homotopy reduces terminal miss from hundreds of meters to sub-meter levels with only moderate increase in control effort; MTF provides additional accuracy/efficiency improvements. Solution times are fast and reliable.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive homotopy is the dominant robustness mechanism; the proposed MTF-adaptive homotopy controller is practically viable for online closed-loop low-thrust rendezvous guidance.

title: Structured Progressive Knowledge Activation for LLM-Driven Neural Architecture Search
summary: This paper focuses on a key challenge in Neural Architecture Search (NAS): integrating established architectural knowledge while exploring new designs under expensive evaluations. Large language models (LLMs) are a promising assistant for NAS because they can translate rich architectural and coding priors into executable code edits. However, in practice, seemingly local revisions often propagate into non-local behavioral and performance shifts because a single edit can inadvertently couple multiple interacting functional factors, a phenomenon we refer to as functional entanglement. To make LLM knowledge usable under such entanglement, we propose Structured Progressive Knowledge Activation (SPARK), which activates relevant priors by explicitly selecting the functional factor to modify and conditioning the edit on that factor. This factor-conditioned editing reduces entangled side effects and yields more targeted, reliable architecture modifications. On CLRS-DFS, SPARK achieves a 28.1x sample-efficient architecture evolution speedup and yields a 22.9 percent relative improvement in OOD accuracy.

AI.tldr: SPARK introduces factor-conditioned editing to mitigate functional entanglement in LLM-assisted NAS, achieving 28.1x sample efficiency gains and 22.9% OOD accuracy improvement.
AI.motivation: LLMs can assist NAS by leveraging architectural knowledge, but local edits often cause unintended non-local performance shifts due to functional entanglement, making reliable architecture search difficult.
AI.method: SPARK (Structured Progressive Knowledge Activation) explicitly selects a functional factor to modify and conditions the LLM edit on that specific factor, reducing entangled side effects from coupled architectural changes.
AI.result: 28.1x sample-efficient architecture evolution speedup and 22.9% relative improvement in Out-of-Distribution accuracy on CLRS-DFS benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Factor-conditioned editing is an effective strategy to unlock LLM knowledge for NAS by mitigating functional entanglement, leading to more targeted and reliable architecture modifications.

title: ANDRE: An Attention-based Neuro-symbolic Differentiable Rule Extractor
summary: Inductive Logic Programming (ILP) aims to learn interpretable first-order rules from data, but existing symbolic and neuro-symbolic approaches struggle to scale to noisy and probabilistic settings. Classical ILP relies on discrete combinatorial rule search and is brittle under uncertainty, while differentiable ILP methods typically depend on predefined rule templates or inaccurate fuzzy operators that suffer from vanishing gradients or poor approximation of logical structure when reasoning over probabilistic predicate valuations. This paper proposes an Attention-based Neuro-symbolic Differentiable Rule Extractor (ANDRE), a novel ILP framework that learns first-order logic programs by optimizing over a continuous rule space with attention-based logical operators. ANDRE replaces both rule templates and logical operators with fully differentiable, attention-driven conjunction and disjunction operators that approximate logical min-max semantics, enabling accurate, stable, and interpretable reasoning over probabilistic data. By softly selecting, negating, or excluding predicates within each rule, ANDRE supports flexible rule induction while preserving symbolic structure. Extensive experiments on classical ILP benchmarks, large-scale knowledge bases, and synthetic datasets with probabilistic predicates and noisy supervision demonstrate that ANDRE achieves competitive or superior predictive performance while reliably recovering correct symbolic rules under uncertainty. In particular, ANDRE remains robust to moderate label noise, substantially outperforming existing differentiable ILP methods in both rule extraction quality and stability.

AI.tldr: ANDRE is a differentiable ILP framework using attention-based logical operators to learn interpretable first-order rules from noisy, probabilistic data, outperforming existing methods in robustness and rule extraction quality.
AI.motivation: Existing symbolic and neuro-symbolic ILP methods struggle with noisy, probabilistic settings due to brittle discrete search, reliance on predefined rule templates, and inaccurate fuzzy operators with vanishing gradients or poor logical approximation.
AI.method: ANDRE replaces rule templates and logical operators with fully differentiable, attention-driven conjunction and disjunction operators that approximate logical min-max semantics, enabling soft selection, negation, or exclusion of predicates within continuous rule space.
AI.result: Competitive/superior predictive performance on classical ILP benchmarks, large-scale knowledge bases, and synthetic probabilistic datasets; robust to moderate label noise; outperforms existing differentiable ILP methods in rule extraction quality and stability.
AI.conclusion: Attention-based differentiable logical operators offer a scalable, robust, and interpretable solution for ILP under uncertainty, bridging symbolic structure with continuous optimization.

title: Z-Opt: A Near-Optimal Reduced-Complexity Two-Dimensional Grassmannian Constellation
summary: Grassmannian constellations are known to achieve the capacity of noncoherent communications over Rayleigh fading channels in the high-SNR regime, yet their efficient construction remains challenging. In this paper, we propose two construction methods for Grassmannian constellations of one-dimensional subspaces in a two-dimensional space, termed S-Opt and Z-Opt, along with two low-complexity detectors. Both the construction and detection procedures are performed on the unit sphere, known as the Bloch sphere in quantum computing. We show that the chordal distance on the Grassmann manifold is proportional to the Euclidean distance on the Bloch sphere and derive a corresponding theoretical upper bound based on the Fejes--Tóth bound on the minimum chordal distance. The S-Opt constellation is constructed from sphere-packing solutions and attains the derived upper bound for the optimal Bloch-sphere packings considered. The S-Opt detector can be applied to arbitrary Grassmannian constellations on $\mathcal{G}(2,1)$, and its time complexity scales linearly with the number of receive antennas and logarithmically with the constellation size, while yielding the same detection performance as the GLRT detector. Furthermore, based on the insight obtained through the S-Opt construction, the Z-Opt constellation is constructed by stacking regular polygons on the Bloch sphere, and its minimum chordal distance approaches the derived upper bound over the evaluated constellation sizes. The Z-Opt detector's time complexity scales linearly with the number of receive antennas, while yielding the same detection performance as the GLRT detector for Z-Opt.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two efficient construction methods (S-Opt and Z-Opt) for Grassmannian constellations on the Bloch sphere for noncoherent communications, along with two low-complexity detectors that match GLRT performance while achieving linear/logarithmic complexity scaling.
AI.motivation: Grassmannian constellations achieve capacity for noncoherent Rayleigh fading channels at high SNR, but their efficient construction remains a challenging problem.
AI.method: The authors map the problem onto the Bloch sphere (unit sphere), show chordal distance is proportional to Euclidean distance on this sphere, and derive an upper bound from the Fejes–Tóth bound. S-Opt uses sphere-packing solutions; Z-Opt stacks regular polygons. Two detectors are designed with low complexity.
AI.result: S-Opt attains the optimal bound for Bloch-sphere packings. Z-Opt approaches the bound as constellation size grows. Both detectors match GLRT performance: S-Opt scales linearly with receive antennas and logarithmically with constellation size; Z-Opt scales linearly with receive antennas.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods and detectors offer efficient, practical solutions for constructing and detecting Grassmannian constellations on $\mathcal{G}(2,1)$ with provable optimality and low complexity.

title: Statistical Model of Time-varying Backscatter Power of Monostatic RF Sensing Channels in Urban Canyons
comment: 8 pages, 12 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
summary: We present a measurement-based statistical model for the backscatter power ratio of monostatic RF sensing in urban canyons with moving clutter, suitable for large-scale system level performance evaluation of RF sensing in 6G networks. A narrowband (CW) 140 GHz sounder used a monostatic radar arrangement with an omnidirectional transmit antenna illuminating streets and a spinning horn 2o receive antenna offset vertically (less than 1 m away) collecting backscattered power as a function of azimuth and time below building height in Manhattan and Valparaiso, Chile. A concise outdoor deterministic model of average backscattered power dependent on distance to nearest building-wall reproduces observations with 3.3 dB RMS error or better. Distribution of power variation in azimuth around this average is reproduced within 0.5 dB by a random azimuth spectrum with a lognormal distribution. Temporal fluctuations for various antenna aims and locations were found to be well modeled by a Rician distribution, with lognormally distributed K-factor, with 0.47-0.73 correlation coefficient to backscatter power deviation from mean. The statistical model does not require a detailed environmental description, aiming to reproduce backscatter clutter statistics (as opposed to a deterministic response) faithfully and efficiently, essential for large-scale system-level performance evaluation.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a statistical model for backscatter power ratio in urban canyons at 140 GHz, based on measurements in Manhattan and Valparaiso. The model captures average backscatter as a function of wall distance (3.3 dB RMS error), azimuth variation (lognormal, <0.5 dB), and temporal fluctuations (Rician with lognormal K-factor, correlation 0.47-0.73). Designed for large-scale 6G RF sensing system evaluation without requiring detailed environmental data.
AI.motivation: To enable large-scale system-level performance evaluation of monostatic RF sensing in 6G networks within urban canyons, by providing a simple statistical model that avoids detailed environmental descriptions while faithfully reproducing backscatter clutter statistics.
AI.method: Measurements were conducted using a narrowband 140 GHz CW sounder in monostatic radar configuration with an omnidirectional transmit antenna and a spinning 2° horn receive antenna in Manhattan and Valparaiso, Chile. Statistical modeling of backscatter power includes: (1) average power vs. distance to nearest building wall, (2) azimuth variation via lognormal spectrum, (3) temporal fluctuations via Rician distribution with lognormal K-factor.
AI.result: Average backscattered power model reproduces observations with ≤3.3 dB RMS error. Azimuth power variation is modeled within 0.5 dB. Temporal fluctuations show Rician distribution with lognormal K-factor, achieving 0.47-0.73 correlation to backscatter power deviation from mean.
AI.conclusion: The proposed statistical model efficiently and faithfully reproduces backscatter clutter statistics for urban canyon environments at 140 GHz, making it suitable for large-scale system-level performance evaluation of RF sensing in future 6G networks without requiring detailed environmental descriptions.

title: Imagery Dataset for Remaining Useful Life Estimation of Synthetic Fibre Ropes
summary: Remaining useful life (RUL) estimation of synthetic fibre ropes (SFRs) is critical for safe operation in offshore-crane, wind turbine installation, and heavy-load handling applications, where rope failure can result in catastrophic safety incidents and costly downtime. Despite growing research interest in data-driven condition monitoring, there is no publicly available image dataset that captures the complete degradation lifecycle of SFRs under controlled cyclic fatigue loading. To address this gap, we present a novel image dataset comprising approximately 34,700 high-resolution images of eleven Dyneema SK75/78 high-modulus polyethylene (HMPE) rope samples subjected to cyclic fatigue on a sheave-bend test stand at seven distinct axial load levels ranging from 60 kN to 280 kN. Ropes were loaded until mechanical failure, with fatigue lifetimes ranging from 695 cycles to 8,340 cycles. After every fixed number of sheave cycles (an inspection burst), ten images were captured at different cross-sectional positions along the rope, providing spatially representative sampling of surface degradation throughout the rope's entire service life. The images obtained from each load are annotated with the corresponding elapsed cycle count, enabling a direct computation of RUL for any rope in the sequence. This dataset aims to support a broad range of machine learning (ML) tasks including RUL regression, damage progression modelling, anomaly detection, and load-conditioned prognostics. The dataset is intended to serve as a benchmark resource for the development and comparison of vision-based condition monitoring (CM) and prognostics algorithms for SFRs.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a new public image dataset of ~34,700 high-resolution images capturing the full degradation lifecycle of Dyneema synthetic fibre ropes under cyclic fatigue at seven load levels (60-280 kN), with cycle-annotated labels for RUL estimation and various ML-based condition monitoring tasks.
AI.motivation: RUL estimation of SFRs is critical for safety in offshore and heavy-lift applications, yet no publicly available image dataset exists that captures the complete degradation lifecycle of these ropes under controlled cyclic fatigue loading, hindering development of vision-based condition monitoring algorithms.
AI.method: Eleven Dyneema HMPE rope samples were subjected to cyclic fatigue on a sheave-bend test stand at seven axial load levels (60-280 kN) until mechanical failure. After fixed cycle intervals (inspection bursts), ten images were captured at different cross-sectional positions along each rope, yielding ~34,700 high-resolution images annotated with elapsed cycle count for direct RUL computation.
AI.result: Fatigue lifetimes ranged from 695 to 8,340 cycles across load levels. The dataset provides spatially representative sampling of surface degradation throughout the entire service life, with cycle-annotated labels enabling direct RUL computation for any rope in the sequence.
