A leasing-aware architecture is presented, and a Lyapunov-based Decision Support Framework is implemented as an illustrative mechanism for intelligent resource acquisition under timevarying prices and availability. Simulation results validate that the DSF achieves cost-efficient, delay-aware orchestration while exhibiting the expected Lyapunov stability properties. These findings highlight the feasibility of combining IDC with intelligent resource leasing in future 6G systems.

AI.tldr: This paper extends Intent-Driven Communication (IDC) for 6G networks by integrating Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS) and dynamic spectrum leasing, proposing a Lyapunov-based Decision Support Framework (DSF) for cost-efficient, delay-aware resource orchestration under time-varying conditions.
AI.motivation: To extend the IDC paradigm by incorporating RIS and dynamic spectrum leasing, enabling autonomous 6G networks to handle resource-constrained environments with intelligent, cost-efficient resource acquisition.
AI.method: Proposes a leasing-aware architecture and implements a Lyapunov-based Decision Support Framework for intelligent resource acquisition under time-varying prices and availability.
AI.result: The DSF achieves cost-efficient, delay-aware orchestration and exhibits expected Lyapunov stability properties.
AI.conclusion: Combining IDC with intelligent resource leasing is feasible and promising for future 6G systems.

title: Optimize-at-Capture: Highly-adaptive Exposure Controlling for In-Vehicle Non-contact Heart-rate Monitoring
summary: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) holds great promise for continuous heart-rate monitoring of drivers in intelligent vehicles. However, its performance is severely degraded by the highly dynamic illumination changes. A critical yet overlooked factor is the lack of exposure controlling during video acquisition -- most existing systems rely on either fixed exposure settings or camera build-in auto-exposure, both of which fail to maintain stable facial brightness under rapidly changing lighting conditions during driving. To address this gap, we propose a highly-adaptive exposure controlling framework that proactively adjusts exposure parameters based on predictive modeling of historical skin reflections. Unlike standard auto-exposure, our method is specifically optimized for rPPG measurement, ensuring the skin region of interest (ROI) remains within the optimal dynamic range for rPPG signal extraction. As an important contribution of this study, we introduce ExpDrive, a public in-vehicle physiological monitoring dataset comprising synchronized facial video and reference ECG from 48 subjects captured under real driving conditions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms fixed exposure and standard auto-exposure strategies. Specifically, it reduces the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) by 6.31 bpm (from 14.1 to 7.79 bpm) and significantly increases the success rate by 32.3 percentage points (p < 0.001) (from 24.9% to 57.2%) across challenging driving scenarios. Notably, it clearly improved the performance of non-contact heart-rate monitoring in both low-light (rainy) and high-glare (sunny) conditions, validating the efficacy of exposure-aware acquisition design.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a highly-adaptive exposure controlling framework for rPPG-based heart-rate monitoring in driving scenarios, and introduces ExpDrive, a public in-vehicle dataset. The method proactively adjusts exposure parameters based on historical skin reflections, reducing MAE from 14.1 to 7.79 bpm and increasing success rate from 24.9% to 57.2%.
AI.motivation: Existing rPPG systems suffer from performance degradation under dynamic illumination changes in driving environments because they rely on fixed exposure or standard auto-exposure, which cannot maintain stable facial brightness under rapidly changing lighting conditions.
AI.method: A highly-adaptive exposure controlling framework that proactively adjusts exposure parameters based on predictive modeling of historical skin reflections, optimized specifically for rPPG measurement to keep the skin ROI within optimal dynamic range.
AI.result: Outperforms fixed exposure and standard auto-exposure: reduces MAE by 6.31 bpm (14.1 → 7.79 bpm), increases success rate by 32.3 percentage points (24.9% → 57.2%), and improves performance in both low-light (rainy) and high-glare (sunny) conditions.
AI.conclusion: Exposure-aware acquisition design is crucial for non-contact heart-rate monitoring in driving scenarios, and the proposed adaptive exposure framework significantly improves rPPG robustness under real-world challenging lighting conditions.

title: From Reach to Insert: Tactile-Augmented Precision Assembly under Sub-Millimeter Tolerances
summary: High-precision assembly frequently involves tight-tolerance insertions, where even slight pose errors can cause jamming or excessive interaction forces, making robust and safe insertion policies difficult to obtain. This paper proposes a tactile-augmented two-stage method that combines Imitation Learning (IL) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) for precision insertion tasks. In the first stage, IL learns a reaching policy with position generalization that grasps the peg and brings it to the vicinity of the target region. In the second stage, RL executes the insertion and enables recovery from failures during contact-rich interactions. To better exploit tactile feedback, we introduce tactile group sampling to increase coverage of critical contact segments during training, and design a tactile critic to more accurately evaluate policy values, improving insertion performance while maintaining low contact forces. We conduct systematic experiments across five hole geometries and three clearance settings. Results show that our method substantially improves insertion performance across all settings; under the most challenging 0.05\,mm clearance, it achieves a 67\% success rate while keeping contact forces low, reducing the maximum interaction force by 60\% and torque by 44\%, thereby validating both effectiveness and safety for precision assembly.

AI.tldr: A tactile-augmented IL+RL method for precision insertion tasks, achieving 67% success at 0.05mm clearance while reducing contact forces by 60% and torque by 44%.
AI.motivation: High-precision assembly tasks suffer from jamming and excessive forces due to tight-tolerance insertions; existing methods struggle with robust and safe policy learning under contact-rich conditions.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: Stage 1 uses Imitation Learning (IL) for reaching policy with position generalization; Stage 2 uses Reinforcement Learning (RL) for insertion and failure recovery. Introduces tactile group sampling for better tactile coverage and a tactile critic for accurate policy evaluation.
AI.result: Tested on 5 hole geometries and 3 clearances; 67% success at 0.05mm clearance; 60% reduction in max force and 44% reduction in torque compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tactile-augmented two-stage method significantly improves both success rate and safety for precision insertion tasks, validating its effectiveness for high-precision assembly.

title: Investigating Trustworthiness of Nonparametric Deep Survival Models for Alzheimer's Disease Progression Analysis
comment: 12 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables, IEEE/ACM Conference on Connected Health: Applications, Systems, and Engineering Technologies
summary: Alzheimer's Dementia (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease marked by irreversible decline, making reliable modeling of its progression essential for effective patient care. Progression-aware methods such as survival analysis are therefore crucial tools for the early detection and monitoring of AD. Recent advancements in deep learning have demonstrated remarkable performance in survival tasks, but alarmingly fewer studies have been conducted in the domain of AD. Further, the studies that do exist do not consider learned bias within the model itself, which could result in unfair and unreliable predictions toward certain marginalized groups. As such, we conduct a rigorous study of fairness in AD progression analysis along with a thorough feature importance study to determine the characteristics which are most important for reliable AD predictions. Furthermore, we propose two novel fairness metrics, called Time-Dependent Concordance Impurity and Kaplan-Meier Fairness, to quantify bias with respect to sensitive attributes such as sex, race, and education in nonparametric survival models. Our study demonstrates that while deep learning powered survival models are robust tools which can aid clinicians in AD care decisions, they often exhibit considerable bias, representing important avenues for future research.

AI.tldr: This paper studies fairness in Alzheimer's Disease progression analysis using deep learning survival models, proposes two novel fairness metrics (Time-Dependent Concordance Impurity and Kaplan-Meier Fairness), and finds that while these models are robust tools, they exhibit considerable bias toward marginalized groups regarding sex, race, and education.
AI.motivation: Reliable modeling of AD progression is essential for patient care, but existing deep learning survival models for AD do not consider learned bias, potentially resulting in unfair and unreliable predictions for marginalized groups.
AI.method: The authors conduct rigorous fairness analysis in AD progression using deep survival models, perform feature importance studies, and propose two novel fairness metrics: Time-Dependent Concordance Impurity and Kaplan-Meier Fairness to quantify bias regarding sensitive attributes (sex, race, education) in nonparametric survival models.
AI.result: Deep learning-powered survival models are robust tools for AD care decisions, but they exhibit considerable bias toward marginalized groups based on sex, race, and education.
AI.conclusion: Fairness in AD progression analysis is an important avenue for future research, as current deep survival models show significant bias despite their robustness.

title: When Context Hurts: The Crossover Effect of Knowledge Transfer on Multi-Agent Design Exploration
comment: 16 pages, 14 tables. 2,700 multi-agent experiments across 10 software design tasks, 7 artifact conditions, and 4 convergence pressure levels
summary: The prevailing assumption in agent orchestration is that more context is better. We test this on multi-agent software design across 10 tasks, 7 context-injection conditions, and over 2,700 runs, and find a crossover effect: the same artifact type improves design exploration on some tasks (up to 20$\times$ tradeoff coverage) and actively degrades it on others (up to 46% reduction). On several tasks, an irrelevant document performs as well as or better than every relevant artifact. The direction is predicted by a single measurable variable--baseline exploration without context--with Pearson $r = -0.82$ ($p < 0.001$). Probing the mechanism by manipulating convergence pressure through prompt design reveals two distinct regimes: convergence driven by training data priors (natural) responds to artifact disruption, while convergence driven by explicit instructions (induced) does not. The implication is that context injection should be conditional, not universal: one no-context trial is a cheap diagnostic that predicts whether knowledge artifacts will help or hurt a given task.

AI.tldr: Adding more context in multi-agent software design can either help or hurt performance depending on the task; a single no-context trial predicts the direction with high accuracy.
AI.motivation: Challenges the prevailing assumption that more context is always better in agent orchestration.
AI.method: Tested multi-agent software design across 10 tasks, 7 context-injection conditions, and 2,700+ runs; measured baseline exploration and convergence pressure.
AI.result: Found a crossover effect: context improves exploration up to 20× on some tasks but reduces it by 46% on others. Baseline exploration predicts direction (r=-0.82). Irrelevant documents sometimes outperformed relevant ones.
AI.conclusion: Context injection should be conditional, not universal; one cheap no-context trial can diagnose whether artifacts will help or hurt.

title: A Comparison Between Co-Located and Distributed MIMO Deployments in OFDM-ISAC Networks
comment: Accepted to the 32nd International Conference on Telecommunications (ICT 2026), Thessaloniki, Greece
summary: This paper investigates network-level integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) under two fundamentally different topology configurations: cell-free massive MIMO (CF-mMIMO) and multi-cell massive MIMO (MC-mMIMO). A unified OFDM-based waveform is adopted for both architectures as the key enabler for ISAC functionalities. The CF system exploits distributed access points (APs) and a scalable user-target-centric operation, whereas the MC system relies on co-located transmit-receive arrays with conventional cell-centric deployment. For both architectures, we derive a GLRT-based sensing detector and the corresponding sensing SNR expressions. We then examine a series of case studies investigating how the number of OFDM subcarriers, the transceiver allocation strategy, and the antenna/node distribution across the network affect the sensing performance. The results consistently demonstrate that CF-mMIMO provides more robust and higher sensing performance across most tested scenarios, particularly when transmit resources or antenna elements are spatially distributed. These findings highlight the inherent advantages of CF deployments for next-generation ISAC networks.

AI.tldr: This paper compares cell-free massive MIMO (CF-mMIMO) and multi-cell massive MIMO (MC-mMIMO) for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC), showing that CF-mMIMO achieves more robust and higher sensing performance, especially with distributed transmit resources and antenna elements.
AI.motivation: To investigate how two fundamentally different network topology configurations (cell-free vs. multi-cell massive MIMO) affect sensing performance in ISAC systems, identifying which architecture better supports next-generation ISAC networks.
AI.method: Adopting a unified OFDM-based waveform for both architectures, deriving GLRT-based sensing detectors and sensing SNR expressions, and conducting case studies varying the number of OFDM subcarriers, transceiver allocation strategies, and antenna/node distributions.
AI.result: CF-mMIMO consistently delivers more robust and higher sensing performance across most scenarios, particularly when transmit resources or antenna elements are spatially distributed.
AI.conclusion: CF-mMIMO's distributed architecture offers inherent advantages over conventional MC-mMIMO for next-generation ISAC networks, making it a preferable deployment topology.

title: Differential Spatial Modulation with Transmit Diversity for Pinching-Antenna Systems
summary: Pinching antenna (PA) systems provide a new spatial degree of freedom by flexible activation of pinching positions. However, the resulting effective channel strongly depends on the activated pinching positions, rendering conventional coherent transmission generally relies on accurate acquisition of instantaneous channel state information (CSI) and incurring substantial pilot overhead. To address this challenge, we propose a differential spatial modulation (DSM) scheme for PA systems, termed as DSM-PA. Specifically, a differential transmission scheme with an embedded Alamouti coding structure is designed, where information bits are conveyed via phase variations between adjacent symbol blocks. This design enables noncoherent transmission without requiring instantaneous CSI while simultaneously achieving transmit diversity. Moreover, to fully exploit the spatial degrees of freedom of PA systems, a pinching position-based index modulation (IM) rule is developed to enhance spectral efficiency. An asymptotically tight upper bound on the average bit error rate (BER) over quasi-static Rician fading channels is derived using the moment-generating function (MGF) method. The diversity analysis also reveals that the proposed DSM-PA scheme achieves full transmit diversity. Finally, simulation results verify the accuracy of the BER analysis and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed DSM-PA scheme.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a differential spatial modulation (DSM) scheme for pinching antenna (PA) systems to enable noncoherent transmission without requiring instantaneous CSI, while achieving transmit diversity and enhanced spectral efficiency through pinching position-based index modulation.
AI.motivation: Conventional coherent transmission in PA systems relies on accurate instantaneous CSI acquisition, which incurs substantial pilot overhead due to the strong dependence of the effective channel on activated pinching positions.
AI.method: A differential spatial modulation (DSM) scheme with embedded Alamouti coding structure is designed, where information is conveyed via phase variations between adjacent symbol blocks. A pinching position-based index modulation rule is developed to exploit spatial degrees of freedom.
AI.result: An asymptotically tight upper bound on average BER over quasi-static Rician fading channels is derived using MGF method. Diversity analysis shows full transmit diversity is achieved. Simulations verify the BER analysis and demonstrate scheme effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DSM-PA scheme enables noncoherent transmission without CSI, achieves full transmit diversity, and enhances spectral efficiency via position-based index modulation, effectively addressing the pilot overhead challenge in PA systems.

title: Detecting Deepfakes via Hamiltonian Dynamics
summary: Driven by the rapid development of generative AI models, deepfake detectors are compelled to undergo periodic recalibration to capture newly developed synthetic artifacts. To break this cycle, we propose a new perspective on deepfake detection: moving from static pattern recognition to dynamical stability analysis. Specifically, our approach is motivated by physics-inspired priors: we hypothesize that natural images, as products of dissipative physical processes, tend to settle near stable, low-energy equilibria. In contrast, generative models optimize for statistical similarity to real images but do not explicitly enforce structural constraints such as geometric smoothness, leaving deepfakes more likely to occupy unstable, high-energy states. To operationalize this, we introduce Hamiltonian Action Anomaly Detection (HAAD), comprising three contributions: \textbf{i)} We model the image latent manifold as a potential energy surface. Under this hypothesis, real images are expected to produce basin-like low-energy responses, whereas fake images are more likely to induce high-potential, high-gradient responses. \textbf{ii)} We employ Hamiltonian-inspired dynamics as a stability probe. By releasing latent states from rest, samples near stable regions remain bounded, while high-gradient samples produce larger trajectory responses. \textbf{iii)} We quantify these dynamic behaviors through two trajectory statistics, \ie, Hamiltonian action and energy dissipation. Extensive experiments show that HAAD outperforms evaluated state-of-the-art baselines on challenging cross-dataset transfer benchmarks, supporting a physics-inspired stability prior for digital forensics.

AI.tldr: HAAD proposes a physics-inspired deepfake detection method that distinguishes real from fake images by analyzing dynamical stability on a latent potential energy surface, outperforming SOTA on cross-dataset benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Deepfake detectors require constant recalibration to keep up with evolving generative models. The authors aim to break this cycle by shifting from static pattern recognition to dynamical stability analysis grounded in physics principles.
AI.method: (1) Model the image latent manifold as a potential energy surface. (2) Use Hamiltonian-inspired dynamics as a stability probe—release latent states from rest and observe trajectories. (3) Quantify trajectory behaviors via Hamiltonian action and energy dissipation to distinguish real (stable, low-energy) from fake (unstable, high-energy) images.
AI.result: HAAD outperforms evaluated state-of-the-art baselines on challenging cross-dataset transfer benchmarks, supporting the effectiveness of physics-inspired stability priors for deepfake detection.
AI.conclusion: Physics-inspired dynamical stability analysis offers a promising new direction for deepfake detection that may reduce the need for constant recalibration against emerging generative models.

title: AI-Aided Advancements in Autonomous Underwater Vehicle Navigation
summary: Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) have become indispensable for deep-sea exploration, spanning critical scientific research and commercial applications. The rapid attenuation of electromagnetic waves renders satellite radio signals unavailable, while the dynamic unpredictability of the marine environment presents formidable navigation challenges. This chapter explores recent advancements in AI-aided AUV positioning, specifically focusing on advanced sensor fusion architectures that integrate inertial navigation systems with Doppler velocity logs and cameras. Beyond traditional model-based filtering, we examine the transformative emergence of AI-driven learning approaches in enhancing inertial dead-reckoning tasks and adaptive fusion algorithms. By addressing these recent milestones, this chapter provides a comprehensive roadmap for achieving the high-precision navigation essential for autonomous underwater missions.

AI.tldr: This chapter reviews AI-aided AUV positioning, focusing on sensor fusion architectures (INS, DVL, cameras) and AI-driven learning approaches for enhanced inertial dead-reckoning and adaptive fusion to achieve high-precision underwater navigation.
AI.motivation: AUVs are essential for deep-sea exploration but face challenges due to electromagnetic wave attenuation (no satellite signals) and unpredictable marine environments, motivating the need for advanced positioning and navigation solutions.
AI.method: The chapter examines advanced sensor fusion architectures combining inertial navigation systems with Doppler velocity logs and cameras, alongside AI-driven learning approaches for inertial dead-reckoning and adaptive fusion algorithms.
AI.result: Recent milestones in AI-aided positioning show transformative improvements over traditional model-based filtering for underwater navigation accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The chapter provides a comprehensive roadmap for achieving high-precision navigation essential for autonomous underwater missions through AI-enhanced sensor fusion.

title: Improving Medical VQA through Trajectory-Aware Process Supervision
summary:
Reasoning capabilities are crucial for reliable medical visual question answering (VQA); however, existing datasets rarely include reasoning explanations.
  We address this by generating reasoning trajectories for six medical VQA benchmarks using the COMCTS algorithm with open-source vision-language models, with an LLM serving as the verification judge.
  Building on these generated datasets, we propose a two-stage training framework: supervised fine-tuning followed by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a novel process-based reward.
  While standard approaches rely solely on exact-match rewards for final answers, we introduce a trajectory-aware reward that measures the similarity between generated and ground-truth reasoning processes.
  Specifically, we embed reasoning steps using sentence transformers and compute the Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) distance between the resulting vector sequences.
  Experiments across six benchmarks demonstrate that combining the DTW-based process reward with exact-match reward consistently outperforms SFT-only training, raising mean accuracy from 0.598 to 0.689, mean BERTScore from 0.845 to 0.881, and mean ROUGE-L from 0.665 to 0.748.
  Our results highlight the importance of process supervision in training reasoning-capable medical VLMs.
  We make our code and generated reasoning datasets publicly available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/MICCAI-R1-MED-VQA-code-B14B/

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a two-stage training framework (SFT + GRPO) with a novel DTW-based process reward to improve reasoning in medical VQA, using COMCTS-generated reasoning trajectories and achieving significant performance gains across six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reasoning capabilities are crucial for reliable medical VQA, but existing datasets rarely include reasoning explanations. The authors aim to address this gap by generating reasoning trajectories and introducing process supervision for training.
AI.method: (1) Generate reasoning trajectories for six medical VQA benchmarks using COMCTS algorithm with open-source VLMs, verified by an LLM judge. (2) Two-stage training: supervised fine-tuning followed by GRPO with a trajectory-aware reward. (3) The reward uses sentence transformers to embed reasoning steps and computes DTW distance between generated and ground-truth reasoning processes, combined with exact-match reward.
AI.result: Combining DTW-based process reward with exact-match reward outperforms SFT-only training: mean accuracy from 0.598→0.689, BERTScore from 0.845→0.881, ROUGE-L from 0.665→0.748 across six benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Process supervision (DTW-based trajectory reward) is crucial for training reasoning-capable medical VLMs. Code and datasets are publicly released.

title: Deployment-Relevant Alignment Cannot Be Inferred from Model-Level Evaluation Alone
summary: Alignment evaluation in machine learning has largely become evaluation of models. Influential benchmarks score model outputs under fixed inputs, such as truthfulness, instruction following, or pairwise preference, and these scores are often used to support claims about deployed alignment. This paper argues that deployment-relevant alignment cannot be inferred from model-level evaluation alone. Alignment claims should instead be indexed to the level at which evidence is collected: model-level, response-level, interaction-level, or deployment-level. Two studies support this position. First, a structured audit of eleven alignment benchmarks, extended to a sixteen-benchmark corpus, dual-coded against an eight-dimension rubric with Cohen's kappa = 0.87, finds that user-facing verification support is absent across every benchmark examined, while process steerability is nearly absent. The few interactional benchmarks identified, including tau-bench, CURATe, Rifts, and Common Ground, remain fragmented in coverage, and benchmark construction rather than data source determines what is measured. Second, a blinded cross-model stress test using 180 transcripts across three frontier models and four scaffolds finds that the same verification scaffold raises one model's verification support to ceiling while leaving another categorically unchanged. This shows that scaffold efficacy is model-dependent and that the gap identified by the audit cannot be closed at the model level alone. We propose a system-level evaluation agenda: alignment profiles instead of single scores, fixed-scaffolding protocols for comparable interactional evaluation, and reporting templates that make the inferential distance between evaluation evidence and deployment claims explicit.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that alignment evaluation cannot be determined from model-level benchmarks alone, and proposes system-level evaluation including alignment profiles, fixed-scaffolding protocols, and explicit reporting of inferential distance.
AI.motivation: Current alignment benchmarks evaluate models under fixed inputs and claim to support deployment-relevant conclusions, but deployment alignment requires evidence at higher levels (response, interaction, deployment) that current benchmarks fail to provide.
AI.method: Two studies: (1) Structured audit of 11-16 alignment benchmarks dual-coded against an 8-dimension rubric (Cohen's kappa=0.87); (2) Blinded cross-model stress test using 180 transcripts across 3 frontier models and 4 scaffolds to test verification support.
AI.result: Audit found user-facing verification support absent and process steerability nearly absent across all benchmarks. Stress test showed scaffold efficacy is model-dependent—same verification scaffold raised one model to ceiling while leaving another unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Alignment claims should be indexed to evidence level (model, response, interaction, deployment). Proposed system-level evaluation agenda: alignment profiles, fixed-scaffolding protocols, and reporting templates for inferential distance.

title: Age of Gossip in Ring Networks With Non-Poisson Updates
summary: We consider a network consisting of $n$ nodes connected in a ring formation and a source that generates updates according to a renewal process and disseminates them to the ring network according to a Poisson process. The nodes in the network gossip with each other according to a push-based gossiping protocol, and disseminate version updates. Gossip between two neighbors happens at the arrivals of renewal processes with finite mean and variance. All renewal processes and Poisson processes in the network are independent but not identically distributed. We consider both uni-directional ring networks and bi-directional ring networks. We use version age of information to quantify the freshness of information at each node. Prior work has used the stochastic hybrid systems (SHS) approach or a first passage percolation (FPP) approach to analyze ring networks with edges following identical Poisson processes. In this work, we use a sample-path backtracking approach to characterize the probabilistic scaling of the version age of information of an arbitrary node in the gossip network, where each edge follows an independent but not identically distributed renewal process. We show that the version age of information of any node in the network is stochastically equivalent to $\sqrt{n}$ at any time instant after the node has received its first update from the source.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes version age of information in ring gossip networks with non-identical renewal processes on edges, showing via a sample-path backtracking approach that version age scales stochastically as √n for any node.
AI.motivation: Prior work on ring networks assumed identical Poisson processes on edges. This work relaxes that assumption to handle independent but not identically distributed renewal processes, which better reflects realistic network heterogeneity.
AI.method: The authors use a sample-path backtracking approach (instead of SHS or FPP) to characterize the probabilistic scaling of version age in both uni-directional and bi-directional ring gossip networks with general renewal processes.
AI.result: The version age of information of any node in the ring network is stochastically equivalent to √n at any time after the node receives its first update from the source.
AI.conclusion: The √n scaling result holds even under heterogeneous renewal edge processes, generalizing prior results that required identical Poisson assumptions.

title: Two-Point Resolution in Spectral Super-Resolution
summary: Two-point super-resolution is an important problem in many signal processing applications. In this paper, we aim to establish a resolution theory for two-point super-resolution from a single snapshot. We consider a complex two-point model with unequal amplitudes and a nontrivial relative phase, and derive super-resolution upper bounds (SRUs) guaranteeing resolvability as well as super-resolution lower bounds (SRLs) below which stable reconstruction is impossible. The resulting bounds provide an explicit characterization of how the amplitude ratio and, more importantly, the relative phase affect the resolution limit for both source-number detection and location estimation. In the in-phase regime, the classical resolution exponents are retained: \((σ/m)^{1/2}\) for source-number detection and \((σ/m)^{1/3}\) for location estimation. In the out-of-phase regimes, the phase term significantly changes the resolution limit: it acts as a direct subtractive term in the near-endpoint regime, and improves the scaling orders in the large-phase regime to \(σ/m\) for source-number detection and \((σ/m)^{1/2}\) for location estimation. Extensive numerical experiments across different phase regimes and reconstruction algorithms validate the predicted scaling laws and theoretical resolution boundaries. Moreover, comparison with our resolution limit in all phase regimes reveals the optimality of \(\ell_0\), ML, and ESPRIT algorithms, and the non-optimality of SVT, MUSIC, and the convex method, a finding that, to the best of our knowledge, has not been reported before. Collectively, our results show that the phase of amplitudes is not merely a nuisance in super-resolution, but a key factor that can be exploited to improve stable resolvability.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a resolution theory for two-point super-resolution from a single snapshot, deriving super-resolution upper and lower bounds that characterize how amplitude ratio and relative phase affect resolution limits for source-number detection and location estimation. The phase is shown to significantly alter resolution scaling laws, from classical exponents to improved orders in certain regimes, with optimality analysis of various algorithms.
AI.motivation: To establish a rigorous resolution theory for two-point super-resolution from a single snapshot, particularly focusing on how unequal amplitudes and nontrivial relative phase affect the fundamental limits of resolvability, moving beyond the common assumption that phase is merely a nuisance parameter.
AI.method: The authors consider a complex two-point model with unequal amplitudes and relative phase, derive super-resolution upper bounds (SRUs) and super-resolution lower bounds (SRLs), analyze scaling laws across different phase regimes (in-phase, near-endpoint, large-phase), and validate their theoretical predictions through numerical experiments with various reconstruction algorithms (ℓ0, ML, ESPRIT, SVT, MUSIC, convex method).
AI.result: In the in-phase regime, classical resolution exponents (σ/m)^{1/2} for detection and (σ/m)^{1/3} for location estimation are retained. In out-of-phase regimes, phase acts as a subtractive term in near-endpoint regime and improves scaling to σ/m for detection and (σ/m)^{1/2} for location estimation in large-phase regime. ℓ0, ML, and ESPRIT are shown to be optimal; SVT, MUSIC, and convex method are not.
AI.conclusion: The phase of amplitudes is not merely a nuisance in super-resolution but a key factor that can be exploited to significantly improve stable resolvability, with phase-dependent resolution scaling laws providing both theoretical insights and practical implications for algorithm selection.

title: UAV as Urban Construction Change Monitor: A New Benchmark and Change Captioning Model
summary: Remote Sensing Image Change Captioning (RSICC) aims to generate spatially grounded natural language descriptions of scene evolution from bi-temporal imagery, moving beyond binary change masks toward semantic-level understanding. However, existing methods rely on implicit feature differencing without explicitly modeling structured change semantics, and struggle to reconcile the conflicting representation demands of change detection and caption generation. In addition, current benchmarks provide limited coverage of high-resolution urban construction scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose PTNet, a prototype-guided task-adaptive framework for joint change captioning and detection. PTNet explicitly models structured change semantics through a learnable prototype bank that guides cross-temporal interaction, disentangles task-specific representations via multi-head gating, and injects detection-derived spatial priors into caption generation, enabling coherent semantic correspondence while preserving fine-grained spatial sensitivity. Furthermore, we construct UCCD, a large-scale UAV-based benchmark comprising 9,000 high-resolution image pairs and 45,000 annotated sentences for urban construction monitoring. Extensive experiments on UCCD and WHU-CDC demonstrate that PTNet consistently outperforms existing methods. The dataset and source code are publicly available at https://github.com/G124556/ptnet.

AI.tldr: PTNet is a prototype-guided task-adaptive framework for joint remote sensing image change captioning and detection, which uses a learnable prototype bank to model structured change semantics and disentangle task-specific representations. The authors also introduce UCCD, a large-scale UAV-based benchmark for urban construction monitoring with 9,000 image pairs and 45,000 sentences.
AI.motivation: Existing RSICC methods rely on implicit feature differencing without explicit structured change semantics, struggle with conflicting demands of change detection vs. caption generation, and current benchmarks lack high-resolution urban construction coverage.
AI.method: PTNet uses: (1) a learnable prototype bank to model structured change semantics and guide cross-temporal interaction, (2) multi-head gating to disentangle task-specific representations, and (3) injection of detection-derived spatial priors into caption generation.
AI.result: PTNet consistently outperforms existing methods on both UCCD and WHU-CDC benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed prototype-guided framework effectively reconciles change detection and caption generation tasks, and the new UCCD benchmark advances urban construction monitoring research.

title: From Pixels to Tokens: A Systematic Study of Latent Action Supervision for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Latent actions serve as an intermediate representation that enables consistent modeling of vision-language-action (VLA) models across heterogeneous datasets. However, approaches to supervising VLAs with latent actions are fragmented and lack a systematic comparison. This work structures the study of latent action supervision from two perspectives: (i) regularizing the trajectory via image-based latent actions, and (ii) unifying the target space with action-based latent actions. Under a unified VLA baseline, we instantiate and compare four representative integration strategies. Our results reveal a formulation-task correspondence: image-based latent actions benefit long-horizon reasoning and scene-level generalization, whereas action-based latent actions excel at complex motor coordination. Furthermore, we find that directly supervising the VLM with discrete latent action tokens yields the most effective performance. Finally, our experiments offer initial insights into the benefits of latent action supervision in mixed-data, suggesting a promising direction for VLA training. Code is available at https://github.com/RUCKBReasoning/From_Pixels_to_Tokens.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically compares four strategies for supervising vision-language-action (VLA) models using latent actions, finding that image-based latent actions benefit long-horizon reasoning, action-based latent actions excel at motor coordination, and direct supervision with discrete latent action tokens works best.
AI.motivation: Latent actions are used as intermediate representations for consistent VLA modeling across heterogeneous datasets, but existing approaches to supervising VLAs with latent actions are fragmented and lack systematic comparison.
AI.method: The authors structure latent action supervision from two perspectives: (i) regularizing trajectories via image-based latent actions, and (ii) unifying the target space with action-based latent actions. Under a unified VLA baseline, they instantiate and compare four representative integration strategies.
AI.result: There is a formulation-task correspondence: image-based latent actions benefit long-horizon reasoning and scene-level generalization, while action-based latent actions excel at complex motor coordination. Directly supervising the VLM with discrete latent action tokens yields the most effective performance.
AI.conclusion: The experiments offer initial insights into the benefits of latent action supervision in mixed-data training, suggesting a promising direction for VLA training.

title: Designing a double deep reinforcement learning selection tool for resilient demand prediction
summary: The use of artificial intelligence in supply chain forecasting has attracted many scientific studies for several decades. However, the process of selecting an appropriate forecasting solution becomes a daunting task. This complexity arises due to the distinct features inherent to each dataset. Research to tackle this issue has been performed since the eighties but recent development of demand forecasting has opened new perspectives. This research aims to enhance automatic forecasting model selection by proposing a novel architecture that acts as a double deep reinforcement learning agent, selecting automatically a forecasting model from the forecasting committee at the time of prediction. Moreover, a novel early-stopping approach based on average reward convergence has been introduced to expedite training time. To evaluate the model's performance, an empirical study was conducted utilizing grocery sales datasets and snack demands datasets. The experimental results demonstrate the robustness of the proposed approach when compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a double deep reinforcement learning agent for automatic supply chain forecasting model selection, along with a novel early-stopping method based on average reward convergence. The approach was validated on grocery and snack demand datasets, showing robustness against state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Selecting an appropriate forecasting solution is difficult due to distinct dataset features. The process has been studied since the 1980s, but recent developments in demand forecasting open new perspectives for improvement.
AI.method: A double deep reinforcement learning agent architecture that automatically selects a forecasting model from a committee at prediction time. Additionally, a novel early-stopping approach based on average reward convergence is introduced to reduce training time.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on grocery sales and snack demand datasets demonstrates the robustness of the proposed approach compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The double deep reinforcement learning agent with average-reward-based early stopping offers a robust solution for automatic forecasting model selection in supply chain contexts.

title: How Does Thinking Mode Change LLM Moral Judgments? A Controlled Instant-vs-Thinking Comparison Across Five Frontier Models
summary: We evaluate whether enabling provider-exposed reasoning mode changes moral judgments within the same model checkpoint. Across 100 moral-judgment scenarios and five frontier reasoning-trained LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT 5.5, Gemini 3 Flash, DeepSeek V3.1, and Qwen3.5 397B), aggregate binary-verdict agreement remains high and statistically indistinguishable between instant and thinking modes (Krippendorff's alpha = 0.78 vs. 0.79). However, disagreement is concentrated in 21 model-disputed scenarios, where instant-mode agreement is near chance (alpha = 0.08). On these scenarios, reasoning directionally narrows cross-model disagreement, increasing mean pairwise agreement from 5.4 to 6.7 out of 10. Reasoning also reduces demographic-judgment inconsistency in three of five models and does not increase it for any model. Across all five model families, reasoning changes self-labeled ethical frameworks more often than binary verdicts.

AI.tldr: Enabling reasoning mode in frontier LLMs does not significantly change aggregate moral judgments, but it reduces cross-model disagreement on disputed scenarios and improves demographic consistency without increasing bias.
AI.motivation: To determine whether exposing the reasoning process of LLMs alters their moral judgments, and whether this leads to more consistent or more biased ethical decisions.
AI.method: Evaluated 5 frontier reasoning-trained LLMs across 100 moral-judgment scenarios in both instant and thinking modes, measuring binary-verdict agreement, cross-model disagreement, demographic consistency, and ethical framework shifts.
AI.result: Aggregate agreement is high and similar between modes (alpha ≈ 0.78–0.79). On 21 disputed scenarios, instant-mode agreement is near chance (alpha = 0.08), while reasoning improves it (mean pairwise agreement from 5.4 to 6.7/10). Reasoning reduces demographic inconsistency in 3/5 models and changes ethical frameworks more often than binary verdicts.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning mode does not fundamentally alter aggregate moral judgments but modestly improves cross-model consistency on difficult cases and can reduce demographic biases, though it shifts underlying ethical reasoning more than surface-level decisions.

title: Private Structured-Subset Retrieval
summary: We introduce the \emph{Private Structured-Subset Retrieval (PSSR)} problem, where a user retrieves $D$ messages from a database of $K$ messages replicated across $N$ non-colluding servers, and the demand is restricted to a known structured family of $D$-subsets. This formulation generalizes classical Private Information Retrieval (PIR) and multi-message PIR (MPIR), and captures settings where the demand space is constrained by application-specific structure. Focusing on balanced ${\{0,1\}}$-linear schemes, we derive converse bounds on the maximum retrieval rate and minimum subpacketization level, and develop an optimization-based framework for constructing schemes for general structured demand families. Our results show that, for certain families, the PSSR rate converse bound can exceed the best-known MPIR rate upper bound; when this PSSR bound is achievable, MPIR rate-optimal schemes become suboptimal for those families. By exploiting demand structure, our PSSR schemes achieve higher retrieval rates for many families and never underperform the best-known balanced ${\{0,1\}}$-linear MPIR schemes. Our results also show that demand structure can reduce the required subpacketization even when the optimal rate is unchanged. Our parallel work on contiguous-demand families further illustrates the scope of this framework by yielding rate-optimal schemes with substantially smaller subpacketization and no field-size restrictions, improving upon MPIR-based schemes.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Private Structured-Subset Retrieval (PSSR), a generalization of PIR and MPIR where the user's demand is restricted to a known structured family of D-subsets. Using balanced {0,1}-linear schemes, the authors derive rate bounds and subpacketization limits, showing that exploiting demand structure can improve retrieval rates and reduce subpacketization compared to standard MPIR schemes.
AI.motivation: To generalize classical PIR and MPIR problems by accounting for structured demand constraints that arise in practical applications, and to show that exploiting such structure can yield better rates and lower subpacketization than existing MPIR approaches.
AI.method: The paper develops converse bounds on maximum retrieval rate and minimum subpacketization for balanced {0,1}-linear schemes, and proposes an optimization-based framework for constructing PSSR schemes tailored to general structured demand families.
AI.result: PSSR rate bounds can exceed the best-known MPIR rate upper bounds; when achievable, MPIR rate-optimal schemes are suboptimal for certain demand families. PSSR schemes achieve higher rates for many families and never underperform balanced {0,1}-linear MPIR schemes. Demand structure can reduce required subpacketization even when optimal rate is unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting demand structure via PSSR yields rate improvements and subpacketization reductions compared to MPIR. A parallel work on contiguous-demand families further demonstrates the framework's effectiveness with rate-optimal schemes having smaller subpacketization and no field-size restrictions.

title: Multi-AP Cooperative Beamforming for Cell-Free ISAC Networks: Balancing Communication SINR and Sensing SCNR
summary: Cell-free integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems are facing the resource allocation challenges due to the deployment of access points (APs) and conflicting beamforming requirements between the communication and sensing functions. Unlike traditional ISAC architectures, the geographic distribution of APs introduces coordination complexity and resource-sharing conflicts that existing single-objective methods cannot adequately address. To address this challenge, we formulate an optimization problem for multi-AP cooperative beamforming that maximizes the sensing signal-to-clutter-plus-noise ratio (SCNR) under the communication rate constraints. The non-convex quadratically constrained quadratic program is transformed into a tractable convex semidefinite program via semidefinite relaxation, enabling efficient polynomial-time solutions and overcoming the local convergence limitations of traditional alternating optimization approaches. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves superior performance in both communication signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) and SCNR compared to existing schemes, confirming its effectiveness for balancing dual-functional objectives.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-AP cooperative beamforming optimization framework for cell-free ISAC systems, using semidefinite relaxation to solve a non-convex QCQP that maximizes sensing SCNR under communication rate constraints, achieving superior SINR and SCNR performance.
AI.motivation: Cell-free ISAC systems face resource allocation challenges due to distributed AP deployment and conflicting beamforming requirements between communication and sensing functions, which existing single-objective methods cannot adequately address.
AI.method: Formulates a multi-AP cooperative beamforming optimization problem to maximize sensing SCNR under communication rate constraints; transforms the non-convex QCQP into a convex semidefinite program via semidefinite relaxation for efficient polynomial-time solution.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieves superior performance in both communication SINR and sensing SCNR compared to existing schemes, overcoming local convergence limitations of traditional alternating optimization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively balances dual-functional objectives in cell-free ISAC systems, confirming its effectiveness for coordinated multi-AP beamforming.

title: Evaluation Cards for XAI Metrics
comment: 7 pages. Accepted at the 5th XAI4CV Workshop, CVPR 2026 (non-archival)
summary: The evaluation of explainable AI (XAI) methods is affected by a lack of standardization. Metrics are inconsistently defined, incompletely reported, and rarely validated against common baselines. In this paper, we identify transparency of evaluation reporting as a central, under-addressed problem. We propose the XAI Evaluation Card, a documentation template analogous to model cards, designed to accompany any study that introduces an XAI evaluation metric. The card covers explicit declaration of target properties, grounding levels, metric assumptions, validation evidence, gaming risks, and known failure cases. We argue that adopting this template as a community norm would reduce evaluation fragmentation, support meta-analysis, and improve accountability in XAI research.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes XAI Evaluation Card, a standardized documentation template for reporting XAI evaluation metrics, aiming to address the lack of transparency and standardization in XAI evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current XAI evaluation suffers from inconsistent metric definitions, incomplete reporting, and lack of validation against baselines. The paper identifies transparency of evaluation reporting as a central, under-addressed problem.
AI.method: The authors propose the XAI Evaluation Card, a documentation template analogous to model cards, covering explicit declaration of target properties, grounding levels, metric assumptions, validation evidence, gaming risks, and known failure cases.
AI.result: A structured template (XAI Evaluation Card) designed to accompany any study introducing an XAI evaluation metric.
AI.conclusion: Adopting this template as a community norm would reduce evaluation fragmentation, support meta-analysis, and improve accountability in XAI research.

title: 3D Printing of Passively Actuated Self-Folding Robots with Integrated Functional Modules
comment: 8pages, 10 figures, This paper is accepted in ICRA 2026
summary: We introduce an elastic-driven self-folding approach that fabricates robots directly from flat 3D-printed conductive PLA nets. Elastic bands routed through printed hooks store energy that folds the sheet into programmed 3D geometries, while the flat state allows accurate placement of electronics and magnets before deployment. The same substrate doubles as electrodes for capacitive touch and supports a reusable platform I/O palette with Hall sensors and eccentric rotating mass (ERM) motors for docking detection and vibration actuation. We also derive a closed-form folding model that balances hinge stiffness with elastic band moment to predict equilibrium fold angles; experiments validate the model and yield a design map linking hinge thickness, band size, and hook spacing to target angles. Using this workflow we realize multiple polyhedral modules and demonstrate three applications: a cube that highlights the potential of self-folding for scalable modular robot collectives, a deployable gripper, and a tendon-driven finger. The method is low cost, stimulus-free, and integrates actuation and sensing.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a low-cost, stimulus-free self-folding method using elastic bands routed through 3D-printed conductive PLA nets to create robotic modules with integrated sensing and actuation.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, low-cost self-folding robots that integrate actuation and sensing directly into the substrate without external stimuli.
AI.method: Elastic bands routed through 3D-printed PLA nets provide fold energy; a closed-form model predicts fold angles; the substrate doubles as capacitive touch electrodes, with integrated Hall sensors and ERM motors.
AI.result: Validated folding model with design maps; demonstrated cube (modular collectives), deployable gripper, and tendon-driven finger.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a stimulus-free, low-cost pathway for fabricating self-folding robots with integrated electronics, sensing, and actuation.

title: LAWS: Learning from Actual Workloads Symbolically -- A Self-Certifying Parametrized Cache Architecture for Neural Inference, Robotics, and Edge Deployment
comment: 45 pages. Companion paper to arXiv:2604.06228 (Probabilistic Language Tries)
summary: We introduce LAWS (Learning from Actual Workloads Symbolically), a self-certifying inference caching architecture that builds a growing library of certified expert functions from deployment observations. Each expert covers a region of input space defined by a node in the Probabilistic Language Trie (PLT) of the base model and carries a formal error bound holding uniformly over all inputs. The central result is a self-certification theorem: for any input x, the LAWS approximation error is bounded by epsilon_fit + 2*Lambda(W)*C_E, where Lambda(W) is the model Lipschitz constant, C_E is the maximum embedding diameter, and epsilon_fit is the expert training error -- all checkable at deployment time without ground truth. We prove that LAWS generalizes both Mixture-of-Experts and KV prefix caching as special cases and is strictly more expressive than any fixed-K MoE or finite cache. Further results include a monotone hit rate theorem (any-match routing ensures coverage only increases), an expert library growth rate of O(2^H log N) where H is workload entropy, a fleet learning convergence theorem with Omega(K) speedup for K-unit fleets, and an over-the-air update bandwidth bound. We conjecture that LAWS is acquisition-optimal among stationary online caching algorithms and that the effective Lipschitz constant on the training distribution grows polynomially rather than exponentially in depth. Applications are developed for LLM inference, robotic control, and multi-agent edge deployment.

AI.tldr: LAWS is a self-certifying inference caching architecture that builds an expert library from deployment data, with formal error bounds and provable guarantees on generalization, growth rate, and fleet learning speedup.
AI.motivation: To combine the benefits of Mixture-of-Experts and KV prefix caching into a unified framework with formal error guarantees that can be checked at deployment time without ground truth labels.
AI.method: LAWS constructs experts from Probabilistic Language Trie (PLT) nodes, each covering a region of input space with formal error bounds. The architecture uses any-match routing and self-certification via a theorem bounding approximation error by epsilon_fit + 2*Lambda(W)*C_E.
AI.result: LAWS generalizes both MoE and KV prefix caching, achieves O(2^H log N) expert library growth, Omega(K) fleet learning speedup, and provably monotone coverage. Self-certification works without ground truth.
AI.conclusion: LAWS provides a principled, certifiable caching framework with applications in LLM inference, robotics, and multi-agent edge deployment, with conjectured optimality among stationary online caching algorithms.

title: From Parameter Dynamics to Risk Scoring : Quantifying Sample-Level Safety Degradation in LLM Fine-tuning
summary: Safety alignment of Large Language Models (LLMs) is extremely fragile, as fine-tuning on a small number of benign samples can erase safety behaviors learned from millions of preference examples. Existing studies attempt to explain this phenomenon by comparing parameters and hidden states before and after fine-tuning, but overlook their dynamic evolution during fine-tuning. In this paper, we uncover a critical mechanism underlying safety degradation by analyzing parameter dynamics, where benign fine-tuning causes parameters to cumulatively drift toward danger-aligned directions, progressively undermining the model's safety. This finding suggests that samples contributing more to this drift has greater fine-tuning risks. Based on this insight, we propose a method of Sample-Level Quantification of Safety Degradation (SQSD), which quantifies the influence of each training sample on safety degradation. Specifically, SQSD computes continuous risk scores to samples by measuring their induced parameter updates' projection difference between danger and safety directions. Extensive experiments across multiple models and datasets demonstrate that SQSD effectively quantifies sample-level fine-tuning risks and exhibits strong transferability across model architectures, parameter scales, and parameter-efficient methods.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that benign fine-tuning causes LLM parameters to drift toward danger-aligned directions, progressively degrading safety. The authors propose SQSD, a method that quantifies sample-level risk by measuring parameter update projection differences between danger and safety directions.
AI.motivation: Safety alignment of LLMs is fragile—fine-tuning on benign samples can erase safety behaviors. Existing studies overlook the dynamic evolution of parameters during fine-tuning, only comparing before/after states.
AI.method: SQSD (Sample-Level Quantification of Safety Degradation): computes continuous risk scores by measuring the projection difference of parameter updates between danger and safety directions for each training sample.
AI.result: SQSD effectively quantifies sample-level fine-tuning risks and exhibits strong transferability across model architectures, parameter scales, and parameter-efficient methods.
AI.conclusion: Benign fine-tuning causes cumulative parameter drift toward danger directions. Samples contributing more to this drift pose greater risks, and SQSD can identify them.

title: Deterministic identification for Bernoulli channels and related channels with continuous input
summary: For memoryless channels with continuous input alphabets, deterministic identification (DI) typically exhibits a linearithmic ($n\log n$) message growth. However, the exact DI capacity has long remained open due to a persistent gap between the best known achievability and converse bounds. This gap was recently closed for AWGN channels via a novel code construction optimising the "galaxy" codes. Here, we extend this approach to the Bernoulli channel and subsequently to any channel $W$ whose image contains a continuous curve of output probability distributions, and hence admits a reduction to the Bernoulli channel restricted to a subinterval of inputs. As a consequence, we prove that the converse bound is tight and establish $\dot{C}_{\text{DI}}(W) = \frac 12$ for this broad class of channels, thereby closing the long-standing capacity gap. A similar gap was also observed for the DI rate-reliability tradeoff. We analyse the tradeoff between rate and error of the proposed code and derive improved lower bounds on the reliability function, approaching the converse at leading order in the regime of small error exponents.

AI.tldr: This paper closes the long-standing capacity gap for deterministic identification (DI) over memoryless channels with continuous input alphabets, proving $\dot{C}_{\text{DI}}(W) = \frac12$ for a broad class of channels including Bernoulli and any channel whose output distributions contain a continuous curve, and provides improved rate-reliability tradeoff bounds.
AI.motivation: The exact deterministic identification (DI) capacity for memoryless channels with continuous input alphabets has remained open due to a persistent gap between achievability and converse bounds. The paper aims to close this gap.
AI.method: The paper extends a novel code construction (optimizing "galaxy" codes) from AWGN channels to the Bernoulli channel, and then to any channel $W$ whose image contains a continuous curve of output probability distributions, enabling a reduction to the Bernoulli channel restricted to a subinterval of inputs.
AI.result: Proves that $\dot{C}_{\text{DI}}(W) = \frac12$ for a broad class of channels, closing the long-standing capacity gap. Also derives improved lower bounds on the reliability function, approaching the converse at leading order for small error exponents.
AI.conclusion: The converse bound is tight for this broad class of channels, and the rate-reliability tradeoff is improved, approaching the converse in the small error exponent regime.

title: Spacing-Based Coupling Radiation Control in Pinching-Antennas Systems for Heterogeneous NOMA Users
summary: Pinching-antennas systems (PASS) offer reconfigurable wireless channels via low-cost dielectric mediums by creating line-of-sight (LoS) communication links. Most of the existing PASS cover mechanisms of equal power pinching antennas for conventional bit-based communication, whereas flexible radiation control remains largely unexplored, particularly for heterogeneous semantic and bit users. In this paper, we investigate the performance of semantic communication (SC) using an adjustable radiation model over PASS, where the coupling strength between the dielectric waveguide and each pinching antenna is determined by the antenna-waveguide spacing. Specifically, the non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA)-assisted heterogeneous users are served by multiple pinching antennas using spacing-controlled adjustable radiation ratios. Uunder this setting, we maximize the semantic spectral efficiency (SE) subject to the bit-user quality of service (QoS) requirement, successive interference cancellation (SIC) feasibility, and the minimum adjacent antennas spacing constraint. An alternating optimization (AO) approach optimizes users power allocation and positions of pinching antennas. Simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proportional power PASS model in providing higher semantic SE in different geometrical and numerical settings compared to conventional benchmark schemes.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates semantic communication over pinching-antenna systems (PASS) with adjustable radiation, using NOMA to serve heterogeneous semantic and bit users. An alternating optimization method maximizes semantic spectral efficiency by jointly optimizing power allocation and antenna positions.
AI.motivation: Existing PASS mechanisms focus on equal-power pinching antennas for conventional bit-based communication, while flexible radiation control for heterogeneous semantic and bit users remains largely unexplored.
AI.method: The paper proposes a NOMA-assisted heterogeneous user scheme where multiple pinching antennas use spacing-controlled adjustable radiation ratios. An alternating optimization (AO) approach jointly optimizes user power allocation and pinching antenna positions to maximize semantic SE under QoS, SIC, and spacing constraints.
AI.result: Simulations show the proportional power PASS model achieves higher semantic spectral efficiency across different geometrical and numerical settings compared to conventional benchmark schemes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adjustable radiation PASS framework with NOMA effectively serves heterogeneous semantic and bit users, outperforming conventional schemes in semantic SE.

title: Structured 3D Latents Are Surprisingly Powerful: Unleashing Generalizable Style with 2D Diffusion
summary: 3D asset generation plays a pivotal role in fields such as gaming and virtual reality, enabling the rapid synthesis of high-fidelity 3D objects from a single or multiple images. Building on this capability, enabling style-controllable generation naturally emerges as an important and desirable direction. However, existing approaches typically rely on style images that lie within or are similar to the training distribution of 3D generation models. When presented with out-of-distribution (OOD) styles, their performance degrades significantly or even fails. To address this limitation, we introduce $\textbf{DiLAST}$: 2D Diffusion-based Latent Awakening for 3D Style Transfer. Specifically, we leverage a pretrained 2D diffusion model as a teacher to provide rich and generalizable style priors. By aligning rendered views with the target style under diffusion-based guidance, our method optimizes the structured 3D latent representation for stylization. We observe that this limitation stems not from insufficient model capacity, but from the underutilization of structured 3D latents, which are inherently expressive. Despite being trained on comparatively limited data, 3D generation models can leverage 2D diffusion guidance to steer denoising toward specific directions in latent space, thereby producing diverse, OOD styles. Extensive experiments across diverse data and multiple 3D generation backbones demonstrate the effectiveness and plug-and-play nature of our approach.

AI.tldr: DiLAST introduces a 2D diffusion-based method to enable style-controllable 3D generation, particularly for out-of-distribution styles, by leveraging pretrained 2D diffusion models as teachers to guide 3D latent representations.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D style transfer methods fail when presented with out-of-distribution (OOD) styles because they rely on style images similar to the training distribution of 3D generation models. The authors aim to address this limitation.
AI.method: DiLAST uses a pretrained 2D diffusion model as a teacher to provide generalizable style priors. It aligns rendered views with the target style via diffusion-based guidance, optimizing structured 3D latent representations. The method "awakens" underutilized structured 3D latents by steering denoising toward specific directions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse data and multiple 3D generation backbones demonstrate effectiveness and plug-and-play nature for producing diverse OOD styles.
AI.conclusion: 3D generation models have sufficient capacity for OOD stylization but underutilize their structured latents. Leveraging 2D diffusion guidance can unlock this capability, enabling robust style-controllable 3D generation.

title: Dr-PoGO: Direct Radar Pose-Graph Optimization
comment: Accepted for presentation at ICRA 2026 Cite as @inproceedings{legentil2026drpogo, title={Dr-PoGO: Direct Radar Pose-Graph Optimization}, author={{Le Gentil}, Cedric and Weican, Li and Brizi, Leonardo and Barfoot, Timothy D.}, booktitle={IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)}, year={2026} }
summary: This paper introduces Dr-PoGO, a method for Simultaneous Localization And Mapping (SLAM) using a 2D spinning radar. Unlike cameras or lidars that require line-of-sight, millimetre-wave radars can `see' through dust, falling snow, rain, etc. Accordingly, it is a great modality for robust perception regardless of the weather conditions. While most existing radar-based SLAM methods rely on the extraction of point clouds or features to perform ego-motion estimation, Dr-PoGO leverages direct registration techniques for odometry (DRO) and loop-closure registration. An off-the-shelf radar-focused place recognition algorithm, RaPlace, provides loop-closure candidates. As RaPlace does not provide relative transformations, Dr-PoGO introduces a coarse-to-fine registration that uses visual features and descriptors to obtain an initial guess for the direct transformation refinement. The global trajectory is optimized in a pose-graph optimization. Dr-PoGO demonstrates state-of-the-art performance over 300km of data in various real-world automotive environments. Our implementation is publicly available: https://github.com/utiasASRL/dr_pogo.

AI.tldr: Dr-PoGO is a radar-based SLAM method using direct registration for odometry and loop closure, achieving state-of-the-art results across 300km of real-world driving data.
AI.motivation: Millimeter-wave radars can operate in adverse weather (dust, snow, rain) where cameras and lidars fail, but most existing radar SLAM relies on point cloud or feature extraction rather than direct registration.
AI.method: Dr-PoGO uses Direct Registration Odometry (DRO) for ego-motion, RaPlace for loop-closure candidate detection, and a coarse-to-fine registration (visual features + direct refinement) to obtain relative transformations, optimized via pose-graph optimization.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance demonstrated over 300km of data in diverse real-world automotive environments.
AI.conclusion: Direct registration techniques are effective for radar SLAM, outperforming existing methods, and the implementation is open-source.

title: FlatASCEND: Autoregressive Clinical Sequence Generation with Continuous Time Prediction and Association-Based Pharmacological Testing
summary: Autoregressive models can predict clinical events, but generating patient-conditioned multi-step trajectories that respond to intervention tokens and testing whether those responses preserve known pharmacological associations has received limited attention. We present FlatASCEND, a 14.5M-parameter autoregressive clinical sequence model using flat composite tokens and a zero-inflated log-normal time head. Standard distributional metrics (Jaccard 0.889-0.954) do not distinguish FlatASCEND from trivial baselines; the model's value lies in conditional generation from patient-specific prefixes. A prompt-shuffle ablation shows patient-specific conditioning amplifies mechanistic pharmacological effects (2.0-2.2x for steroid to glucose, diuretic to potassium) while leaving confounding-driven associations unchanged (0.9x for insulin to glucose). An incident-user framework assesses directional consistency against prior pharmacological knowledge on MIMIC-IV (N=500 per comparison): 4/10 recover correct mechanistic directions, 2 reproduce treatment-context associations, 4 are incorrect (9/10 significant, Wilcoxon p<0.05). This pattern - partial recovery under residual confounding - is consistent with learned observational associations without causal distinction. Direct preference optimisation with surrogate reward destroys all correct associations (3/3 to 0/3), illustrating reward exploitation when reward and evaluation share an outcome domain. Generative evidence is strongest for short-horizon ICU data; outpatient temporal fidelity is weaker (median 10 vs 154 days on INSPECT), and zero-shot cross-site transfer degrades without adaptation.

AI.tldr: FlatASCEND is a 14.5M-parameter autoregressive model for clinical event prediction that, despite strong distributional metrics, primarily excels at patient-conditioned multi-step generation; it partially recovers known pharmacological associations but fails under direct preference optimization, and shows degradation in outpatient and cross-site settings.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive models can predict clinical events, but generating patient-conditioned multi-step trajectories that respond to intervention tokens and testing whether those responses preserve known pharmacological associations has received limited attention.
AI.method: FlatASCEND uses flat composite tokens and a zero-inflated log-normal time head for autoregressive clinical sequence modeling. It employs prompt-shuffle ablation and an incident-user framework with MIMIC-IV (N=500 per comparison) to evaluate pharmacological directional consistency.
AI.result: Standard metrics (Jaccard 0.889-0.954) don't distinguish the model from trivial baselines. Patient-specific conditioning amplifies mechanistic pharmacological effects (2.0-2.2x) but not confounding-driven associations (0.9x). 4/10 correct mechanistic directions recovered, 2 reproduce treatment-context associations, 4 incorrect. Direct preference optimization destroys all correct associations (3/3 to 0/3).
AI.conclusion: The model shows partial recovery of pharmacological knowledge under residual confounding, consistent with learned observational associations without causal distinction. Generative evidence is strongest for short-horizon ICU data; outpatient and cross-site transfer performance degrade significantly.

title: SensingAgents: A Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework for Robust IMU Activity Recognition
summary: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) using Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) sensors is a cornerstone of mobile health, smart environments, and human-computer interaction. However, current deep learning-based HAR models often struggle with heavy reliance on labeled data, position-specific ambiguity, and a lack of transparent reasoning. Inspired by the advanced agents framework, which emulates a collaborative agent using Large Language Models (LLMs), we propose SensingAgents, a novel multi-agent system for robust IMU activity recognition. SensingAgents organizes LLM-powered agents into specialized roles: a group of Analyst Agents for position-specific sensor analysis (arm, wrist, belt, pocket), a pair of Advocate Agents that resolves sensor conflicts through dynamic and static dialectical debates, and a Decision Agent that ensures reliability under sensor drift or failure. Evaluation on the Shoaib dataset demonstrates that SensingAgents significantly outperforms state-of-the-art single-agent and multi-agent LLM models, achieving an accuracy of 79.5% in a zero setting--29% higher than existing agent models and 9.4% higher than deep learning baselines--particularly in complex scenarios where multi-sensor data is conflicting or noisy. Our work highlights the potential of multi-agent collaborative reasoning for advancing the robustness and interpretability of ubiquitous sensing systems.

AI.tldr: SensingAgents is a multi-agent LLM system for IMU-based human activity recognition that uses specialized Analyst, Advocate (debate-based), and Decision agents, achieving 79.5% zero-shot accuracy—29% higher than existing agent models and 9.4% higher than deep learning baselines.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning HAR models struggle with heavy reliance on labeled data, position-specific sensor ambiguity, and lack of transparent reasoning, motivating the need for a more robust and interpretable approach.
AI.method: SensingAgents organizes LLM-powered agents into three roles: (1) Analyst Agents for position-specific sensor analysis, (2) Advocate Agents that resolve sensor conflicts through dynamic and static dialectical debates, and (3) a Decision Agent ensuring reliability under sensor drift or failure.
AI.result: On the Shoaib dataset, SensingAgents achieves 79.5% zero-shot accuracy—29% higher than existing agent models and 9.4% higher than deep learning baselines—especially effective in noisy or conflicting multi-sensor scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent collaborative reasoning with LLMs can significantly advance the robustness and interpretability of ubiquitous sensing systems like HAR.

title: Private Contiguous-Block Retrieval
summary: We introduce the \emph{Private Contiguous-Block Retrieval (PCBR)} problem, where a user retrieves a block of $D$ messages with contiguous indices from $K$ replicated messages stored across $N$ non-colluding servers, while hiding the identity of the requested block from each server. This problem is motivated by storage and streaming systems where files are split into ordered segments. Unlike multi-message Private Information Retrieval (MPIR), where any $D$-subset may be requested, PCBR restricts the demand family to contiguous blocks. This relaxation raises a natural question: Can this structure be exploited to improve retrieval efficiency? We answer this question for balanced $\{0,1\}$-linear schemes. We establish an upper bound on the achievable retrieval rate for all problem parameters, derive a lower bound on the subpacketization level required by any scheme achieving the rate upper bound, and construct a rate-optimal scheme whose subpacketization level matches the lower bound for a broad range of problem parameters. Although the optimal PCBR rate coincides with the best-known MPIR rate converse bound, existing MPIR schemes can be suboptimal for PCBR and can require a much larger subpacketization level. In contrast, our scheme exploits the contiguous-block structure to achieve the optimal rate with reduced subpacketization.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Private Contiguous-Block Retrieval (PCBR), a variant of Private Information Retrieval where the user requests a contiguous block of D messages, and shows that this structure can be exploited to achieve optimal retrieval rates with lower subpacketization compared to general MPIR schemes.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the contiguous-block structure in private retrieval (where a user requests an ordered block of messages, as is common in storage/streaming systems) can improve retrieval efficiency over general multi-message PIR.
AI.method: The authors analyze balanced {0,1}-linear schemes for PCBR, establishing theoretical upper bounds on retrieval rate, lower bounds on subpacketization, and constructing a rate-optimal scheme that leverages the contiguous-block structure.
