summary: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models primarily focus on mapping 2D observations to actions, but exhibit notable limitations in spatiotemporal perception and reasoning: 1) spatial representations often rely on additional sensors, introducing substantial computational overhead; 2) visual reasoning is typically limited to future-frame prediction, lacking alignment with the instruction-grounded scene and thus compromising spatiotemporal consistency. To address these challenges, we propose ConsisVLA-4D, a unified and efficient framework that enhances spatiotemporal consistency in 3D perception and 4D reasoning. Specifically, we design: 1) CV-Aligner, which ensures cross-view object semantic consistency by filtering instruction-relevant regions and aligning object identities across multiple viewpoints; 2) CO-Fuser, which guarantees cross-object spatial geometric consistency by eliminating spatial relation ambiguities between objects across views using compact latent representations. Building upon these, we introduce 3) CS-Thinker to achieve cross-scene spatiotemporal consistency as actions unfold. It learns implicit knowledge of local dynamics from object-semantic tokens of CV-Aligner and global depth from geometric tokens of CO-Fuser, thereby enhancing efficient visual reasoning under scene variations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, benefiting from its efficient spatiotemporal consistency design, ConsisVLA-4D achieves 21.6% and 41.5% performance improvements, along with 2.3-fold and 2.4-fold inference speedups compared to OpenVLA on the LIBERO benchmark and real-world platforms, respectively.ConsisVLA-4D is open-sourced and publicly available at

AI.tldr: ConsisVLA-4D is a unified VLA framework that enhances spatiotemporal consistency in 3D perception and 4D reasoning through three components (CV-Aligner, CO-Fuser, CS-Thinker), achieving ~21-41% performance gains and ~2.3-2.4x speedups over OpenVLA on benchmarks and real-world platforms.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models lack spatiotemporal perception and reasoning: they rely on extra sensors for spatial understanding (high overhead) and only do future-frame prediction without instruction-grounded scene alignment, hurting spatiotemporal consistency.
AI.method: ConsisVLA-4D has three modules: (1) CV-Aligner for cross-view object semantic consistency via instruction-relevant region filtering and identity alignment; (2) CO-Fuser for cross-object spatial geometric consistency via compact latent representations; (3) CS-Thinker for cross-scene spatiotemporal consistency by learning local dynamics from CV-Aligner tokens and global depth from CO-Fuser tokens.
AI.result: 21.6% and 41.5% performance improvements, with 2.3x and 2.4x inference speedups over OpenVLA on LIBERO benchmark and real-world platforms respectively.
AI.conclusion: ConsisVLA-4D effectively improves spatiotemporal consistency in VLA models without heavy extra sensors, achieving both higher performance and faster inference, and is open-sourced.

title: Balanced Aggregation: Understanding and Fixing Aggregation Bias in GRPO
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a central paradigm for improving reasoning and code generation in large language models, and GRPO-style training is widely adopted for its simplicity and effectiveness. However, an important design choice remains underexplored: how token-level policy gradient terms are aggregated within each sampled group. Standard GRPO uses sequence aggregation, while recent work has advocated token aggregation as a better alternative. We show that these two rules induce different optimization biases: token aggregation introduces sign-length coupling, while sequence aggregation implicitly downweights longer responses through sequence-level equal weighting. To address this tension, we propose \textbf{Balanced Aggregation (BA)}, a simple drop-in replacement that computes token-level means separately within the positive and negative subsets and then combines them with sequence-count-based weights. Experiments with Qwen2.5-Math-7B and Qwen3-1.7B on DAPO-17k and Polaris, evaluated on six reasoning and coding benchmarks, show that BA consistently improves training stability and final performance over standard token and sequence aggregation. Our analysis further shows that the relative effectiveness of token and sequence aggregation is largely governed by response-length variation and the positive-negative length gap, highlighting aggregation as a critical design dimension in GRPO-style RLVR.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies that token-level vs. sequence-level aggregation in GRPO-style RLVR induces different optimization biases, and proposes Balanced Aggregation (BA)—a simple method that computes token-level means separately within positive and negative subsets and combines them with sequence-count weights—which consistently improves training stability and final performance on reasoning and coding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The design choice of how token-level policy gradient terms are aggregated within sampled groups in GRPO-style RLVR remains underexplored, yet standard sequence aggregation and recent token aggregation induce different optimization biases (sign-length coupling vs. implicit downweighting of longer responses).
AI.method: Proposes Balanced Aggregation (BA): a drop-in replacement that computes token-level means separately within positive and negative subsets, then combines them with sequence-count-based weights, balancing the biases of token and sequence aggregation.
AI.result: Experiments on Qwen2.5-Math-7B and Qwen3-1.7B across DAPO-17k and Polaris, evaluated on six reasoning/coding benchmarks, show BA consistently improves training stability and final performance over both standard token and sequence aggregation.
AI.conclusion: Aggregation is a critical design dimension in GRPO-style RLVR; the relative effectiveness of token vs. sequence aggregation is governed by response-length variation and positive-negative length gap, and BA effectively resolves this tension.

title: Strat-Reasoner: Reinforcing Strategic Reasoning of LLMs in Multi-Agent Games
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in certain reasoning tasks, they struggle in multi-agent games where the final outcome depends on the joint strategies of all agents. In multi-agent games, the non-stationarity of other agents brings significant challenges on the evaluation of the reasoning process and the credit assignment over multiple reasoning steps. Existing single-agent reinforcement learning (RL) approaches and their multi-agent extensions fail to address these challenges as they do not incorporate other agents in the reasoning process. In this work, we propose Strat-Reasoner, a novel RL-based framework that improves LLMs' strategic reasoning ability in multi-agent games. We introduce a novel recursive reasoning paradigm where an agent's reasoning also integrates other agents' reasoning processes. To provide effective reward signals for the intermediate reasoning sequences, we employ a centralized Chain-of-Thought (CoT) comparison module to evaluate the reasoning quality. Finally, we compute an accurate hybrid advantage and develop a group-relative RL approach to optimize the LLM policy. Experimental results show that Strat-Reasoner substantially improves strategic abilities of underlying LLMs, achieving 22.1\% average performance improvements across various multi-agent games.

AI.tldr: Strat-Reasoner is an RL-based framework that enhances LLMs' strategic reasoning in multi-agent games by incorporating recursive reasoning over other agents' thought processes, using a centralized CoT comparison module for intermediate reward signals, and a group-relative RL optimization approach, achieving 22.1% average improvement across multiple games.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle in multi-agent games due to non-stationarity from other agents, making reasoning evaluation and credit assignment difficult. Existing single-agent and multi-agent RL methods fail because they do not incorporate other agents into the reasoning process.
AI.method: 1) A recursive reasoning paradigm where each agent's reasoning integrates other agents' reasoning processes. 2) A centralized Chain-of-Thought (CoT) comparison module to evaluate the quality of intermediate reasoning sequences. 3) A hybrid advantage computation and group-relative RL approach for policy optimization.
AI.result: Strat-Reasoner substantially improves strategic reasoning in LLMs, achieving a 22.1% average performance improvement across various multi-agent games.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating recursive reasoning with other agents' thought processes and using centralized CoT evaluation with group-relative RL effectively enhances LLMs' strategic reasoning in multi-agent settings.

title: Fast Full-Wave Simulation of Indoor RSS Maps for Pre-Measurement Validation in Device-Free Localization
summary: Human localization is gaining momentum in security, healthcare, logistics, and smart spaces applications. While global navigation systems are unreliable indoor, device-free (a.k.a. passive) localization methods that exploit human-induced perturbations of radio propagation can be effectively used. This paper investigates the use of a compact full-wave electromagnetic (EM) setup as a fast and reliable tool to simulate indoor Wi-Fi propagation for human sensing. The goal is to provide a practical baseline for validating simplified propagation models, such as diffraction-based descriptions, and to reduce the need for costly measurement campaigns. Two-dimensional attenuation maps from received signal strength are generated and compared in controlled environments, focusing on attenuation statistics and interference patterns. The simulations reproduce the main spatial features, though discrepancies remain due to simplified material characterization. Diffraction-aware refinements are proposed to mitigate these effects. Overall, the approach provides an efficient pre-measurement reference to support device-free system design and to guide experimental planning.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using a compact full-wave EM simulation as a fast, reliable tool to model indoor Wi-Fi propagation for device-free human localization, providing a baseline that reduces the need for costly measurement campaigns and supports system design.
AI.motivation: To reduce reliance on expensive and time-consuming measurement campaigns by providing a practical, simulation-based baseline for validating simplified propagation models in device-free human localization systems.
AI.method: A compact full-wave electromagnetic (EM) simulation setup is used to generate 2D attenuation maps from received signal strength in controlled indoor environments, with diffraction-aware refinements proposed to address discrepancies from simplified material characterization.
AI.result: The simulations successfully reproduce main spatial features of human-induced perturbations, though some discrepancies remain due to simplified material modeling; diffraction-aware refinements help mitigate these effects.
AI.conclusion: The compact EM simulation approach serves as an efficient pre-measurement reference for device-free localization system design and experimental planning, reducing dependency on costly field measurements.

title: StableI2I: Spotting Unintended Changes in Image-to-Image Transition
summary: In most real-world image-to-image (I2I) scenarios, existing evaluations primarily focus on instruction following and the perceptual quality or aesthetics of the generated images. However, they largely fail to assess whether the output image preserves the semantic correspondence and spatial structure of the input image. To address this limitation, we propose StableI2I, a unified and dynamic evaluation framework that explicitly measures content fidelity and pre--post consistency across a wide range of I2I tasks without requiring reference images, including image editing and image restoration. In addition, we construct StableI2I-Bench, a benchmark designed to systematically evaluate the accuracy of MLLMs on such fidelity and consistency assessment tasks. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that StableI2I provides accurate, fine-grained, and interpretable evaluations of content fidelity and consistency, with strong correlations to human subjective judgments. Our framework serves as a practical and reliable evaluation tool for diagnosing content consistency and benchmarking model performance in real-world I2I systems.

AI.tldr: StableI2I is a unified evaluation framework for image-to-image tasks that measures content fidelity and pre-post consistency without reference images, plus StableI2I-Bench for benchmarking MLLMs on these tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing I2I evaluations focus on instruction following and perceptual quality but fail to assess semantic correspondence and spatial structure preservation between input and output images.
AI.method: Proposes StableI2I, a reference-free evaluation framework for content fidelity and consistency, and StableI2I-Bench for assessing MLLM accuracy on these tasks.
AI.result: StableI2I provides accurate, fine-grained, interpretable evaluations with strong correlations to human subjective judgments.
AI.conclusion: The framework is a practical and reliable tool for diagnosing content consistency and benchmarking I2I model performance.

title: When Life Gives You BC, Make Q-functions: Extracting Q-values from Behavior Cloning for On-Robot Reinforcement Learning
summary: Behavior Cloning (BC) has emerged as a highly effective paradigm for robot learning. However, BC lacks a self-guided mechanism for online improvement after demonstrations have been collected. Existing offline-to-online learning methods often cause policies to replace previously learned good actions due to a distribution mismatch between offline data and online learning. In this work, we propose Q2RL, Q-Estimation and Q-Gating from BC for Reinforcement Learning, an algorithm for efficient offline-to-online learning. Our method consists of two parts: (1) Q-Estimation extracts a Q-function from a BC policy using a few interaction steps with the environment, followed by online RL with (2) Q-Gating, which switches between BC and RL policy actions based on their respective Q-values to collect samples for RL policy training. Across manipulation tasks from D4RL and robomimic benchmarks, Q2RL outperforms SOTA offline-to-online learning baselines on success rate and time to convergence. Q2RL is efficient enough to be applied in an on-robot RL setting, learning robust policies for contact-rich and high precision manipulation tasks such as pipe assembly and kitting, in 1-2 hours of online interaction, achieving success rates of up to 100% and up to 3.75x improvement against the original BC policy. Code and video are available at https://pages.rai-inst.com/q2rl_website/

AI.tldr: Q2RL enables efficient offline-to-online robot learning by extracting Q-functions from BC policies and using Q-gating to switch between BC and RL actions, achieving up to 100% success rates with 1-2 hours of online interaction.
AI.motivation: BC lacks self-guided online improvement after demonstrations; offline-to-online methods suffer from distribution mismatch causing policies to replace good actions.
AI.method: Q2RL has two components: (1) Q-Estimation extracts a Q-function from a BC policy with minimal environment interaction, (2) Q-Gating switches between BC and RL policy actions based on Q-values to collect samples for RL training.
AI.result: Outperforms SOTA baselines on D4RL and robomimic benchmarks; achieves 100% success rates and up to 3.75x improvement over BC in pipe assembly and kitting tasks with 1-2 hours of online interaction.
AI.conclusion: Q2RL provides an efficient and effective solution for offline-to-online RL in robotics, particularly for contact-rich and high-precision manipulation tasks.

title: Validity-Calibrated Reasoning Distillation
summary: Reasoning distillation aims to transfer multi-step reasoning capabilities from large language models to smaller, more efficient ones. While recent methods have shown promising gains, they typically rely on static teacher-student hierarchies and frame distillation as trajectory imitation. This is misaligned with the structure of reasoning, where intermediate steps are often locally under-specified: global correctness constrains the final answer, but does not uniquely determine each intermediate move. We propose validity-calibrated reasoning distillation, a framework that treats reasoning distillation as a problem of local learning-signal allocation rather than path alignment. Instead of enforcing token-level imitation, we compare the student's and teacher's proposed next-step actions under the same prefix and use their relative local validity to modulate the strength of the distillation update. This yields a dynamic, context-dependent supervision mechanism that preserves the teacher's structural guidance while adapting update strength to local reasoning quality. Across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and instruction-following benchmarks, our method consistently outperforms strong distillation baselines. These results indicate that effective LLM reasoning distillation is governed not by rigid trajectory imitation, but by principled, locally calibrated allocation of learning signal.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes validity-calibrated reasoning distillation, a framework that replaces rigid trajectory imitation in LLM distillation with dynamic, locally-calibrated supervision based on comparing student and teacher next-step actions.
AI.motivation: Existing reasoning distillation methods rely on static teacher-student hierarchies and treat distillation as trajectory imitation, which misaligns with the under-specified nature of intermediate reasoning steps where global correctness doesn't uniquely determine each local move.
AI.method: The method compares the student's and teacher's proposed next-step actions under the same prefix, using their relative local validity to modulate distillation update strength, creating a dynamic, context-dependent supervision mechanism.
AI.result: The method consistently outperforms strong distillation baselines across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and instruction-following benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Effective LLM reasoning distillation is governed not by rigid trajectory imitation but by principled, locally calibrated allocation of learning signal.

title: Curated AI beats frontier LLMs at pharma asset discovery
summary: General-purpose LLMs with web search are increasingly used to scout the competitive landscape of pharmaceutical pipelines. We benchmark Gosset -- an AI platform with a chat interface backed by curated target-, modality-, and indication-level drug-asset annotations -- against four frontier systems with web access (Claude Opus 4.7, GPT 5.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Perplexity sonar-pro) on ten niche oncology/immunology targets where most of the pipeline lives in the long tail of preclinical and Asian-developed assets. All five systems receive the same natural-language query and the same JSON output schema. Across 10 targets Gosset returns 3.2x more verified drugs per query than the best frontier system, at perfect precision and 100% recall against the cross-system union of verified drugs. The same curated index is exposed as a Gosset MCP server that any frontier model can call as a tool, suggesting that each of these systems can close most of the recall gap by swapping generic web search for a curated index behind the same chat interface.

AI.tldr: Gosset, an AI platform with a curated drug-asset index, outperforms four frontier LLMs with web search (Claude Opus 4.7, GPT 5.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Perplexity sonar-pro) on ten niche oncology/immunology targets, returning 3.2x more verified drugs per query at perfect precision and 100% recall.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs with web search are increasingly used for competitive intelligence in pharmaceutical pipelines, but their recall on niche, long-tail targets (preclinical and Asian-developed assets) is limited.
AI.method: Benchmark Gosset (curated index + chat interface) against four frontier systems using the same natural-language queries and JSON output schema on ten niche oncology/immunology targets.
AI.result: Gosset returns 3.2x more verified drugs per query than the best frontier system, with perfect precision and 100% recall against the cross-system union. The curated index is also exposed as an MCP server that any frontier model can call as a tool, closing most of the recall gap.
AI.conclusion: Swapping generic web search for a curated index behind the same chat interface can dramatically improve recall for pharmaceutical pipeline scouting, even when using frontier LLMs.

title: Stream-T1: Test-Time Scaling for Streaming Video Generation
summary: While Test-Time Scaling (TTS) offers a promising direction to enhance video generation without the surging costs of training, current test-time video generation methods based on diffusion models suffer from exorbitant candidate exploration costs and lack temporal guidance. To address these structural bottlenecks, we propose shifting the focus to streaming video generation. We identify that its chunk-level synthesis and few denoising steps are intrinsically suited for TTS, significantly lowering computational overhead while enabling fine-grained temporal control. Driven by this insight, we introduced Stream-T1, a pioneering comprehensive TTS framework exclusively tailored for streaming video generation. Specifically, Stream-T1 is composed of three units: (1) Stream -Scaled Noise Propagation, which actively refines the initial latent noise of the generating chunk using historically proven, high-quality previous chunk noise, effectively establishes temporal dependency and utilizing the historical Gaussian prior to guide the current generation; (2) Stream -Scaled Reward Pruning, which comprehensively evaluates generated candidates to strike an optimal balance between local spatial aesthetics and global temporal coherence by integrating immediate short-term assessments with sliding-window-based long-term evaluations; (3) Stream-Scaled Memory Sinking, which dynamically routes the context evicted from KV-cache into distinct updating pathways guided by the reward feedback, ensuring that previously generated visual information effectively anchors and guides the subsequent video stream. Evaluated on both 5s and 30s comprehensive video benchmarks, Stream-T1 demonstrates profound superiority, significantly improving temporal consistency, motion smoothness, and frame-level visual quality.

AI.tldr: Stream-T1 is a Test-Time Scaling framework for streaming video generation that improves temporal consistency and visual quality through noise propagation, reward pruning, and memory sinking mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Current test-time video generation methods suffer from high candidate exploration costs and lack temporal guidance; the authors aim to address these bottlenecks by shifting to streaming video generation which is naturally suited for TTS due to chunk-level synthesis and few denoising steps.
AI.method: Stream-T1 consists of three units: (1) Stream-Scaled Noise Propagation - refines initial latent noise using historical high-quality chunk noise; (2) Stream-Scaled Reward Pruning - evaluates candidates balancing local aesthetics and global temporal coherence; (3) Stream-Scaled Memory Sinking - routes KV-cache context via reward-guided pathways.
AI.result: Evaluated on 5s and 30s benchmarks, Stream-T1 shows significant improvements in temporal consistency, motion smoothness, and frame-level visual quality over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Streaming video generation is an effective and efficient paradigm for test-time scaling, and Stream-T1 demonstrates that TTS can be practically applied to video generation without prohibitive computational costs.

title: A Closed-Form Dual-Barrier CBF Safety Filter for Holonomic Robots on Incrementally Built Occupancy Grid Maps
summary: We present a dual-barrier control barrier function (CBF) safety filter for real-time, safety-critical velocity control of holonomic robots operating in incrementally built occupancy grid maps. As a robot explores an unknown environment, unmapped regions introduce irreducible uncertainty, since obstacle geometry beyond the explored frontier is unknown, making entry into such regions a source of collision risk, especially with front-facing sensors. To address this, we enforce two constraints: avoidance of mapped obstacles and restriction from unexplored regions. Both constraints are derived analytically from the occupancy grid's signed distance field, yielding a closed-form safety filter that requires only a small linear system solve per cycle. On resource-constrained platforms such as the Raspberry Pi, where SLAM and planning already consume significant compute, the low overhead of the proposed filter preserves resources. An adaptive gain schedule relaxes the frontier constraint in information-rich regions and tightens it in well-mapped areas, improving exploration efficiency while maintaining safety. The filter operates in velocity space as a minimally invasive correction and composes with arbitrary nominal controllers, including learning-based methods. Hardware flight experiments on a PX4-controlled quadrotor demonstrate zero collisions across multiple indoor runs.

AI.tldr: A dual-barrier CBF safety filter for holonomic robots that avoids mapped obstacles and restricts unexplored regions using occupancy grid signed distance fields, with adaptive gain scheduling for exploration efficiency, demonstrated on a quadrotor with zero collisions.
AI.motivation: Unmapped regions in unknown environments introduce irreducible collision risk, especially with front-facing sensors, requiring a safety filter that accounts for both mapped obstacles and unexplored areas.
AI.method: Dual-barrier CBF safety filter with two constraints (mapped obstacle avoidance + unexplored region restriction) derived analytically from occupancy grid signed distance fields, solved via a small linear system, with adaptive gain scheduling.
AI.result: Zero collisions across multiple indoor hardware flight experiments on a PX4-controlled quadrotor; low computational overhead suitable for resource-constrained platforms like Raspberry Pi.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual-barrier CBF filter provides real-time, minimally invasive safety correction for velocity control in unknown environments, composing with arbitrary nominal controllers while maintaining collision-free exploration.

title: Efficient Handwriting-Based Alzheimer,s Disease Diagnosis Using a Low-Rank Mixture of Experts Deep Learning Framework
summary: Early and reliable detection of Alzheimer's disease (AD) is crucial for timely clinical intervention and improved patient management. It also supports the evaluation of emerging therapeutic strategies. In this paper, we propose a Low-Rank Mixture of Experts (LoRA-MoE) deep learning framework for Alzheimer's disease diagnosis based on handwriting analysis. Handwriting signals provide a non-invasive and scalable digital biomarker that captures subtle cognitive-motor impairments associated with early AD progression. The proposed architecture allows multiple experts to specialize in different handwriting patterns while sharing a common base network. This design enables efficient learning of general representations while reducing interference between experts. Each expert is equipped with lightweight low-rank adapters. This mechanism significantly reduces the number of trainable parameters compared with standard Mixture of Experts (MoE) models and improves training stability. The proposed framework is evaluated on the Diagnosis AlzheimeR WIth haNdwriting (DARWIN) dataset. Extensive experiments are conducted, including ablation studies on key architectural parameters such as hidden dimension size, number of experts, and LoRA rank. The method is compared with multilayer perceptron (MLP) and conventional MoE architectures. In addition, stacking ensemble strategies (StackMean and StackMax) are investigated to improve robustness and predictive performance. Experimental results show that the LoRA-MoE framework achieves powerful diagnostic performance while activating significantly fewer parameters during inference. These results highlight the potential of the proposed approach as an accurate and computationally efficient solution for handwriting-based Alzheimer's disease screening and digital health applications.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a LoRA-MoE deep learning framework for Alzheimer's disease diagnosis using handwriting analysis, achieving strong diagnostic performance with fewer activated parameters on the DARWIN dataset.
AI.motivation: Early and reliable detection of Alzheimer's disease is critical for timely intervention. Handwriting analysis offers a non-invasive digital biomarker for capturing cognitive-motor impairments, motivating an efficient deep learning solution.
AI.method: The authors propose a Low-Rank Mixture of Experts (LoRA-MoE) framework where multiple experts specialize in different handwriting patterns using lightweight low-rank adapters, reducing trainable parameters compared to standard MoE. Stacking ensemble strategies (StackMean, StackMax) are also explored.
AI.result: LoRA-MoE achieves powerful diagnostic performance on the DARWIN dataset while activating significantly fewer parameters during inference. Ablation studies on hidden dimension, number of experts, and LoRA rank were conducted.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach is an accurate and computationally efficient solution for handwriting-based Alzheimer's disease screening, highlighting its potential for digital health applications.

title: A Foundation Model for Zero-Shot Logical Rule Induction
comment: Camera-ready version accepted at IJCAI 2026, with full appendices
summary: Inductive Logic Programming (ILP) learns interpretable logical rules from data. Existing methods are transductive: their learned parameters are bound to specific predicates and require retraining for each new task. We introduce Neural Rule Inducer (NRI), a pretrained model for zero-shot rule induction. Rather than encoding literal identities, NRI represents literals using domain-agnostic statistical properties such as class-conditional rates, entropy, and co-occurrence, which generalize across variable identities and counts without retraining. The model consists of a statistical encoder and a parallel slot-based decoder. Parallel decoding preserves the permutation invariance of logical disjunction; an autoregressive decoder would instead impose an arbitrary clause order. Product T-norm relaxation makes rule execution differentiable, allowing end-to-end training on prediction accuracy alone. We evaluate NRI on rule recovery, robustness to label noise and spurious correlations, and zero-shot transfer to real-world benchmarks, and we believe this work opens up the possibility of foundation models for symbolic reasoning. Code and the reference checkpoint are available at https://github.com/phuayj/neural-rule-inducer.

AI.tldr: NRI is a pretrained model for zero-shot rule induction that uses domain-agnostic statistical properties of literals instead of fixed predicate embeddings, enabling generalization across tasks without retraining.
AI.motivation: Existing ILP methods are transductive, binding learned parameters to specific predicates, requiring retraining for each new task. The paper aims to enable zero-shot rule induction that generalizes across variable identities and counts.
AI.method: NRI uses a statistical encoder (domain-agnostic features like class-conditional rates, entropy, co-occurrence) + parallel slot-based decoder (preserving permutation invariance of logical disjunction) + Product T-norm relaxation (enabling differentiable end-to-end training on prediction accuracy).
AI.result: Evaluated on rule recovery, robustness to label noise and spurious correlations, and zero-shot transfer to real-world benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: This work opens up the possibility of foundation models for symbolic reasoning, enabling pretrained models for zero-shot ILP.

title: Information Coordination as a Bridge: A Neuro-Symbolic Architecture for Reliable Autonomous Driving Scene Understanding
summary: Reliable autonomous driving requires scene understanding that is semantically consistent across heterogeneous sensors and verifiable at the reasoning stage. However, many recent LLM-driven driving systems attach the language model as a post-processor and force it to reason over redundant or conflicting perception outputs, which can amplify hallucinated entities and unsafe conclusions. This paper proposes InfoCoordiBridge, a BEV-centric neuro-symbolic architecture that inserts an explicit coordination bridge between perception and language reasoning. InfoCoordiBridge comprises (i) a unified multi-agent perception layer that outputs typed structured facts together with modality-focused synopses, (ii) an ICA module that aligns and fuses multi-source outputs into a single SceneSummary, and (iii) an SSRE module that performs SceneSummary-grounded reasoning with verification. Experiments on nuScenes and Waymo show that ICA preserves competitive 3D detection accuracy while substantially improving fusion consistency, reducing redundancy to below 1% and achieving about 98% attribute agreement. On NuScenes-QA and a template-aligned Waymo-QA benchmark, SSRE improves factual grounding and reduces hallucinated entity mentions compared with representative VLM and agentic baselines. Overall, by coordinating multi-sensor outputs into a single conflict-aware SceneSummary before prompting, InfoCoordiBridge prevents redundant and cross-modally inconsistent perception evidence from propagating into high-level reasoning.

AI.tldr: InfoCoordiBridge is a BEV-centric neuro-symbolic architecture that inserts a coordination bridge between perception and language reasoning. It uses a unified multi-agent perception layer, an ICA module for fusion, and an SSRE module for verified reasoning, achieving high fusion consistency and reduced hallucination on nuScenes and Waymo benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-driven driving systems attach the language model as a post-processor that reasons over redundant or conflicting perception outputs, which can amplify hallucinated entities and lead to unsafe conclusions.
AI.method: InfoCoordiBridge consists of three components: (i) a unified multi-agent perception layer that outputs typed structured facts with modality-focused synopses, (ii) an ICA module that aligns and fuses multi-source outputs into a single SceneSummary, and (iii) an SSRE module that performs SceneSummary-grounded reasoning with verification.
AI.result: ICA preserves competitive 3D detection accuracy while improving fusion consistency, reducing redundancy to below 1% and achieving about 98% attribute agreement. SSRE improves factual grounding and reduces hallucinated entity mentions compared to VLM and agentic baselines on NuScenes-QA and Waymo-QA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: By coordinating multi-sensor outputs into a single conflict-aware SceneSummary before prompting, InfoCoordiBridge prevents redundant and cross-modally inconsistent perception evidence from propagating into high-level reasoning.

title: Constraint-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning Based on Dynamic Decoupled Spherical Radial Squashing
summary: When deploying reinforcement learning policies to physical robots, actuator rate constraints -- hard limits on how fast each joint can move per control step -- are unavoidable. These limits vary substantially across joints due to differences in motor inertia, power bandwidth, and transmission stiffness, creating pronounced heterogeneity that existing methods fail to handle geometrically: the per-joint feasible region forms a high-dimensional box in action-increment space, yet QP projection and spherical parameterization methods impose isotropic ball-shaped constraints, exponentially under-covering the true feasible set as heterogeneity grows. This paper proposes Dynamic Decoupled Spherical Radial Squashing (DD-SRad), which resolves this mismatch by computing a position-adaptive radius independently for each actuator, achieving tight alignment with the true per-joint feasible region. DD-SRad satisfies per-step hard constraints with probability~1, preserves well-conditioned gradients throughout training, and admits exact policy gradient backpropagation with zero runtime solver overhead. MuJoCo benchmark experiments demonstrate the highest task return at zero constraint violation -- matching the unconstrained upper bound -- with 30%--50% improvement in constraint-space coverage over spherical baselines. High-fidelity IsaacLab simulations with Unitree H1 and G1 humanoid robots confirm end-to-end optimality parameterized directly from official joint specifications, validating a systematic pathway from hardware datasheets to safe deployment.

AI.tldr: DD-SRad is a method that handles heterogeneous actuator rate constraints in reinforcement learning by computing position-adaptive radii per joint, achieving tight feasible region coverage and zero constraint violation.
AI.motivation: Existing RL methods for physical robots fail to handle heterogeneous actuator rate constraints because they impose isotropic ball-shaped constraints (e.g., QP projection, spherical parameterization) that exponentially under-cover the true per-joint feasible box-shaped region as heterogeneity increases.
AI.method: DD-SRad computes a position-adaptive radius independently for each actuator, achieving tight alignment with the true per-joint feasible box region, satisfying hard constraints with probability 1, preserving well-conditioned gradients, and enabling exact policy gradient backpropagation with zero runtime solver overhead.
AI.result: MuJoCo benchmarks show highest task return with zero constraint violation (matching unconstrained upper bound) and 30%-50% improvement in constraint-space coverage over spherical baselines. IsaacLab simulations on Unitree H1 and G1 humanoid robots confirm end-to-end optimality from hardware datasheets.
AI.conclusion: DD-SRad provides a systematic pathway from hardware datasheets to safe deployment by geometrically matching heterogeneous actuator constraints, outperforming spherical methods significantly.

title: Time series causal discovery with variable lags
summary: Causal Bayesian Networks (CBNs) are a powerful tool for reasoning under uncertainty about complex real-world problems. Such problems evolve over time, responding to external shocks as they occur. To support decision-making, CBNs require a cause-and-effect map of the variables under consideration, known as the network's structure. Learning the graphical structure of a causal model from data remains challenging; learning it from time-series data is even harder because dependencies may arise at different time lags. Existing time-series causal discovery methods often assume a fixed lag window and do not explicitly optimise edge-specific lags. We propose a Tabu-based structure learning algorithm that searches for a time-ordered directed structure (i.e., where every edge respects time) while allowing edge-specific lags up to a specified maximum lag. The approach uses a decomposable BIC-based score with node-specific effective sample sizes and an explicit lag-length penalty encouraging parsimonious delay assignments while preserving efficient local score updates. We provide theoretical guarantees of validity and local optimality, and we also describe a parallel implementation for improved scalability. In simulations, the method recovered graph structure competitively and estimated lags accurately when true adjacencies were recovered. On a real-world UK COVID-19 policy dataset, the learnt structure was dominated by short delays while retaining a substantial minority of longer-lag dependencies, consistent with delayed behavioural and epidemiological effects.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Tabu-based structure learning algorithm for Causal Bayesian Networks from time-series data that assigns edge-specific time lags, with theoretical guarantees and competitive empirical performance on synthetic and real COVID-19 data.
AI.motivation: Existing time-series causal discovery methods often assume a fixed lag window and do not explicitly optimise edge-specific lags, limiting their ability to capture real-world temporal dependencies where different causal relationships may operate at different time scales.
AI.method: The authors propose a Tabu-based structure learning algorithm that searches for time-ordered directed graphs with edge-specific lags up to a specified maximum. It uses a decomposable BIC-based score with node-specific effective sample sizes and an explicit lag-length penalty, along with a parallel implementation for scalability.
AI.result: In simulations, the method recovered graph structure competitively and estimated lags accurately when true adjacencies were recovered. On a real UK COVID-19 dataset, the learnt structure showed mainly short delays but also a substantial minority of longer-lag dependencies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm effectively discovers time-lagged causal structures with edge-specific delays, offering theoretical guarantees of validity and local optimality, and demonstrates practical utility on real-world policy data.

title: On-line Learning in Tree MDPs by Treating Policies as Bandit Arms
comment: Accepted as a full paper in the Main Track of AAMAS 2026
summary: A Tree Markov Decision Problem (T-MDP) is a finite-horizon MDP with a starting state $s_{1}$, in which every state is reachable from $s_{1}$ through exactly one state-action trajectory. T-MDPs arise naturally as abstractions of decision making in sequential games with perfect recall, against stationary opponents. We consider the problem of on-line learning in T-MDPs, both in the PAC and the regret-minimisation regimes. We show that well-known bandit algorithms -- \textsc{Lucb} and \textsc{Ucb} -- can be applied on T-MDPs by treating each policy as an arm. The apparent technical challenge in this approach is that the number of policies is exponential in the number of states. Our main innovation is in the design of confidence bounds based on data shared by the policies, so that the bandit algorithms can yet be implemented with polynomial memory and per-step computation. We obtain instance-dependent upper bounds on sample complexity and regret that sum a ``gap term'' from every terminal state, rather than every policy. Empirically, our algorithms consistently outperform available alternatives on a suite of hidden-information games.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces efficient bandit algorithms (Lucb and Ucb) for online learning in Tree Markov Decision Problems (T-MDPs), addressing the exponential policy challenge by designing shared confidence bounds that enable polynomial-time implementation, with sample complexity and regret bounds depending on terminal states rather than policies.
AI.motivation: To develop efficient online learning algorithms for T-MDPs that naturally model decision-making in sequential games with perfect recall against stationary opponents, overcoming the challenge that the number of policies grows exponentially with states.
AI.method: Apply bandit algorithms (Lucb and Ucb) by treating each policy as an arm, but design novel confidence bounds based on data shared across policies to enable polynomial memory and per-step computation despite the exponential policy space.
AI.result: Instance-dependent upper bounds on sample complexity and regret that sum a "gap term" from every terminal state rather than every policy; empirical outperformance over alternatives on hidden-information games.
AI.conclusion: Shared confidence bounds effectively make bandit algorithms tractable for T-MDPs, with theoretical guarantees that depend on terminal states rather than policies, and strong empirical performance.

title: Example-Based Object Detection
summary: In recent years, object detection has achieved significant progress, especially in the field of open-vocabulary object detection. Unlike traditional methods that rely on predefined categories, open-vocabulary approaches can detect arbitrary objects based on human-provided prompts. With the advancement of prompt-based detection techniques, models such as SAM3 can even outperform some category-specific detectors trained on particular datasets without requiring additional training on those datasets. However, despite these advancements, false positives and false negatives still occur. In practical engineering applications, persistent misdetections or missed detections of the same object are unacceptable. Yet retraining the model every time such errors occur incurs substantial costs in terms of human effort, computational resources, and time. Therefore, how to leverage existing false positive and false negative samples to prevent such errors from recurring remains a highly challenging and urgent problem. To address this issue, we propose EBOD (Example-Based Object Detection), which integrates a prompt-based detector (SAM3) with robust feature matching modules (DINOv3 and LightGlue). The proposed framework effectively suppresses the repeated occurrence of false positives and false negatives by leveraging previous error examples, without requiring additional model retraining. Code is available at https://github.com/sunzx97/examples_based_object_detection.

AI.tldr: EBOD integrates SAM3, DINOv3, and LightGlue to correct persistent false positives/negatives in open-vocabulary object detection using error examples, without retraining.
AI.motivation: In open-vocabulary object detection, persistent false positives and false negatives are unacceptable in engineering applications, but retraining models for every error is costly. The paper aims to prevent recurring errors without model retraining.
AI.method: EBOD combines a prompt-based detector (SAM3) with robust feature matching modules (DINOv3 and LightGlue). It leverages previous false positive and false negative examples to suppress repeated errors, requiring no additional model retraining.
AI.result: The proposed framework effectively suppresses repeated false positives and false negatives by using past error examples.
AI.conclusion: Example-based error correction without retraining is a viable and efficient approach for improving open-vocabulary object detection reliability in practical applications.

title: Enhancing the interpretability of spatially variable N2O model predictions with soft sensors during wastewater treatment
summary: Model-based solutions for nitrous oxide (N2O) emissions from wastewater treatment plants (WWTP) are informed by operational datasets designed to control nutrient levels in liquid waste, coupled with dedicated campaigns for N2O measurements. We analysed how machine learning (ML) models predict disturbances to WWT operation and spatially variable N2O emissions. A real dataset was investigated to validate the modelling framework from N2O emissions predicted by four ML models (R2 = 0.79 - 0.89). Monitoring campaigns for N2O were simulated with a plant-wide mechanistic model to include additional sensors, site-level N2O datasets, and wastewater disturbances (n = 16). ML models were highly accurate (0.97 +- 0.02, n = 80), but the feature importance depended on the model, the scenario and the N2O measurement scale (reactor vs. WWTP). We argue that N2O soft sensor model predictions are limited to the measuring location and the methodological uncertainty of the dataset, which affect the interpretability of the model. Lastly, the analysis of the mechanistic model structure exposed interactions between autotrophic and heterotrophic pathways over nitric oxide which can overestimate aerobic nitrite production and bias the N2O pathway contributions.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates machine learning models for predicting N2O emissions from wastewater treatment plants, finding high accuracy (R²=0.79-0.89, and 0.97±0.02 for simulated data) but highlighting limitations related to measurement location, dataset uncertainty, and mechanistic model biases in N2O pathway contributions.
AI.motivation: To improve model-based solutions for predicting nitrous oxide (N2O) emissions from wastewater treatment plants, which are currently informed by operational datasets and dedicated N2O measurement campaigns, by analyzing how machine learning models handle disturbances and spatial variability.
AI.method: Four ML models were tested on real WWTP data (R²=0.79-0.89) and on simulated data from a plant-wide mechanistic model with 16 disturbance scenarios and additional sensors; feature importance was analyzed across models, scenarios, and measurement scales.
AI.result: ML models achieved high accuracy (0.97±0.02, n=80) on simulated data, but feature importance varied by model, scenario, and N2O measurement scale; mechanistic model analysis revealed interactions between autotrophic and heterotrophic pathways that can overestimate aerobic nitrite production and bias N2O pathway contributions.
AI.conclusion: N2O soft sensor predictions are limited to the measuring location and dataset methodological uncertainty; mechanistic model structures have inherent biases that affect interpretability of N2O pathway contributions.

title: Uno-Orchestra: Parsimonious Agent Routing via Selective Delegation
summary: Large language model (LLM) multi-agent systems typically rely on rigid orchestration, committing either to flat per-query routing or to hand-engineered task decomposition, so decomposition depth, worker choice, and inference budget are not jointly optimized under one objective. We introduce Uno-Orchestra, a unified orchestration policy that selectively decomposes a task and dispatches each subtask to an admissible (model, primitive) pair, with both decisions learned together from curated RL trajectories grounded in real worker interactions. Against 22 baselines on a 13-benchmark suite spanning math, code, knowledge, long-context, and agentic tool-use, Uno-Orchestra reaches 77.0% macro pass@1, roughly 16% above the strongest workflow baseline, at roughly an order of magnitude lower per-query cost, advancing the accuracy-efficiency frontier of selective delegation.

AI.tldr: Uno-Orchestra is a unified RL-based orchestration policy for LLM multi-agent systems that jointly optimizes task decomposition, worker selection, and inference budget, achieving 77.0% macro pass@1 (16% above baselines) at ~10x lower cost across 13 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM multi-agent systems use rigid orchestration (flat routing or hand-engineered decomposition) that fails to jointly optimize decomposition depth, worker choice, and inference budget under a single objective.
AI.method: Uno-Orchestra learns a unified policy via RL from curated trajectories grounded in real worker interactions, selectively decomposing tasks and dispatching subtasks to admissible (model, primitive) pairs.
AI.result: Against 22 baselines on 13 benchmarks (math, code, knowledge, long-context, agentic tool-use): 77.0% macro pass@1 (~16% above strongest workflow baseline) at ~10x lower per-query cost.
AI.conclusion: Uno-Orchestra advances the accuracy-efficiency frontier of selective delegation in LLM multi-agent systems.

title: Adaptive Learning Strategies for AoA-Based Outdoor Localization: A Comprehensive Framework
summary: Localization in 5G and 6G networks is essential for important use cases such as intelligent transportation, smart factories, and smart cities. Although deep learning has enabled improving localization accuracy, depending on the deployment scenario and the effort required for dataset collection campaigns on a given infrastructure, the training process for localization models can vary significantly. Furthermore, with respect to feature selection, recent works have demonstrated the robustness of angle-of-arrival (AoA) based localization. In view of these two points, we propose an adaptive framework for AoA-based localization that consists of two alternative learning strategies, each suited either for large or small training datasets. The proposed framework is evaluated on a real, massive multiple input multiple output (mMIMO) orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) outdoor channel state information (CSI) dataset. First, we investigate offline learning when large training datasets are available; we propose a hierarchical framework that first distinguishes between line of sight (LoS) and non line of sight (NLoS) regions and then moves to more fine grained localization in the respective region. This approach provides high-performance localization through accumulated batch retraining and an integrated hyperparameter optimization mechanism. Second, when only a small training dataset is available, an online learning framework is proposed, using incremental tree-based and ensemble-based models for handling streaming data and continuously updating mode, as well as an online few-shot learning model for rapidly initializing new classes from a limited labeled support set. These results showcase that highly accurate robust localization can be achieved incrementally during network operation by exploiting online learning, alleviating the need for large dataset collection campaigns.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an adaptive framework for AoA-based localization in 5G/6G networks with two learning strategies: offline hierarchical learning for large datasets and online/incremental learning for small datasets, evaluated on real mMIMO OFDM outdoor CSI data.
AI.motivation: Localization is critical for 5G/6G use cases like intelligent transportation and smart factories. However, training localization models varies significantly depending on deployment scenarios and dataset collection efforts. Additionally, AoA-based localization has shown robustness as a feature.
AI.method: Two alternative learning strategies: (1) Offline hierarchical learning (large datasets) — first distinguishes LoS/NLoS regions, then fine-grained localization, with batch retraining and hyperparameter optimization. (2) Online learning (small datasets) — uses incremental tree-based/ensemble models for streaming data and online few-shot learning for rapidly initializing new classes from limited labeled support sets.
AI.result: Evaluated on a real mMIMO OFDM outdoor CSI dataset. The offline approach provides high-performance localization; the online approach achieves accurate robust localization incrementally during network operation.
AI.conclusion: Highly accurate robust localization can be achieved incrementally via online learning, alleviating the need for large dataset collection campaigns.

title: DiffCap-Bench: A Comprehensive, Challenging, Robust Benchmark for Image Difference Captioning
summary: Image Difference Captioning (IDC) generates natural language descriptions that precisely identify differences between two images, serving as a key benchmark for fine-grained change perception, cross-modal reasoning, and image editing data construction. However, existing benchmarks lack diversity and compositional complexity, and standard lexical-overlap metrics (e.g., BLEU, METEOR) fail to capture semantic consistency or penalize hallucinations, which together prevent a comprehensive and robust evaluation of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on IDC. To address these gaps, we introduce DiffCap-Bench, a comprehensive IDC benchmark covering ten distinct difference categories to ensure diversity and compositional complexity. Furthermore, we propose an LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation protocol grounded in human-validated Difference Lists, enabling a robust assessment of models' ability to both capture and describe visual changes. Through extensive evaluation of state-of-the-art MLLMs, we reveal significant performance gaps between proprietary and open-source models, highlight the critical importance of reasoning capability, and identify clear limitations in model scaling. Our framework also demonstrates strong alignment with human expert judgments and strong correlation with downstream image editing data construction quality. These findings establish DiffCap-Bench as both a reliable IDC evaluation framework and a practical predictor of downstream utility. The benchmark and code will be made publicly available to support further research.

AI.tldr: DiffCap-Bench is a new benchmark for Image Difference Captioning that covers 10 difference categories and uses LLM-as-a-Judge with human-validated Difference Lists for evaluation, revealing performance gaps between proprietary and open-source MLLMs and strong correlation with downstream image editing quality.
AI.motivation: Existing IDC benchmarks lack diversity and compositional complexity, and standard lexical-overlap metrics (BLEU, METEOR) fail to capture semantic consistency or penalize hallucinations, preventing comprehensive evaluation of MLLMs on IDC.
AI.method: The authors introduce DiffCap-Bench covering ten distinct difference categories, and propose an LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation protocol grounded in human-validated Difference Lists for robust assessment.
AI.result: Evaluation reveals significant performance gaps between proprietary and open-source MLLMs, highlights the importance of reasoning capability, identifies limitations in model scaling, and demonstrates strong alignment with human judgments and correlation with downstream image editing quality.
AI.conclusion: DiffCap-Bench serves as both a reliable IDC evaluation framework and a practical predictor of downstream utility; benchmark and code will be made publicly available.

title: CRAFT: Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning for Driving Policies
summary: Open-loop imitation learning has advanced modern autonomous driving policy architectures, but closed-loop deployment remains vulnerable to policy-induced distribution shift. Existing post-training paradigms exhibit fundamental trade-offs: closed-loop RL fine-tuning provides grounded feedback from executed actions but is constrained by the sparsity of informative events, whereas counterfactual fine-tuning provides dense supervision over candidate futures but inherits bias from imperfect future estimates. We introduce Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (CRAFT), an on-policy framework that formulates closed-loop post-training as proxy-residual optimization. CRAFT uses group-normalized counterfactual advantages as a dense proxy for real closed-loop advantages and aligns this proxy with the closed-loop world through grounded residual correction from interaction-critical events. To stabilize adaptation, CRAFT regularizes the online policy toward an EMA teacher via asymmetric KL self-distillation. Theoretically, CRAFT decomposes the real closed-loop policy gradient into proxy and residual terms under the same visited-state distribution, reducing residual variance with an aligned proxy while mitigating proxy bias through grounded residual approximation. Empirically, CRAFT achieves the strongest closed-loop gains on Bench2Drive across hierarchical planning, vision-language-action, and vocabulary-scoring architectures. Ablations, scaling behavior, stability analyses, and transfer results further validate the complementary roles of dense counterfactual proxy and grounded residual correction. Project page: https://currychen77.github.io/CRAFT.

AI.tldr: CRAFT is a closed-loop post-training framework for autonomous driving that combines dense counterfactual advantages (as proxy) with grounded residual correction from interactive events, achieving strong closed-loop gains across multiple architectures on Bench2Drive.
AI.motivation: Existing post-training paradigms (closed-loop RL vs. counterfactual fine-tuning) have fundamental trade-offs: RL suffers from sparse informative events, while counterfactual methods inherit bias from imperfect future estimates. CRAFT aims to combine the strengths of both.
AI.method: CRAFT formulates closed-loop post-training as proxy-residual optimization: (1) group-normalized counterfactual advantages serve as dense proxy for real closed-loop advantages; (2) grounded residual correction from interaction-critical events aligns the proxy with real world; (3) asymmetric KL self-distillation toward an EMA teacher stabilizes online adaptation.
AI.result: CRAFT achieves the strongest closed-loop gains on Bench2Drive across hierarchical planning, vision-language-action, and vocabulary-scoring architectures. Ablations, scaling, stability, and transfer results validate the complementary roles of dense counterfactual proxy and grounded residual correction.
AI.conclusion: CRAFT effectively decomposes policy gradients into proxy and residual terms under the same visited-state distribution, reducing residual variance via aligned proxy while mitigating proxy bias through grounded residual approximation, offering a principled solution to the post-training dilemma.

title: AsymmetryZero: A Framework for Operationalizing Human Expert Preferences as Semantic Evals
summary: Much of the focus in RL today is on evaluation design: building meaningful evals that serve simultaneously as benchmarks and as well-defined reward signals for post-training. Yet, many real-world tasks are governed by subjective, procedural, and domain-specific requirements that are difficult to encode as exact-match targets or open-ended preference judgments frequently used in RL pipelines today. In this work, we present AsymmetryZero, a framework for operationalizing human expert preferences as semantic evals. AsymmetryZero represents each task as a stable evaluation contract that makes grading criteria explicit: what is being graded, how each criterion is judged, and how criterion-level decisions are aggregated into a task outcome. The same contract can be executed using Inspect for model-only evaluations, as well as the Harbor Framework for agentic evaluations, enabling comparable scores and shared audit artifacts across both settings. We argue that the central challenge in post-training today is the faithful encoding of expert requirements into the evaluation itself. To that end, we present a study using Harbor that holds task contracts fixed and compares a five-model frontier jury against a five-model compact jury across four frontier-class solvers (Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5.4, Grok-4.20, Gemini-3.1-Pro). We find that criterion-level frontier-vs-compact agreement ranges from $75.9\%$ to $89.6\%$ (strict common-subset agreement: $77.8\%$ to $92.1\%$), while compact juries exhibit substantially higher internal dissent (3--2 split rate $28.7\%$--$32.4\%$) than frontier juries ($6.1\%$--$11.5\%$). Verifier traces further show that compact juries reduce per-criterion judging cost to roughly $4.2\%$--$5.6\%$ of frontier and latency to roughly $21.7\%$--$27.1\%$, even as aggregated task-level outcomes often remain comparatively stable.

AI.tldr: AsymmetryZero is a framework that operationalizes human expert preferences as semantic evals using "evaluation contracts" that make grading criteria explicit. A study comparing frontier-model juries vs compact-model juries shows compact models achieve 75.9%-89.6% criterion-level agreement with frontiers while costing only 4.2%-5.6% as much and running at 21.7%-27.1% latency, though they exhibit higher internal dissent (28.7%-32.4% vs 6.1%-11.5%).
AI.motivation: Many real-world tasks are governed by subjective, procedural, and domain-specific requirements that are difficult to encode as exact-match targets or open-ended preference judgments, yet most RL evaluation design relies on these limited formats.
AI.method: AsymmetryZero represents tasks as "evaluation contracts" specifying grading criteria, how each is judged, and how decisions are aggregated. Contracts can be executed via Inspect (model-only) or Harbor (agentic). The study compares five-model frontier juries (Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5.4, Grok-4.20, Gemini-3.1-Pro) against five-model compact juries with fixed contracts.
AI.result: Criterion-level frontier-vs-compact agreement: 75.9%-89.6% (strict common-subset: 77.8%-92.1%). Compact juries have higher internal dissent (3-2 split rate 28.7%-32.4%) vs frontiers (6.1%-11.5%). Compact juries reduce judging cost to ~4.2%-5.6% and latency to ~21.7%-27.1% of frontier levels.
AI.conclusion: The central challenge in post-training today is faithfully encoding expert requirements into the evaluation itself. Compact juries can approximate frontier-level evaluations at substantially lower cost, though with higher internal dissent, while aggregated task-level outcomes remain comparatively stable.

title: Position: Embodied AI Requires a Privacy-Utility Trade-off
summary: Embodied AI (EAI) systems are rapidly transitioning from simulations into real-world domestic and other sensitive environments. However, recent EAI solutions have largely demonstrated advancements within isolated stages such as instruction, perception, planning and interaction, without considering their coupled privacy implications in high-frequency deployments where privacy leakage is often irreversible. This position paper argues that optimizing these components independently creates a systemic privacy crisis when deployed in sensitive settings, thereby advancing the position that privacy in EAI is a life cycle-level architectural constraint rather than a stage-local feature. To address these challenges, we propose Secure Privacy Integration in Next-generation Embodied AI (SPINE), a unified privacy-aware framework that treats privacy as a dynamic control signal governing cross-stage coupling throughout the entire EAI life cycle. SPINE decomposes the EAI pipeline into various stages and establishes a multi-criterion privacy classification matrix to orchestrate contextual sensitivity across stage boundaries. We conduct preliminary simulation and real-world case studies to conceptually validate how privacy constraints propagate downstream to reshape system behavior, illustrating the insufficiency of fragmented privacy patches and motivating future research directions into secure yet functional embodied AI systems. We detail the SPINE framework and case studies at https://github.com/rminshen03/EAI_Privacy_Position.

AI.tldr: This position paper argues that privacy in Embodied AI (EAI) must be treated as a life cycle-level architectural constraint rather than isolated stage-local features, proposing the SPINE framework for unified privacy-aware cross-stage integration.
AI.motivation: Current EAI systems optimize instruction, perception, planning, and interaction independently, ignoring coupled privacy implications in sensitive real-world deployments where privacy leakage is irreversible.
AI.method: Proposes SPINE (Secure Privacy Integration in Next-generation Embodied AI), a unified framework that treats privacy as a dynamic control signal governing cross-stage coupling, using a multi-criterion privacy classification matrix to orchestrate contextual sensitivity across the entire EAI life cycle.
AI.result: Preliminary simulation and real-world case studies conceptually validate that privacy constraints propagate downstream to reshape system behavior, demonstrating the insufficiency of fragmented privacy patches.
AI.conclusion: Privacy in EAI must be a life cycle-level architectural constraint, not a stage-local feature; future research should pursue secure yet functional embodied AI systems.

title: SpecPL: Disentangling Spectral Granularity for Prompt Learning
summary: Existing prompt learning for VLMs exhibits a modality asymmetry, predominantly optimizing text tokens while still relying on frozen visual encoder as holistic extractor and neglecting the spectral granularity essential for fine-grained discrimination. To bridge this, we introduce Disentangling Spectral Granularity for Prompt Learning (SpecPL), which approaches prompt learning from a novel spectral perspective via Counterfactual Granule Supervision. Specifically, we leverage a frozen VAE to decompose visual signals into semantic low-frequency bands and granular high-frequency details. A frozen Visual Semantic Bank anchors text representations to universal low-frequency invariants, mitigating overfitting. Crucially, fine-grained discrimination is driven by counterfactual granule training: by permuting high-frequency signals, we compel the model to explicitly distinguish visual granularity from semantic invariance. Uniquely, SpecPL serves as a universal plug-and-play booster, revitalizing text-oriented baselines like CoOp and MaPLe via visual-side guidance. Experiments on 11 benchmarks demonstrate competitive state-of-the-art performance, achieving a new performance ceiling of 81.51\% harmonic-mean accuracy. These results validate that spectral disentanglement with counterfactual supervision effectively bridges the gap in the stability-generalization trade-off. Code is released at https://github.com/Mlrac1e/SpecPL-Prompt-Learning.

AI.tldr: SpecPL introduces spectral disentanglement via counterfactual supervision to bridge modality asymmetry in prompt learning for VLMs, achieving 81.51% harmonic-mean accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing prompt learning suffers from modality asymmetry — it optimizes only text tokens while using a frozen visual encoder that neglects spectral granularity essential for fine-grained discrimination.
AI.method: SpecPL uses a frozen VAE to decompose visual signals into low-frequency (semantic) and high-frequency (granular) bands. A Visual Semantic Bank anchors text to universal low-frequency invariants. Fine-grained discrimination is achieved via counterfactual granule training that permutes high-frequency signals to compel distinguishing visual granularity from semantic invariance.
AI.result: Competitive SOTA on 11 benchmarks with 81.51% harmonic-mean accuracy. Serves as a plug-and-play booster for text-oriented baselines (CoOp, MaPLe).
AI.conclusion: Spectral disentanglement with counterfactual supervision effectively bridges the stability-generalization trade-off gap in prompt learning.

title: Dream-MPC: Gradient-Based Model Predictive Control with Latent Imagination
summary: State-of-the-art model-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches either use gradient-free, population-based methods for planning, learned policy networks, or a combination of policy networks and planning. Hybrid approaches that combine Model Predictive Control (MPC) with a learned model and a policy prior to leverage the advantages of both paradigms have shown promising results. However, these approaches typically rely on gradient-free optimization methods, which can be computationally expensive for high-dimensional control tasks. While gradient-based methods are a promising alternative, recent works have empirically shown that gradient-based methods often perform worse than their gradient-free counterparts. We propose Dream-MPC, a novel approach that generates few candidate trajectories from a rolled-out policy and optimizes each trajectory by gradient ascent using a learned world model, uncertainty regularization and amortization of optimization iterations over time by reusing previously optimized actions. Our results on 24 continuous control tasks show that Dream-MPC can significantly improve the performance of the underlying policy and can outperform gradient-free MPC and state-of-the-art baselines. We will open source our code and more at https://dream-mpc.github.io.

AI.tldr: Dream-MPC enhances model-based RL by using gradient ascent to optimize trajectories from a policy prior, with uncertainty regularization and temporal amortization, outperforming gradient-free MPC on 24 continuous control tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing hybrid MPC approaches combine planning and policy networks but rely on computationally expensive gradient-free optimization methods for high-dimensional control. Gradient-based methods are more efficient but have empirically underperformed, creating a need for an improved gradient-based approach.
AI.method: Dream-MPC generates few candidate trajectories from a rolled-out policy, optimizes each via gradient ascent using a learned world model, applies uncertainty regularization, and amortizes optimization over time by reusing previously optimized actions.
AI.result: Demonstrated on 24 continuous control tasks, Dream-MPC significantly improves underlying policy performance and outperforms gradient-free MPC and state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-based optimization with proper regularization and amortization can effectively surpass gradient-free methods in model-based RL, offering a more computationally efficient alternative for high-dimensional control.

title: FASQ: Flexible Accelerated Subspace Quantization for Calibration-Free LLM Compression
summary: Compressing large language models (LLMs) for deployment on commodity GPUs remains challenging: conventional scalar quantization is limited to fixed bit-widths (e.g., 8/4/3-bit), offers only a few discrete compression points, and typically requires calibration data. We present FASQ (Flexible Accelerated Subspace Quantization), a calibration-free framework that applies product quantization to LLM weight matrices. By tuning two parameters, sub-vector size and codebook cardinality, FASQ exposes a continuous design space spanning 27-49% of the original FP16 model size, filling compression gaps that fixed-bit schemes cannot reach. On Meta-Llama-3-8B, FASQ surpasses 4-bit GPTQ and AWQ in accuracy (67.1-67.7 avg.) at 37-42% model size, with consistent results on Qwen3-8B and Qwen3.5-9B-Base. To make product quantization practical at inference time, we design custom CUDA kernels: a LUT-free direct-compute GEMV for decode and an output-stationary double-buffered LUT GEMM for prefill, both with split-K parallelism. On an RTX~3090, FASQ achieves 45.2 tok/s decode at effective 4-bit (2.56x memory reduction) and 51.8 tok/s at effective 3-bit (2.80x), both surpassing FP16 tensor-core performance (43.9 tok/s) and delivering 1.6 to 1.8x the throughput of AWQ, 2.5 to 2.5x of GPTQ, and 4.3 to 5x of RTN. FASQ is the only compressed method that accelerates decode beyond FP16, offering calibration-free compression, continuous size-quality trade-offs, and real-time inference on a single consumer GPU.

AI.tldr: FASQ applies product quantization to LLM weights with continuous compression ratios (27-49% of FP16), achieving better accuracy than GPTQ/AWQ at 37-42% model size on Llama-3-8B, and delivering faster decode (45.2 tok/s) than FP16 tensor-core performance on a single RTX 3090 via custom CUDA kernels.
AI.motivation: Existing scalar quantization methods are limited to fixed bit-widths (e.g., 8/4/3-bit), offer only discrete compression points, and typically require calibration data, creating gaps in compression-quality trade-offs and deployment flexibility.
AI.method: FASQ applies product quantization to LLM weight matrices with two tunable parameters (sub-vector size and codebook cardinality) for continuous compression ratios, and designs custom CUDA kernels (LUT-free direct-compute GEMV for decode, output-stationary double-buffered LUT GEMM for prefill) for efficient inference.
AI.result: On Meta-Llama-3-8B, FASQ achieves 67.1-67.7 avg. accuracy at 37-42% model size, surpassing 4-bit GPTQ and AWQ. On RTX 3090, it delivers 45.2 tok/s decode (effective 4-bit, 2.56x memory reduction) and 51.8 tok/s (effective 3-bit, 2.80x), both exceeding FP16 tensor-core performance (43.9 tok/s), with 1.6-1.8x, 2.5-2.5x, and 4.3-5x throughput over AWQ, GPTQ, and RTN respectively.
AI.conclusion: FASQ is the only compressed method that accelerates decode beyond FP16, offering calibration-free compression, continuous size-quality trade-offs, and real-time inference on a single consumer GPU.

title: Executable World Models for ARC-AGI-3 in the Era of Coding Agents
summary: We evaluate an initial coding-agent system for ARC-AGI-3 in which the agent maintains an executable Python world model, verifies it against previous observations, refactors it toward simpler abstractions as a practical proxy for an MDL-like simplicity bias, and plans through the model before acting. The system is intentionally direct: it uses a scripted controller, predefined world-model interfaces, verifier programs, and a plan executor, but no hand-coded game-specific logic. We report results on the 25 public ARC-AGI-3 games. Each recorded playthrough uses a fresh agent instance with no access to previous playthrough-specific files or conversation state. Most games have a single recorded playthrough; for a few games, we report multiple independent fresh-agent playthroughs to expose run-to-run variability. The agent fully solved 7 games, achieved a Relative Human Action Efficiency greater than 75%, on 6 games, and obtained a mean per-game RHAE of 32.58%. Because the system uses no game-specific code, it can serve as a game-general baseline for ARC-AGI-3. Performance on the private validation set remains to be tested. Overall, the results provide preliminary evidence that verifier-driven executable world models are a promising approach for ARC-AGI-3 agents.

AI.tldr: The authors built a coding agent for ARC-AGI-3 that uses an executable world model, verifier-driven testing, and simplicity-biased refactoring without game-specific code. It fully solved 7 of 25 public games, achieved a mean RHAE of 32.58%, and serves as a game-general baseline.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether a verifier-driven executable world-model approach, without hand-coded game-specific logic, can serve as a general baseline agent for ARC-AGI-3.
AI.method: A scripted agent maintains a Python world model, verifies it against observations, refactors toward simpler abstractions (MDL proxy), and plans through the model before acting. No game-specific code is used; fresh agent instances per playthrough.
AI.result: Fully solved 7/25 public games; ≥75% RHAE on 6 games; mean per-game RHAE of 32.58%. Performance on private validation set untested.
AI.conclusion: Verifier-driven executable world models show promise as a game-general approach for ARC-AGI-3, with preliminary evidence supporting further development.

title: Physiologically Grounded Driver Behavior Classification: SHAP-Driven Elite Feature Selection and Hybrid Gradient Boosting for Multimodal Physiological Signals
summary: An interpretable and scalable framework for decoding driving behaviors from multimodal physiological signals is proposed in this study. We utilize multimodal physiological driving behavior large-scale dataset comprising synchronized electroencephalogram (EEG), electromyography (EMG), and galvanic skin response (GSR) signals. Our approach involves rigorous preprocessing followed by a domain-specific feature extraction pipeline targeting time-domain, frequency-domain, and derived physiological indices. To address high dimensionality, we employ SHAP-based elite feature selection, retaining the top 250 features to reduce computational overhead while preserving predictive power. Hyperparameter optimization for extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) and light gradient boosting machine (LightGBM) models is conducted using Bayesian optimization via Optuna. Finally, a weighted soft-voting ensemble is constructed to leverage the complementary strengths of both gradient boosting frameworks.
