AI.tldr: This paper proposes ISA (In-context Sparse Attention), a near-lossless sparse attention framework for in-context learning video editing that reduces attention latency by ~60%, and builds LIVEditor, a lightning video editing model using ISA and a curated 1.7M video-editing dataset, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Video editing with In-Context Learning (ICL) suffers from quadratic attention costs, creating a critical computational bottleneck that limits efficiency and scalability.
AI.method: ISA leverages two insights: (1) context tokens have lower saliency than source tokens, (2) Query sharpness correlates with approximation error. It uses a pre-selection strategy to prune redundant context and a dynamic query grouping mechanism that routes high-error queries to full attention and low-error ones to 0-th order Taylor sparse attention.
AI.result: LIVEditor achieves ~60% reduction in attention-module latency while surpassing state-of-the-art methods on EditVerseBench, IVE-Bench, and VIE-Bench benchmarks with near-lossless acceleration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ISA framework provides efficient, near-lossless acceleration for ICL video editing, and LIVEditor demonstrates that high-quality video editing can be achieved with significantly reduced computational cost.

title: Climate-based Pre-screening of Self-sustaining Regreening Opportunities in Drylands: A Case Study for Saudi Arabia
summary: Large-scale restoration in drylands is widely promoted to address land degradation and biodiversity loss, yet many efforts rely on long-term irrigation, limiting sustainability in water-scarce regions. A key challenge is identifying locations where native vegetation can persist without intensive management while minimizing costly field campaigns. A scalable pre-screening framework is presented that integrates climate and remote sensing data to enable cost-efficient site selection in arid environments using Saudi Arabia as a case study. A Climate Suitability Score (CSS), derived from machine learning models trained on expert-curated reference sites, captures complex climatic dependencies on vegetation persistence. Using multi-year ERA5-Land data for Saudi Arabia, national-scale prediction maps are generated and combined with vegetation indices to identify areas where climate is favorable, but vegetation remains underdeveloped. Multi-criteria screening reduces candidates to thirteen priority locations. Climatically analogous intact ecosystems provide benchmarks for restoration targets and indicate that an average 2.5 fold increase in vegetation coverage is a realistic target for restoration efforts. Overall, this approach narrows the search space, reduces costs, and supports resilient ecosystem recovery planning in water-limited regions.

AI.tldr: A machine learning-based pre-screening framework using climate and remote sensing data (Climate Suitability Score) helps identify cost-effective restoration sites in drylands without requiring irrigation, demonstrated in Saudi Arabia where 13 priority locations were identified with a potential 2.5x increase in vegetation coverage.
AI.motivation: Large-scale dryland restoration often fails due to reliance on unsustainable irrigation; there is a need to identify locations where native vegetation can persist without intensive management while reducing costly field surveys.
AI.method: A Climate Suitability Score (CSS) is developed using machine learning trained on expert-curated reference sites with ERA5-Land climate data, combined with vegetation indices to identify favorable-but-degraded areas, then multi-criteria screening narrows candidates to priority locations.
AI.result: National-scale prediction maps were generated for Saudi Arabia; 13 priority locations were identified; climatically analogous intact ecosystems suggest a realistic 2.5-fold increase in vegetation coverage as a restoration target.
AI.conclusion: This scalable pre-screening framework reduces search space and costs, supporting resilient ecosystem recovery planning in water-limited dryland regions without long-term irrigation dependence.

title: VL-UniTrack: A Unified Framework with Visual-Language Prompts for UAV-Ground Visual Tracking
summary: UAV-ground visual tracking (UGVT) aims to simultaneously track the same object from both the UAV and the ground view. However, existing two-stream methods suffer from isolated feature extraction and rely heavily on implicit appearance matching, which struggles to establish reliable correspondence under drastic view differences, leading to tracking unreliability. To address these limitations, we propose VL-UniTrack, a fully unified framework enhanced by visual-language prompts. By encoding features from both views within a single shared encoder, our method breaks the barrier of feature isolation to facilitate sufficient cross-view interaction. To overcome the ambiguity caused by relying solely on appearance matching, we design visual-language geometric prompting module, which fuses language descriptions with visual features to generate learnable prompts. These prompts are then fed into our prompt-guided cross-view adapter module to enable sufficient cross-view feature interaction and to guide the learning of view-specific feature representations. Furthermore, a confidence-modulated mutual distillation loss is proposed to regularize the training by mitigating noise propagation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the latest benchmark. The code can be downloaded in https://github.com/xuboyue1999/VL-UniTrack.git

AI.tldr: VL-UniTrack is a unified visual-language framework for UAV-ground visual tracking that uses a shared encoder, visual-language geometric prompting, and confidence-modulated mutual distillation to achieve state-of-the-art cross-view tracking performance.
AI.motivation: Existing two-stream methods suffer from isolated feature extraction and rely heavily on implicit appearance matching, which fails to establish reliable correspondence under drastic view differences between UAV and ground cameras, leading to tracking unreliability.
AI.method: (1) A fully unified framework with a single shared encoder for both views to enable cross-view interaction. (2) Visual-language geometric prompting module that fuses language descriptions with visual features to generate learnable prompts. (3) Prompt-guided cross-view adapter module for enhanced feature interaction and view-specific representation learning. (4) Confidence-modulated mutual distillation loss to regularize training and mitigate noise propagation.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on the latest UAV-ground visual tracking benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed VL-UniTrack effectively addresses cross-view tracking challenges by unifying feature encoding and leveraging visual-language prompts, outperforming existing methods.

title: Predict-then-Diffuse: Adaptive Response Length for Compute-Budgeted Inference in Diffusion LLMs
summary: Diffusion-based Large Language Models (D-LLMs) represent a promising frontier in generative AI, offering fully parallel token generation that can lead to significant throughput advantages and superior GPU utilization over traditional autoregressive paradigm. However, this parallelism is constrained by the requirement of a fixed-size response length prior to generation. This architectural limitation imposes a severe trade-off: oversized response length results in computational waste on semantically meaningless padding tokens, while undersized response length cause output truncation requiring costly re-computations that introduce unpredictable latency spikes. To tackle this issue, we propose Predict-then-Diffuse, a simple and model-agnostic framework, that enables compute-budgeted inference per input query by first estimating the response length and then using it to run inference with D-LLM. At its core lies a Adaptive Response Length Predictor (AdaRLP) auxiliary predictor that predicts the optimal response length given an input query. As a measure against under-predicting the response length and re-running inference with a higher response length, we introduce a data-driven safety mechanism, which trades a negligible padding overhead. As a whole, our framework limits the significant waste of computation on padding tokens and preserves output quality. Experimental validation on multiple datasets demonstrate that Predict-then-Diffuse significantly reduces computational costs (FLOP) compared to the default D-LLM inference mechanism and baselines based on heuristics, while being robust to skewed data distributions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Predict-then-Diffuse, a framework that predicts optimal response length before running inference with Diffusion-based LLMs, reducing computational waste from fixed-size generation while maintaining output quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based LLMs require fixed-size response length before generation, creating a trade-off where oversized lengths waste computation on padding tokens and undersized lengths cause truncation requiring costly re-computation.
AI.method: The framework uses an Adaptive Response Length Predictor (AdaRLP) to estimate optimal response length per query, plus a data-driven safety mechanism to handle under-prediction with minimal padding overhead.
AI.result: Experimental validation shows Predict-then-Diffuse significantly reduces computational costs (FLOP) compared to default D-LLM inference and heuristic baselines, while being robust to skewed data distributions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively limits wasteful computation on padding tokens and preserves output quality, offering a practical solution to the fixed-length limitation of D-LLMs.

title: GTF: Omnidirectional EPI Transformer for Light Field Super-Resolution
summary: Light field (LF) image super-resolution benefits from Epipolar Plane Images (EPIs), whose line slopes explicitly encode disparity. However, existing Transformer-based LF SR methods mainly attend to horizontal and vertical EPIs, leaving diagonal epipolar geometry underexplored. We present GTF, an omnidirectional EPI Transformer that explicitly models horizontal, vertical, 45-degree, and 135-degree EPIs within a unified reconstruction framework. GTF combines directional EPI processing, MacPI-based prior injection, adaptive directional fusion, and a topology-preserving feed-forward network to better exploit LF geometry. For the NTIRE 2026 fidelity tracks, we use GTF as the main model, while a lightweight GTF-Tiny variant targets the efficiency track. On five standard LF SR benchmarks covering both real-captured and synthetic scenes, GTF reaches 32.78 dB without inference-time enhancement, and stronger inference settings with EPSW and test-time augmentation further improve performance. Under the NTIRE 2026 efficiency constraint, GTF-Tiny attains 32.57 dB with only 0.915M parameters and 19.81 GFLOPs. In the NTIRE 2026 Light Field Image Super-Resolution Challenge, our submissions rank 3rd on Track 1 and Track 3 and 4th on Track 2. Architecture-evolution, channel-width, and inference analyses further support the effectiveness of diagonal EPI modeling, directional fusion, and the lightweight design.

AI.tldr: GTF is an omnidirectional EPI Transformer that models horizontal, vertical, and diagonal (45°, 135°) epipolar geometry for LF SR, achieving 32.78 dB on benchmarks and ranking top-4 in the NTIRE 2026 challenge.
AI.motivation: Existing Transformer-based LF SR methods mainly focus on horizontal and vertical EPIs, leaving diagonal epipolar geometry underexplored, which limits reconstruction quality.
AI.method: GTF combines directional EPI processing (horizontal, vertical, 45°, 135°), MacPI-based prior injection, adaptive directional fusion, and a topology-preserving feed-forward network within a unified framework.
AI.result: GTF reaches 32.78 dB on five standard benchmarks; GTF-Tiny achieves 32.57 dB with 0.915M params and 19.81 GFLOPs; ranked 3rd on Tracks 1&3 and 4th on Track 2 in NTIRE 2026.
AI.conclusion: Diagonal EPI modeling, directional fusion, and lightweight design are effective for LF SR, supported by architecture, channel-width, and inference analyses.

title: Jordan-RoPE: Non-Semisimple Relative Positional Encoding via Complex Jordan Blocks
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables; code available at https://github.com/ybzhang-nxu/jordan_rope
summary:
Relative positional encodings determine which functions of query-key lag can enter the primitive attention logit. RoPE supplies a rotary phase, while ALiBi supplies an additive distance bias. Motivated by group-theoretic views of linear translation-invariant positional encodings, we study a non-semisimple case in which a complex rotary eigenvalue and a nilpotent response live in the same defective Jordan block. The resulting relative operator generates oscillatory-polynomial features such as $e^{-γd}\cos(ωd)$, $e^{-γd}\sin(ωd)$, $d e^{-γd}\cos(ωd)$, and $d e^{-γd}\sin(ωd)$, for causal lag $d=i-j\geq 0$. Thus the construction realizes a distance-modulated phase basis $d e^{iωd}$, rather than merely adding a separate distance channel to RoPE.
  We formulate Exact Jordan-RoPE as a non-semisimple one-parameter representation, give its real block form, and specify the contragredient query action required by non-orthogonal positional maps. We also distinguish this exact representation from stabilized variants whose bounded shear improves numerical behavior but breaks the exact group law. Kernel-level diagnostics and a Jordan-friendly synthetic language-model task show that the coupled Jordan basis is useful when the target contains distance-modulated phase interactions. On a small WikiText-103 byte language model, a scaled-exact variant improves over RoPE and direct-sum baselines within the Jordan family, while RoPE+ALiBi remains strongest overall. The evidence is structural rather than a broad performance claim.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Jordan-RoPE, a novel relative positional encoding that combines rotary and nilpotent components in a defective Jordan block, generating oscillatory-polynomial features like d*e^{iωd} for causal attention. It provides exact mathematical formulations and shows modest improvements on small-scale language tasks, though RoPE+ALiBi remains strongest overall.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing positional encodings (RoPE, ALiBi) by exploring non-semisimple group-theoretic representations that can generate coupled distance-modulated phase interactions (e.g., d*e^{iωd}) which neither RoPE nor ALiBi alone can capture.
AI.method: The authors propose Exact Jordan-RoPE as a non-semisimple one-parameter representation using a defective Jordan block combining a complex rotary eigenvalue with a nilpotent response. They derive real block forms, specify contragredient query actions for non-orthogonal positional maps, and distinguish exact from stabilized variants. Evaluations include kernel-level diagnostics, synthetic tasks, and a small WikiText-103 byte language model.
AI.result: The Jordan basis is useful when targets contain distance-modulated phase interactions. On WikiText-103, a scaled-exact variant outperforms RoPE and direct-sum baselines within the Jordan family, but RoPE+ALiBi remains the strongest overall method.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides structural evidence that non-semisimple positional encodings (Jordan-RoPE) can capture oscillatory-polynomial features useful for certain attention patterns, but the improvements are modest and the contribution is primarily mathematical/structural rather than a broad performance claim.

title: From Diffusion to Rectified Flow: Rethinking Text-Based Segmentation
summary: Text-based image segmentation aims to delineate object boundaries within an image from text prompts, offering higher flexibility and broader application scope compared to traditional fixed-category segmentation tasks. Recent studies have shown that diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) can provide rich multimodal semantic features, leading to studies of using diffusion models as feature extractors for segmentation tasks. Such methods, however, inherit the generative natures of diffusion models that are harmful to discriminative segmentation tasks. In response, we propose RLFSeg, a novel framework that leverages Rectified Flow to learn direct mapping from the image to the segmentation mask within the latent space. The model is thus freed from the noise-denoise process and the need to optimize the time step of diffusion models, resulting in substantially better performance than previous diffusion-based methods, especially on zero-shot scenarios. By introducing label refinement and an Adaptive One-Step Sampling strategy, the model achieves higher accuracy even on a single inference step. The framework redirects a pretrained generative model to the discriminative segmentation task with zero modification to model structure, thus reveals promising application potential and significant research value.

AI.tldr: RLFSeg proposes using Rectified Flow instead of diffusion models for text-based image segmentation, eliminating the noise-denoise process to achieve better performance, especially in zero-shot scenarios, with an Adaptive One-Step Sampling strategy for single-step accuracy.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models used as feature extractors for segmentation inherit generative natures harmful to discriminative tasks (noise-denoise process, time step optimization). The authors aim to eliminate these drawbacks while retaining rich multimodal features.
AI.method: RLFSeg uses Rectified Flow to learn direct mapping from image to segmentation mask in latent space, bypassing the noise-denoise process. It introduces label refinement and Adaptive One-Step Sampling for single-step high accuracy, redirecting a pretrained generative model without structural modification.
AI.result: Substantially better performance than previous diffusion-based methods, especially in zero-shot scenarios. Achieves higher accuracy even on a single inference step.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively redirects pretrained generative models to discriminative segmentation tasks with zero structural modification, showing promising application potential and research value.

title: Capabilities of Auto-encoders and Principal Component Analysis of the Reduction of Microstructural Images; Application on the Acceleration of Phase-Field Simulations
comment: 21 pages, 8 figures. Preprint version of article published in Computational Materials Science
summary: In this work, a data-driven framework based on Phase-Field simulations data is proposed to highlight the capabilities of neural networks to ensure accurate low dimensionality reduction of simulated microstructural images and to provide time-series analysis. The dataset was indeed constructed from high-fidelity Phase-Field simulations. Analyses demonstrated that the association of auto-encoder neural networks and principal component analyses leads to ensure efficient and significant dimensionality reduction: 1/196 of reduction ratio with more than 80% of accuracy. These findings give insight to apply analyses on data from the latent dimension. Application of Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks showed the possibility of making next frame predictions; that makes possible the acceleration of Phase-Field simulation without the need of high computing resources. We discussed the application of such a framework on various areas of research. Different methods are proposed from the conducted analyses, in order to ensure dimensionality reduction, including auto-encoders, principal component analysis and Artificial Neural Networks, and time-series analysis, including LSTM and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU).

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a data-driven framework using Phase-Field simulation data to demonstrate neural networks' capabilities for dimensionality reduction and time-series analysis. Auto-encoder + PCA achieved 1/196 reduction ratio with >80% accuracy, and LSTM enabled next-frame predictions for accelerating Phase-Field simulations.
AI.motivation: To enable efficient dimensionality reduction of microstructural images from Phase-Field simulations and to accelerate these simulations through time-series prediction without requiring high computing resources.
AI.method: The framework uses auto-encoder neural networks combined with Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction, and LSTM/GRU neural networks for time-series analysis and next-frame prediction of microstructural evolution.
AI.result: Achieved a 1/196 reduction ratio with over 80% accuracy using auto-encoder + PCA. LSTM successfully enabled next-frame predictions, potentially accelerating Phase-Field simulations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed data-driven framework effectively combines dimensionality reduction (auto-encoders, PCA, ANNs) and time-series analysis (LSTM, GRU) methods, offering a pathway to accelerate Phase-Field simulations and enabling broader applications across various research areas.

title: DiCLIP: Diffusion Model Enhances CLIP's Dense Knowledge for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation
comment: This work is accepted by IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
summary: Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation (WSSS) with image-level labels typically leverages Class Activation Maps (CAMs) to achieve pixel-level predictions. Recently, Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) has been introduced to generate CAMs in WSSS. However, previous WSSS methods solely adopt CLIP's vision-language paired property for dense localization, neglecting its inherently limited dense knowledge across both visual and text modalities, which renders CAM generation suboptimal. In this work, we propose DiCLIP, a novel WSSS framework that leverages the generative diffusion model to enhance CLIP's dense knowledge across two modalities. Specifically, Visual Correlation Enhancement (VCE) and Text Semantic Augmentation (TSA) modules are proposed for dense prediction enhancement. To improve the spatial awareness of visual features, our VCE module utilizes diffusion's reliable spatial consistency to mitigate the over-smoothing issue in CLIP's attention. It designs the Attention Clustering Refinement (ACR) module to reliably extract diverse correlation maps from the diffusion model. The correlation maps act as a diversity bias for CLIP's self-attention, recursively pushing its visual features towards a more discriminative dense distribution. To augment the semantics of text embeddings, our TSA module argues that a single text modality is insufficient to encompass the variability of visual categories. Thus, we leverage diffusion's generative power to maintain a dynamic key-value cache model, shifting CAM generation from a patch-text matching mechanism to a novel visual knowledge retrieval paradigm. With these enhancements, DiCLIP not only outperforms state-of-the-art methods on PASCAL VOC and MS COCO but also significantly reduces training costs. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/zwyang6/DiCLIP.

AI.tldr: DiCLIP enhances CLIP-based Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation by using diffusion models to improve dense knowledge across visual and text modalities, achieving state-of-the-art results on PASCAL VOC and MS COCO with lower training costs.
AI.motivation: Previous WSSS methods using CLIP only exploit its vision-language paired property for dense localization but neglect CLIP's limited dense knowledge across both visual and text modalities, leading to suboptimal CAM generation.
AI.method: DiCLIP proposes two modules: (1) Visual Correlation Enhancement (VCE) with Attention Clustering Refinement (ACR) that uses diffusion's spatial consistency to improve CLIP's visual feature discriminability; (2) Text Semantic Augmentation (TSA) that leverages diffusion's generative power to build a dynamic key-value cache, shifting from patch-text matching to visual knowledge retrieval.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art WSSS methods on PASCAL VOC and MS COCO datasets while significantly reducing training costs.
AI.conclusion: Generative diffusion models can effectively enhance CLIP's dense knowledge for weakly supervised semantic segmentation, and the proposed framework achieves superior performance with higher efficiency.

title: Layerwise LQR for Geometry-Aware Optimization of Deep Networks
summary: Geometry-aware optimizers such as Newton and natural gradient can improve conditioning in deep learning, but scalable variants such as K-FAC, Shampoo, and related preconditioners usually impose structural approximations early, often discarding cross-layer interactions induced by the network computation. We introduce Layerwise LQR (LLQR), a framework for learning structured inverse preconditioners under a global layerwise optimal-control objective. The starting point is an exact equivalence: the steepest-descent step under a broad class of divergence-induced quadratic models--including Newton, Gauss-Newton, Fisher/natural-gradient, and intermediate-layer metrics--can be written as a finite-horizon Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) problem. This formulation serves as a reference that exposes the layerwise dynamics and cost matrices encoding the original dense geometry. We then derive a scalable relaxation that learns diagonal, (E-)Kronecker-factored, or other structured inverse preconditioners by minimizing the LQR objective and reusing them across iterations. The resulting optimizer wraps standard methods while retaining a principled connection to second-order geometry, without forming or inverting the global curvature matrix. Experiments on ResNets and Transformers show that LLQR improves optimization dynamics and often translates these gains into improved final test performance, while adding only modest wall-clock overhead. It establishes LLQR as a practical framework for geometry-aware second-order methods and a reference for evaluating scalable approximations.

AI.tldr: LLQR reformulates second-order optimization steps as LQR problems, enabling structured preconditioners that preserve layerwise dynamics while remaining computationally scalable, improving optimization and test performance in deep learning.
AI.motivation: Existing geometry-aware optimizers (K-FAC, Shampoo) impose early structural approximations that discard cross-layer interactions. The authors aim to learn structured preconditioners under a global layerwise optimal-control objective without sacrificing these interactions.
AI.method: The paper establishes an equivalence between steepest-descent under divergence-induced quadratic models and a finite-horizon LQR problem. This LQR formulation exposes layerwise dynamics and cost matrices. A scalable relaxation is then derived to learn diagonal, Kronecker-factored, or other structured inverse preconditioners by minimizing the LQR objective and reusing them across iterations.
AI.result: Experiments on ResNets and Transformers demonstrate improved optimization dynamics and often improved final test performance, with only modest wall-clock overhead compared to standard methods.
AI.conclusion: LLQR provides a practical, principled framework for geometry-aware second-order methods that bridges the gap between full second-order geometry and scalable approximations, serving as both an optimizer and a reference for evaluating approximations.

title: Reference-based Category Discovery: Unsupervised Object Detection with Category Awareness
summary: Traditional one-shot detection methods have addressed the closed-set problem in object detection, but the high cost of data annotation remains a critical challenge. General unsupervised methods generate pseudo boxes without category labels, thus failing to achieve category-aware classification. To overcome these limitations, we propose Reference-based Category Discovery (RefCD), an unsupervised detector that enables category-aware\footnotemark[1] detection without any manually annotated labels. It leverages feature similarity between predicted objects and unlabeled reference images. Unlike previous unsupervised methods that lack category guidance and one-shot methods which require labeled data, RefCD introduces a carefully designed feature similarity loss to explicitly guide the learning of potential category-specific features. Additionally, RefCD supports category-agnostic detection without reference images, serving as a unified framework. Comprehensive quantitative and qualitative analysis of category-aware and category-agnostic detection results demonstrates its effectiveness, and RefCD can learn category information in an unsupervised paradigm even without category labels.

AI.tldr: RefCD proposes an unsupervised object detection method that achieves category-aware detection without labeled data by using feature similarity between predicted objects and unlabeled reference images.
AI.motivation: Traditional one-shot methods address closed-set detection but require expensive annotations; unsupervised methods generate pseudo boxes without category labels, failing to achieve category-aware classification.
AI.method: RefCD leverages feature similarity between predicted objects and unlabeled reference images, with a carefully designed feature similarity loss to guide learning of potential category-specific features. It also supports category-agnostic detection without reference images as a unified framework.
AI.result: Comprehensive quantitative and qualitative analysis demonstrates effectiveness in both category-aware and category-agnostic detection tasks.
AI.conclusion: RefCD can learn category information in an unsupervised paradigm even without category labels, overcoming limitations of both traditional one-shot and unsupervised methods.

title: Adaptive Consensus in LLM Ensembles via Sequential Evidence Accumulation: Automatic Budget Identification and Calibrated Commit Signals
summary: Large Language Model ensembles improve reasoning accuracy up to a performance boundary; beyond it, additional deliberation degrades accuracy. Static-budget methods cannot detect this boundary. Extended-thinking architectures compound the problem: a wrong answer after 120k tokens is indistinguishable from a correct one. We introduce DASE (Deliberative Adaptive Stopping Ensemble), a stopping heuristic for iterative ensemble deliberation that commits early on genuine consensus and applies a global-frequency fallback on fragmented evidence. Two configurations are evaluated: a persistence heuristic and DASE-Spatial (arena half-width W). Three contributions. (1) DASE produces a commit-type routing partition complementary to verbalized single-call confidence. On a contamination-controlled corpus (AIME 2010-2023, N=254, 3 seeds), a 120B ensemble achieves a 24.8 pp routing gap (right-wall 97.1% vs. left-wall 73.6%), statistically equivalent to Opus 4.6 Standard verbalized confidence at coverage-matched threshold (25.7 pp gap; bootstrap CI on difference: [-12.0, +10.3] pp, p=0.873). The two mechanisms disagree on 27% of routing assignments, establishing them as complements rather than substitutes; every DASE decision is accompanied by a machine-readable deliberation record. (2) Adaptive stopping, not injection bandwidth, drives accuracy gains. On AIME-300, bandwidth accounts for only 0.3 pp (ns); on GPQA-Extended, 4.4 pp bandwidth versus 5.0 pp stopping effect. DASE-Spatial ties Debate-Dense at its optimal budget using one-tenth the injection bandwidth and identifies that budget automatically; W=8 (65.0%) significantly outperforms W=4 (59.3%) on AIME-300 (adj p=0.0042). (3) Injection-based methods exhibit a retrospective accuracy-vs-inference inverted-U on both benchmarks; this pattern is hypothesis-generating for future work.

AI.tldr: DASE is a stopping heuristic for LLM ensemble deliberation that detects genuine consensus early and applies a global-frequency fallback, avoiding accuracy degradation from over-deliberation. It achieves a 24.8 pp routing gap on AIME problems, statistically equivalent to Opus 4.6 verbalized confidence, and demonstrates that adaptive stopping—not injection bandwidth—drives accuracy gains.
AI.motivation: LLM ensembles improve reasoning accuracy only up to a performance boundary; beyond it, further deliberation degrades accuracy. Static-budget methods cannot detect this boundary, and extended-thinking architectures compound the problem by making wrong answers after many tokens indistinguishable from correct ones.
AI.method: DASE (Deliberative Adaptive Stopping Ensemble): a stopping heuristic that commits early when genuine consensus is detected and falls back to global-frequency analysis on fragmented evidence. Evaluated via a persistence heuristic and DASE-Spatial (arena half-width W).
AI.result: (1) 24.8 pp routing gap (97.1% vs 73.6%) on AIME, statistically equivalent to Opus 4.6 verbalized confidence (25.7 pp gap; p=0.873). Two mechanisms disagree on 27% of assignments, establishing complementarity. (2) Adaptive stopping drives gains (5.0 pp) over bandwidth (4.4 pp on GPQA). DASE-Spatial W=8 (65.0%) outperforms W=4 (59.3%) on AIME-300. (3) Injection-based methods exhibit an inverted-U accuracy-vs-inference pattern.
AI.conclusion: DASE effectively detects the accuracy boundary in LLM ensemble deliberation, providing a principled stopping mechanism. Adaptive stopping, not bandwidth, is the key driver of accuracy improvements. The observed inverted-U pattern in injection methods suggests avenues for future research.

title: Advancing Aesthetic Image Generation via Composition Transfer
summary: Composition is a cornerstone of visual aesthetics, influencing the appeal of an image. While its principles operate independently of specific content, in practice, composition is often coupled with semantics. As a result, existing methods often enhance composition either through implicit learning or by semantics-based layout control, rather than explicitly modeling composition itself. To address this gap, we introduce Composer, a framework rooted in aesthetic theory, designed to model composition in a semantic-agnostic manner. First, it supports composition transfer by extracting key composition-aware representations from a reference image and leveraging a tailored conditional guidance module to control composition based on pre-trained diffusion models. Second, when users specify only text themes without a composition reference, Composer supports theme-driven composition retrieval by leveraging the in-context learning capabilities of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), achieving explicit composition planning. To enhance composition in a reference-free mode, we conduct text-to-composition fine-tuning on the trained control module to enable implicit composition planning. Furthermore, we curated a high-quality dataset comprising 2 million image-text pairs using state-of-the-art generative models to support model training. Experimental results demonstrate that Composer significantly enhances aesthetic quality in text-to-image tasks and facilitates personalized composition control and transfer, offering users precision and flexibility in the creative process.

AI.tldr: Composer is a framework that models visual composition in a semantic-agnostic way using diffusion models and LVLMs, enabling composition transfer, retrieval, and planning for enhanced aesthetic quality in text-to-image generation.
AI.motivation: Existing composition enhancement methods typically couple composition with semantics via implicit learning or layout control, lacking explicit modeling of composition itself as a separate aesthetic concept.
AI.method: (1) Extracts composition-aware representations from reference images with a conditional guidance module in pre-trained diffusion models for composition transfer. (2) Uses LVLMs' in-context learning for theme-driven composition retrieval when no reference is given. (3) Conducts text-to-composition fine-tuning for reference-free implicit planning. (4) Curates a 2M image-text pair dataset using generative models.
AI.result: Composer significantly improves aesthetic quality in text-to-image tasks and enables personalized composition control and transfer with precision and flexibility.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, semantic-agnostic composition modeling is effective for enhancing aesthetics, and the framework offers users flexible composition control across reference-based, retrieval-based, and reference-free modes.

title: Road Risk Monitor: A Deployable U.S. Road Incident Forecasting System with Live Weather and Road-Level Tiles
summary: Nationwide road-incident forecasting is a systems problem before it is a modeling problem. A usable service must connect historical incident archives, historicalandliveweather,nationalroadgeometry, offline model training, tile generation, web serving and runtime handoff. This paper presents Road Risk Monitor, a U.S.-wide road-safety stack that combines a nationwide H3 baseline trained on FARS fatal-crash data with a road-segment forecasting pipeline trained from TIGER/Line geometry and US-Accidents events, then serves predictions through live APIs, raster tiles, JSON road tiles, and a public web application.

AI.tldr: Road Risk Monitor is a full-stack system for nationwide road-incident forecasting, combining historical crash data, weather, road geometry, and live APIs to serve predictions via multiple interfaces.
AI.motivation: The authors argue that road-incident forecasting is fundamentally a systems engineering problem (connecting data sources, training, serving) rather than just a modeling problem.
AI.method: Combines an H3-based baseline trained on FARS fatal-crash data with a road-segment forecasting pipeline using TIGER/Line geometry and US-Accidents events, then deploys via live APIs, raster tiles, JSON road tiles, and a web app.
AI.result: A U.S.-wide operational road-safety stack with multiple serving interfaces (APIs, tiles, web application).
AI.conclusion: Building a usable nationwide road-risk service requires addressing the full systems pipeline—data integration, model training, tile generation, and runtime deployment—not just model accuracy.

title: Temporal Structure Matters for Efficient Test-Time Adaptation in Wearable Human Activity Recognition
summary: Wearable human activity recognition (WHAR) models often suffer from performance degradation under real-world cross-user distribution shifts. Test-time adaptation (TTA) mitigates this degradation by adapting models online using unlabeled test streams, yet existing methods largely inherit assumptions from vision tasks and underexploit the inherent inter-window temporal structure in WHAR streams. In this paper, we revisit such temporal structure as a feature-conditioned inference signal rather than merely an output-space smoothing prior. We derive the insight that temporal continuity and observation-induced feature deviations provide complementary cues for determining when to preserve or release temporal inertia and where to route prediction refinement during likely transitions. Building upon this insight, we propose SIGHT, a lightweight and backpropagation-free TTA framework for WHAR, enabling real-time edge deployment. SIGHT estimates predictive surprise by comparing the current feature with a prototype-based expected state, and then uses the resulting feature deviation to guide geometry-aware transition routing based on prototype alignment and stream-level marginal habit tracking. Evaluations on real-world datasets confirm that SIGHT outperforms existing TTA baselines while reducing computational and memory costs.

AI.tldr: SIGHT is a lightweight, backpropagation-free test-time adaptation framework for wearable human activity recognition that leverages temporal continuity and feature deviations to guide transition-aware prediction refinement, outperforming existing methods with lower computational cost.
AI.motivation: WHAR models degrade under cross-user distribution shifts. Existing TTA methods inherit vision-based assumptions and fail to exploit the inherent inter-window temporal structure in WHAR streams.
AI.method: SIGHT uses prototype-based predictive surprise estimation from feature deviations, then guides geometry-aware transition routing via prototype alignment and stream-level marginal habit tracking. It is backpropagation-free and lightweight for edge deployment.
AI.result: Outperforms existing TTA baselines on real-world WHAR datasets while reducing computational and memory costs.
AI.conclusion: Temporal continuity and feature deviations offer complementary cues for effective online adaptation in WHAR, enabling efficient real-time deployment on edge devices.

title: Hierarchical Support Vector State Partitioning for Distilling Black Box Reinforcement Learning Policies
comment: Accepted for poster presentation at HHAI 2026
summary: We introduce State Vector Space Partitioning (SVSP), a novel method to mimic a black box reinforcement learning policy using a set of human-interpretable subpolicies. By partitioning a distillation dataset of state action pairs with linear support vector machine splits, SVSP constructs a compact and structured representation of the original policy. Our method improves mean return by +7.4\% over previous critic driven state partitioning attempts such as Voronoi State Partitioning (VSP) and +2.8\% over the original TD3 policy, while reducing the number of required subpolicies against VSP by 82.1\%. Our results pave the path towards a more flexible form of distillation where both the decision boundary and surrogate models can be chosen within a margin of the original black box behavior.

AI.tldr: SVSP uses linear SVM splits to partition state-action pairs into interpretable subpolicies, achieving better performance (+7.4% over VSP, +2.8% over TD3) while reducing subpolicies by 82.1%.
AI.motivation: To create human-interpretable subpolicies from a black-box reinforcement learning policy by partitioning distillation data with linear SVM splits, improving upon prior state partitioning methods.
AI.method: State Vector Space Partitioning (SVSP) partitions a distillation dataset of state-action pairs using linear SVM splits to construct a compact, structured representation of the original policy as a set of interpretable subpolicies.
AI.result: SVSP improves mean return by +7.4% over Voronoi State Partitioning (VSP) and +2.8% over the original TD3 policy, while reducing the number of required subpolicies by 82.1% compared to VSP.
AI.conclusion: The results enable a more flexible form of distillation where both decision boundaries and surrogate models can be chosen within a margin of the original black-box behavior.

title: UniPCB: A Generation-Assisted Detection Framework for PCB Defect Inspection
summary: Printed Circuit Board (PCB) defect inspection faces two compounding challenges: scarce and imbalanced defect samples that limit model training, and insufficient feature representation under complex circuit backgrounds. Existing generation methods rely on single-modality conditions with coarse structural control, while detection methods improve architectures without addressing the data bottleneck. To resolve both challenges jointly, we propose a generation-assisted PCB defect inspection framework that integrates controlled defect synthesis with task-specific defect detection. On the generation side, a Multi-modal Condition Generator extracts complementary edge, depth, and text conditions in parallel. A ScaleEncoder then embeds these conditions into the diffusion U-Net at four resolutions, and a Condition Modulation applies FiLM-style spatially-adaptive modulation at each scale, enabling structurally aligned and defect-aware sample synthesis. On the detection side, an Inverted Residual Shift Attention couples self-attention with shift-wise convolution to jointly capture global context and local texture, and a Cross-level Complementary Fusion Block generates pixel-level gates for selective cross-level feature fusion. The synthesized samples directly enrich the detection training set, so that improvements in generation compound with improvements in detection. Extensive experiments on DsPCBSD+ demonstrate that UniPCB achieves mAP@0.5 of 98.0% and mAP@0.5:0.95 of 61.8% on defect detection, surpassing all compared methods, while the generation branch attains an FID of 129.61 and SSIM of 0.619, outperforming existing conditional generation approaches.

AI.tldr: UniPCB integrates multi-modal conditional diffusion-based defect synthesis with a task-specific detection network (Inverted Residual Shift Attention + Cross-level Fusion) to jointly solve data scarcity and detection challenges in PCB defect inspection, achieving state-of-the-art results on both generation (FID 129.61, SSIM 0.619) and detection (mAP@0.5 98.0%).
AI.motivation: PCB defect inspection suffers from two intertwined challenges: scarce/imbalanced defect samples that limit model training, and insufficient feature representation under complex circuit backgrounds. Existing generation methods use single-modality conditions with coarse control, while detection methods improve architectures without solving the underlying data bottleneck.
AI.method: UniPCB has two sides: (1) Generation side: Multi-modal Condition Generator extracts complementary edge, depth, and text conditions; ScaleEncoder embeds these into diffusion U-Net at four resolutions; Condition Modulation applies FiLM-style spatially-adaptive modulation at each scale for structurally aligned and defect-aware synthesis. (2) Detection side: Inverted Residual Shift Attention couples self-attention with shift-wise convolution for global context and local texture; Cross-level Complementary Fusion Block generates pixel-level gates for selective cross-level feature fusion. Synthesized samples enrich the detection training set.
AI.result: On DsPCBSD+, UniPCB achieves mAP@0.5 of 98.0% and mAP@0.5:0.95 of 61.8% on defect detection, surpassing all compared methods. The generation branch attains FID of 129.61 and SSIM of 0.619, outperforming existing conditional generation approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed generation-assisted framework effectively addresses both data scarcity and detection performance jointly. The compound improvement loop—better generation enriches training data, which in turn improves detection—demonstrates a promising paradigm for PCB defect inspection and potentially similar industrial anomaly detection tasks.

title: HUGO-CS: A Hybrid-Labeled, Uncertainty-Aware, General-Purpose, Observational Dataset for Cold Spray
summary: Cold spraying is an increasingly common approach for repairing and manufacturing components due to its solid-state manufacturing capabilities. However, process optimization remains difficult due to many interdependent parameters and the lack of large-scale, machine-readable data to support modeling. While the scientific literature contains many relevant experiments, results are inconsistently reported (often in tables and figures) and use non-uniform units, limiting utilization at scale. To address these limitations, this work presents HUGO-CS, a literature-derived dataset of 4,383 cold-spray experiments with 144 features from 1,124 sources, exceeding the previous largest dataset (137 samples) by 30x. With completely manual extraction requiring an average of 91 minutes per document, this work designs and leverages a Hybrid-labeled, Uncertainty-aware, General-purpose, Observational extraction framework, called HUGO, to support this extraction. HUGO combines automated LLM-based labeling with targeted manual label refinement to handle this experimental result extraction process from scientific literature. To balance labeling efficiency with extraction accuracy, HUGO introduces a Hierarchical Risk Mitigation (HRM) to route LLM outputs with a high risk of potential errors for manual review, while retaining low-risk records as auto-labeled. Lastly, HUGO post-processing consolidates categorical descriptors, maps reported feedstock chemistries into structured continuous compositions, and normalizes units across sources. Of the 4,383 reported experiments, 1,765 are hand-labeled, providing a high-quality labeled subset for benchmarking, error analysis, and higher-fidelity data points. All code to replicate this work, along with the complete HUGO-CS dataset, are released under a CC-BY license at https://github.com/sprice134/HUGO.

AI.tldr: HUGO-CS is a literature-derived dataset of 4,383 cold-spray experiments with 144 features from 1,124 sources, created using a Hybrid-labeled, Uncertainty-aware, General-purpose, Observational (HUGO) extraction framework that combines LLM-based auto-labeling with manual refinement, featuring Hierarchical Risk Mitigation to route high-error-risk outputs for human review.
AI.motivation: Cold spray process optimization is difficult due to many interdependent parameters and lack of large-scale, machine-readable data. Existing results are inconsistently reported with non-uniform units, limiting utilization at scale.
AI.method: The HUGO framework combines automated LLM-based labeling with targeted manual label refinement, introduces Hierarchical Risk Mitigation (HRM) to route high-risk LLM outputs for manual review while retaining low-risk auto-labeled records, and uses post-processing to consolidate categorical descriptors, map feedstock chemistries into structured continuous compositions, and normalize units.
AI.result: The dataset contains 4,383 experiments with 144 features from 1,124 sources, exceeding the previous largest dataset (137 samples) by 30x. 1,765 experiments are hand-labeled, providing a high-quality subset for benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a large-scale, machine-readable cold-spray dataset and a generalizable extraction framework (HUGO) for converting scientific literature into structured data, with all code and data released under CC-BY license.

title: CAST: Mitigating Object Hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models via Caption-Guided Visual Attention Steering
summary: Although Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance on downstream tasks, they frequently produce contents that deviate from visual information, leading to object hallucination. To tackle this, recent works mostly depend on expensive manual annotations and training cost, or decoding strategies which significantly increase inference time. In this work, we observe that LVLMs' attention to visual information is significantly enhanced when answering caption queries compared to non-caption queries. Inspired by this phenomenon, we propose Caption-guided Visual Attention Steering (CAST), a training-free, plug-and-play hallucination mitigation method that leverages the attention activation pattern corresponding to caption queries to enhance LVLMs' visual perception capability. Specifically, we use probing techniques to identify attention heads that are highly sensitive to caption queries and estimate optimized steering directions for their outputs. This steering strengthens LVLM's fine-grained visual perception capabilities, thereby effectively mitigating object hallucination. CAST reduced object hallucination by an average of 6.03% across five widely used LVLMs and five benchmarks including both discriminative and generative tasks, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance while adding little inference cost and preserving other foundational capabilities.

AI.tldr: CAST is a training-free, plug-and-play method that leverages caption-guided attention steering to mitigate object hallucination in LVLMs by enhancing their visual perception with minimal inference cost.
AI.motivation: LVLMs frequently produce object hallucinations (content deviating from visual information). Existing solutions rely on expensive manual annotations/training or decoding strategies that increase inference time.
AI.method: CAST uses probing to identify attention heads sensitive to caption queries, then estimates optimized steering directions for their outputs to enhance fine-grained visual perception.
AI.result: CAST reduced object hallucination by an average of 6.03% across five LVLMs and five benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art performance with minimal inference cost.
AI.conclusion: CAST effectively mitigates object hallucination in a training-free, plug-and-play manner while preserving other foundational capabilities and adding little computational overhead.

title: Explaining and Preventing Alignment Collapse in Iterative RLHF
summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) typically assumes a static or non-strategic reward model (RM). In iterative deployment, however, the policy generates the data on which the RM is retrained, creating a feedback loop. Building on the Stackelberg game formulation of this interaction, we derive an analytical decomposition of the policy's true optimization gradient into a standard policy gradient and a parameter-steering term that captures the policy's influence on the RM's future parameters. We show that standard iterative RLHF, which drops this steering term entirely, suffers from alignment collapse: the policy systematically exploits the RM's blind spots, producing low-quality, high-reward outputs whose feedback reinforces the very errors it exploits. To mitigate this, we propose foresighted policy optimization (FPO), a mechanism-design intervention that restores the missing steering term by regularizing the policy's parameter-steering effect on RM updates. We instantiate FPO via a scalable first-order approximation and demonstrate that it prevents alignment collapse on both controlled environments and an LLM alignment pipeline using Llama-3.2-1B.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies that iterative RLHF creates a feedback loop where the policy exploits the reward model's blind spots, leading to alignment collapse. The authors propose Foresighted Policy Optimization (FPO) which adds a regularization term to account for the policy's influence on future RM updates, preventing this collapse.
AI.motivation: Standard iterative RLHF assumes a static reward model, but in practice the policy's outputs become training data for future RM retraining, creating a feedback loop. This causes the policy to exploit RM blind spots, producing low-quality but high-reward outputs that reinforce errors.
AI.method: The authors derive an analytical gradient decomposition into a standard policy gradient and a parameter-steering term (policy's influence on future RM parameters). They propose FPO, which restores the missing steering term via regularization, implemented as a scalable first-order approximation.
AI.result: FPO prevents alignment collapse in both controlled environments and LLM alignment with Llama-3.2-1B, compared to standard iterative RLHF which suffers from systematic exploitation of RM blind spots.
AI.conclusion: Iterative RLHF requires accounting for the feedback loop between policy and reward model; FPO provides a principled, scalable solution to prevent alignment collapse by incorporating the policy's influence on RM updates.

title: Contact Matrix: Enhancing Dance Motion Synthesis with Precise Interaction Modeling
summary: Generating realistic reactive motions, in which one person reacts to the fixed motions of others, is challenging due to strict interaction constraints and a limited feasible solution space. This paper focuses on a typical scenario: duet dance, where high-quality data is scarce, motion patterns are complex, and the details of human interactions are both intricate and abundant. To tackle these challenges, we propose a novel two-stage framework. In the first stage, we introduce a motion VQ-VAE with separate body-part encoders and a joint decoder, enabling specialized codebooks to enhance representation capacity while dynamically modeling dependencies across body parts during decoding, thereby preventing inconsistencies in the generated motions. In the second stage, we propose a contact-aware diffusion model for reactive motion generation that jointly generates motion and a contact matrix between individuals, enabling explicit interaction modeling and providing guidance toward more precise and constrained interaction dynamics during sampling. Experiments show that our method outperforms Duolando with lower $\text{FID}_k$ (8.89 vs. 25.30) and $\text{FID}_{cd}$ (8.01 vs. 9.97), as well as a higher BED (0.4606 vs. 0.2858), indicating improved interaction fidelity and rhythmic synchronization.

AI.tldr: A two-stage framework for generating realistic duet dance motions: first using a motion VQ-VAE with separate body-part encoders and joint decoder, then a contact-aware diffusion model that jointly generates motion and contact matrices, outperforming existing methods on interaction fidelity and rhythmic synchronization.
AI.motivation: Generating realistic reactive motions for duet dance is challenging due to scarce high-quality data, complex motion patterns, intricate interaction details, strict interaction constraints, and limited feasible solution space.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) Motion VQ-VAE with separate body-part encoders and a joint decoder for enhanced representation and dependency modeling; (2) Contact-aware diffusion model that jointly generates motion and contact matrix for explicit interaction modeling.
AI.result: Outperformed Duolando with lower FID_k (8.89 vs 25.30), lower FID_cd (8.01 vs 9.97), and higher BED (0.4606 vs 0.2858).
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework achieves improved interaction fidelity and rhythmic synchronization for duet dance reactive motion generation.

title: QUIVER: Cost-Aware Adaptive Preference Querying in Surrogate-Assisted Evolutionary Multi-Objective Optimization
comment: Accepted at Genetic and Evolutionary Computation Conference (GECCO '26)
summary:
Interactive multi-objective optimization systems face a budget allocation dilemma: one can spend resources on expensive objective evaluations or on eliciting decision-maker preferences that identify the relevant region of the Pareto set. Moreover, preference elicitation itself spans modalities with different information content and cognitive burden, ranging from cheap, noisy pairwise preference statements (PS) to richer but costlier indifference adjustments (IA).
  We study cost-aware optimization under an unknown scalarization and introduce QUIVER (Query-Informed Value Estimation for Regret), a surrogate-assisted evolutionary multi-objective optimizer that adaptively chooses between objective evaluations and heterogeneous preference queries. At each step, QUIVER selects the next action by maximizing the expected decision-quality improvement per unit total cost. Across DTLZ and WFG benchmarks under synthetic decision-maker models, QUIVER achieves the lowest final utility regret on challenging WFG problems (utility regret of 2.14 on WFG4, 2.82 on WFG9: a 25% improvement over baselines), outperforming all single-modality baselines. We analyze how the optimal mix of PS and IA adapts to problem difficulty: on easy problems (DTLZ2), QUIVER selects 80\% PS queries; on hard problems (WFG9), it shifts to 35% IA queries. This adaptive modality selection demonstrates cost-aware preference learning in action.

AI.tldr: QUIVER is a surrogate-assisted evolutionary multi-objective optimizer that adaptively balances spending between expensive objective evaluations and heterogeneous preference queries (pairwise vs. indifference adjustments) to minimize regret under a total-cost budget.
AI.motivation: Interactive multi-objective optimization faces a budget allocation dilemma between costly objective evaluations and preference elicitation, where different preference modalities offer varying information content and cognitive burden.
AI.method: QUIVER uses surrogate-assisted optimization with adaptive action selection, choosing between objective evaluations and heterogeneous preference queries (PS vs. IA) by maximizing expected decision-quality improvement per unit cost.
AI.result: On WFG benchmarks, QUIVER achieves utility regret of 2.14 (WFG4) and 2.82 (WFG9), a 25% improvement over baselines. On easy problems (DTLZ2) it selects 80% PS queries; on hard problems (WFG9) it shifts to 35% IA queries.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive modality selection based on cost-awareness improves optimization efficiency, with the optimal mix of cheap pairwise vs. costly indifference queries shifting based on problem difficulty.

title: Physical Adversarial Clothing Evades Visible-Thermal Detectors via Non-Overlapping RGB-T Pattern
summary: Visible-thermal (RGB-T) object detection is a crucial technology for applications such as autonomous driving, where multimodal fusion enhances performance in challenging conditions like low light. However, the security of RGB-T detectors, particularly in the physical world, has been largely overlooked. This paper proposes a novel approach to RGB-T physical attacks using adversarial clothing with a non-overlapping RGB-T pattern (NORP). To simulate full-view (0$^{\circ}$--360$^{\circ}$) RGB-T attacks, we construct 3D RGB-T models for human and adversarial clothing. NORP is a new adversarial pattern design using distinct visible and thermal materials without overlap, avoiding the light reduction in overlapping RGB-T patterns (ORP). To optimize the NORP on adversarial clothing, we propose a spatial discrete-continuous optimization (SDCO) method. We systematically evaluated our method on RGB-T detectors with different fusion architectures, demonstrating high attack success rates both in the digital and physical worlds. Additionally, we introduce a fusion-stage ensemble method that enhances the transferability of adversarial attacks across unseen RGB-T detectors with different fusion architectures.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes NORP, a non-overlapping RGB-T adversarial pattern on clothing, with SDCO optimization method, to enable effective physical-world attacks on RGB-T object detectors across full-view angles, demonstrating high success rates and cross-architecture transferability.
AI.motivation: The security of RGB-T detectors, particularly against physical-world attacks, has been largely overlooked despite their importance in applications like autonomous driving under challenging conditions.
AI.method: The authors design adversarial clothing with non-overlapping RGB-T patterns (NORP), construct 3D RGB-T models for full-view simulation, and propose a spatial discrete-continuous optimization (SDCO) method. They also introduce a fusion-stage ensemble method for transferability.
AI.result: High attack success rates achieved both in digital and physical worlds across RGB-T detectors with different fusion architectures. The fusion-stage ensemble method enhances transferability to unseen detectors.
AI.conclusion: RGB-T detectors are vulnerable to physical adversarial attacks via specially designed clothing patterns, and the proposed NORP+SDCO approach is effective and transferable across different fusion architectures.

title: A Mean Curvature Approach to Boundary Detection: Geometric Insights for Unsupervised Learning
summary: Accurate boundary detection in high-dimensional data remains a central challenge in unsupervised learning, particularly in the presence of non-linear structures and heterogeneous densities. In this work, we introduce Mean Curvature Boundary Points (MCBP), a novel geometric framework grounded in Geometric Machine Learning that departs from traditional density-based approaches by explicitly modeling the intrinsic curvature of the data manifold. The method relies on a discrete approximation of the shape operator, estimated from local k-nearest neighbor patches, to compute pointwise mean curvature without requiring explicit manifold parametrization. The key insight of MCBP is to use mean curvature as a principled descriptor of boundary structure: high-curvature regions naturally correspond to transitions between clusters, geometric irregularities, and low-density interfaces. This yields a unified geometric interpretation of boundary, outlier, and transition points. We further introduce an adaptive percentile-based thresholding scheme that enables multiscale boundary extraction without relying on ad hoc density parameters. Beyond detection, we propose a curvature-driven data decomposition that separates samples into smooth (low-curvature) and boundary (high-curvature) subsets, effectively acting as a non-linear geometric filtering mechanism. This representation enhances cluster separability and improves the robustness of downstream unsupervised algorithms. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that MCBP consistently improves clustering performance, particularly in complex and high-dimensional scenarios. These results position MCBP as a concrete contribution to Geometric Machine Learning, highlighting the potential of curvature-aware analysis as a unifying paradigm bridging differential geometry and data-driven modeling.

AI.tldr: MCBP introduces a geometric framework that uses mean curvature (estimated from local neighborhoods) to detect boundaries and transition regions in high-dimensional data, improving clustering performance through curvature-driven data decomposition.
AI.motivation: Accurate boundary detection in high-dimensional data is challenging due to non-linear structures and heterogeneous densities. Traditional density-based approaches fail to capture geometric properties of the data manifold.
AI.method: MCBP computes pointwise mean curvature via discrete approximation of the shape operator from k-nearest neighbor patches, without requiring explicit manifold parametrization. It uses adaptive percentile-based thresholding for multiscale boundary extraction and curvature-driven data decomposition into smooth (low-curvature) and boundary (high-curvature) subsets.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show MCBP consistently improves clustering performance, especially in complex and high-dimensional scenarios.
AI.conclusion: MCBP offers a principled geometric alternative to density-based boundary detection, positioning curvature-aware analysis as a unifying paradigm bridging differential geometry and data-driven modeling.

title: Multi-Level Bidirectional Biomimetic Learning for EEG-Based Visual Decoding
summary: EEG-based visual neural decoding aims to align neural responses with visual stimuli for tasks such as image retrieval. However, limited paired data and a fundamental mismatch between high-fidelity digital images and biological visual perception - distorted by retinotopic mapping and subject-specific neuroanatomy - severely impede cross-modal alignment. To address this, we propose MB2L, a Multi-Level Bidirectional Biomimetic Learning framework that incorporates structured physiological inductive biases into representation learning. Specifically, we propose Adaptive Blur with Visual Priors to mitigate perceptual-structural mismatch by reweighting visual inputs according to retinotopic priors. We further propose Biomimetic Visual Feature Extraction to learn multi-level visual representations consistent with hierarchical cortical processing, enhancing subject-invariant encoding. These modules are jointly optimized via Multi-level Bidirectional Contrastive Learning, which aligns EEG and visual features in a shared semantic space through bidirectional contrastive objectives. Experiments show MB2L achieves 80.5% Top-1 and 97.6% Top-5 accuracy on zero-shot EEG-to-image retrieval, significantly outperforming prior methods and demonstrating strong generalization across subjects and experimental settings.

AI.tldr: MB2L introduces a biomimetic framework with adaptive blur, hierarchical visual feature extraction, and bidirectional contrastive learning to achieve superior EEG-to-image retrieval by bridging the gap between digital images and biological visual perception.
AI.motivation: Limited paired data and fundamental mismatch between high-fidelity digital images and biological visual perception (distorted by retinotopic mapping and subject-specific neuroanatomy) severely impede cross-modal alignment in EEG-based visual neural decoding.
AI.method: Proposes MB2L framework with three components: (1) Adaptive Blur with Visual Priors to mitigate perceptual-structural mismatch via retinotopic reweighting, (2) Biomimetic Visual Feature Extraction for hierarchical cortical-aligned representations, (3) Multi-level Bidirectional Contrastive Learning for joint EEG-visual alignment.
AI.result: 80.5% Top-1 and 97.6% Top-5 accuracy on zero-shot EEG-to-image retrieval, outperforming prior methods with strong generalization.
AI.conclusion: MB2L achieves 80.5% Top-1 and 97.6% Top-5 accuracy on zero-shot EEG-to-image retrieval, significantly outperforming prior methods with strong generalization across subjects and settings.

title: Gradient Flow Structure and Quantitative Dynamics of Multi-Head Self-Attention
summary:
Transformer self-attention can be interpreted as a gradient flow on the unit sphere, in which tokens evolve under softmax interaction potentials and tend to form clusters. While prior work has established clustering behavior for single-head attention, the multi-head setting remains less understood due to geometric interference between heads, which invalidates standard monotonicity arguments.
  In this work, we develop a theoretical framework for multi-head self-attention dynamics and resolve several open questions. We show that, under suitable conditions on the score matrices, a natural multi-head energy functional is non-decreasing along both flat and spherical dynamics. We identify the key obstruction to per-head monotonicity as radial shadow terms, which are projections of each head's output onto token directions, persisting even under orthogonality assumptions. We introduce a sufficient condition ensuring monotonicity and establish robustness to approximate orthogonality.
  In a simplified scalar-head regime with equiangular token configurations, we derive a closed-form expression for the critical inverse temperature governing clustering behavior, and show that heterogeneous heads exhibit super-additive clustering rates. In this regime, we also prove a separation in clustering time between ReLU and softmax attention in the linearized dynamics. Finally, we establish an entropy production identity and show that attention entropy increases monotonically toward equilibrium as clustering progresses.
  Our results provide a unified perspective on the dynamics of multi-head attention and clarify the mechanisms underlying clustering and stability in transformer models.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a theoretical framework for multi-head self-attention dynamics, showing that a multi-head energy functional is non-decreasing under certain conditions, identifying radial shadow terms as the key obstruction to per-head monotonicity, deriving closed-form critical temperatures for clustering in scalar-head regimes, and establishing entropy production identities.
AI.motivation: While single-head attention clustering behavior is established, multi-head attention remains poorly understood due to geometric interference between heads that invalidates standard monotonicity arguments. The authors aim to resolve open questions about multi-head self-attention dynamics.
AI.method: The authors develop a theoretical framework for multi-head self-attention dynamics, analyzing both flat and spherical dynamics. They identify radial shadow terms as obstructions to monotonicity, derive sufficient conditions for monotonicity, analyze scalar-head regimes with equiangular token configurations, and establish entropy production identities.
AI.result: Multi-head energy functional is non-decreasing under suitable score matrix conditions. Radial shadow terms persist even under orthogonality assumptions. In scalar-head regimes, closed-form critical inverse temperature is derived, heterogeneous heads show super-additive clustering rates, and a separation in clustering time between ReLU and softmax attention is proven. Attention entropy increases monotonically toward equilibrium.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a unified theoretical perspective on multi-head attention dynamics, clarifying clustering and stability mechanisms in transformer models.

title: FaithfulFaces: Pose-Faithful Facial Identity Preservation for Text-to-Video Generation
summary: Identity-preserving text-to-video generation (IPT2V) empowers users to produce diverse and imaginative videos with consistent human facial identity. Despite recent progress, existing methods often suffer from significant identity distortion under large facial pose variations or facial occlusions. In this paper, we propose \textit{FaithfulFaces}, a pose-faithful facial identity preservation learning framework to improve IPT2V in complex dynamic scenes. The key of FaithfulFaces is a pose-shared identity aligner that refines and aligns facial poses across distinct views via a pose-shared dictionary and a pose variation-identity invariance constraint. By mapping single-view inputs into a global facial pose representation with explicit Euler angle embeddings, FaithfulFaces provides a pose-faithful facial prior that guides generative foundations toward robust identity-preserving generation. In particular, we develop a specialized pipeline to curate a high-quality video dataset featuring substantial facial pose diversity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FaithfulFaces achieves state-of-the-art performance, maintaining superior identity consistency and structural clarity even as pose changes and occlusions occur.

AI.tldr: FaithfulFaces proposes a pose-shared identity aligner with Euler angle embeddings to achieve robust identity-preserving text-to-video generation under large pose variations and occlusions, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing identity-preserving text-to-video generation (IPT2V) methods suffer from significant identity distortion under large facial pose variations or facial occlusions.
AI.method: A pose-shared identity aligner with a pose-shared dictionary and pose variation-identity invariance constraint; uses explicit Euler angle embeddings to map single-view inputs into a global facial pose representation; includes a specialized pipeline for curating a high-quality video dataset with facial pose diversity.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance, maintaining superior identity consistency and structural clarity under pose changes and occlusions.
AI.conclusion: FaithfulFaces effectively improves IPT2V in complex dynamic scenes by providing a pose-faithful facial prior for robust identity-preserving generation.

title: Hardware-Aware Neural Feature Extraction for Resource-Constrained Devices
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication at the IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) Workshops, 2026. \c{opyright}IEEE
summary: Visual SLAM is a core component of spatial computing systems, yet deploying learned local feature extractors on microcontroller-class hardware remains challenging due to memory, bandwidth, and quantization constraints. While modern neural descriptors provide strong robustness, their practical adoption is often hindered by system-level bottlenecks that are not captured by FLOP-based efficiency metrics. In this work, we introduce Gideon, a hardware-aware neural feature extractor explicitly designed for resource-constrained devices. Our approach combines relational knowledge distillation from a SuperPoint teacher with differentiable neural architecture search (DNAS) under strict memory and operator constraints. Unlike conventional design pipelines, we treat quantization stability and dynamic-range compactness as first-class objectives. We show that architectural choices such as replacing Batch Normalization with affine layers significantly improve INT8 robustness, and that descriptor dimensionality directly governs quantization resilience. Deployed on STM32N6, Gideon achieves 9.003 ms inference time (111 fps) while remaining below a 1.5 MB memory footprint. Remarkably, INT8 quantization induces negligible degradation and occasionally matches full-precision performance. These results demonstrate that robust learned feature extraction can be reconciled with embedded hardware constraints through holistic hardware-algorithm co-design.

AI.tldr: Gideon is a hardware-aware neural feature extractor for resource-constrained devices, combining knowledge distillation and DNAS to achieve 111 fps on STM32N6 with INT8 quantization and under 1.5 MB memory.
AI.motivation: Deploying learned local feature extractors on microcontroller-class hardware is challenging due to memory, bandwidth, and quantization constraints, and FLOP-based metrics fail to capture real system-level bottlenecks.
AI.method: Combines relational knowledge distillation from a SuperPoint teacher with differentiable neural architecture search (DNAS) under strict memory and operator constraints, treating quantization stability and dynamic-range compactness as first-class objectives. Replaces Batch Normalization with affine layers for INT8 robustness.
AI.result: Deployed on STM32N6, Gideon achieves 9.003 ms inference time (111 fps) with under 1.5 MB memory. INT8 quantization shows negligible degradation, occasionally matching full-precision performance.
AI.conclusion: Robust learned feature extraction can be reconciled with embedded hardware constraints through holistic hardware-algorithm co-design.

title: Not Every Subject Should Stay: Machine Unlearning for Noisy Engagement Recognition
summary: Engagement recognition datasets are typically subject-indexed and often contain noisy, subjective supervision, making post-hoc dataset revision a practical problem. Existing noisy-label and data-cleaning methods largely operate at the sample level before or during training, but do not directly address a different question: once a model has already been trained, can the influence of an entire problematic subject be removed without full retraining? We study this setting through subject-level machine unlearning as a post-hoc sanitization mechanism for engagement recognition. Starting from a baseline trained on all subjects, we rank candidate harmful subjects using a model-dependent proxy, apply a lightweight approximate unlearning update, and compare the result against an oracle model retrained from scratch on the retained subjects only. We instantiate this protocol on DAiSEE and EngageNet using Tensor-Convolution and Convolution-Transformer Network (TCCT-Net) as a fixed platform and evaluate three matched model states under the same removal scenario: baseline, unlearned, and oracle. In representative K=3 forget-set settings, the unlearned model recovers 89.3% and 92.5% of the oracle gain on EngageNet and DAiSEE, respectively, at roughly one quarter of retraining cost. Across the tested small-audit regimes, effectiveness is strongest at an intermediate forget-set size, indicating that approximate subject-level unlearning is a useful low-cost correction mechanism, but one whose benefit depends on subject selection quality and removal regime.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes subject-level machine unlearning for engagement recognition, where entire problematic subjects can be removed post-hoc without full retraining. Using DAiSEE and EngageNet datasets with TCCT-Net, the unlearned model recovers ~89-93% of oracle retraining gain at ~25% of the cost.
