AI.tldr: The paper studies online prediction for marginally stable linear dynamical systems under nonstochastic disturbances, proposing an unconstrained online least squares method with stabilizing hints that achieves logarithmic regret even with unbounded trajectories, and extends to model-free settings for symmetric systems.
AI.motivation: Standard online learning methods rely on bounded domains/gradients, which fail to handle potentially unbounded trajectories in marginally stable systems. The goal is to minimize cumulative squared prediction loss and compete with the best-in-hindsight Luenberger predictor under nonstochastic disturbances.
AI.method: An unconstrained online least squares method is introduced that uses tailored predictive hints to stabilize the learning process. With model knowledge, hints from any stabilizing Luenberger predictor ensure uniformly bounded hint residuals. For model-free settings, a simple universal hint for symmetric systems is proposed.
AI.result: The method achieves logarithmic regret despite unbounded trajectory growth when model knowledge is available. For symmetric systems, logarithmic regret is maintained without model knowledge using the universal hint.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides an adaptive, instance-wise optimal online predictor that outperforms classical fixed-gain observers under nonstochastic disturbances.

title: Demystifying Manifold Constraints in LLM Pre-training
summary: The empirical success of large language model (LLM) pre-training relies heavily on heuristic stabilization techniques, such as explicit normalization layers and weight decay. While recent constrained optimization approaches that explicitly restrict weights may improve numerical stability and performance, the mechanism and motivation for adding constraints still remain elusive. This paper systematically demystifies the role of explicit manifold constraints in LLM pre-training. By introducing the Msign-Aligned Constrained Riemannian Optimizer (MACRO)-a provably convergent, single-loop optimization framework-our study disentangles weight regularization heuristics from interacting mechanisms like RMS normalization and decoupled weight decay. Theoretical analyses and comprehensive empirical evaluations reveal that manifold constraints independently bound forward activation scales and enforce stable rotational equilibrium, thereby subsuming the roles of these heuristic mechanisms. Evaluations on large-scale LLM architectures demonstrate that MACRO achieves highly competitive performance while rigorously preserving the theoretical guarantees of exact Riemannian optimization.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MACRO, a constrained Riemannian optimization framework for LLM pre-training that uses manifold constraints to replace traditional heuristic stabilization techniques like normalization layers and weight decay, achieving competitive performance with theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: To demystify the role of explicit manifold constraints in LLM pre-training and disentangle weight regularization heuristics from interacting mechanisms like RMS normalization and decoupled weight decay.
AI.method: Proposes MACRO (Msign-Aligned Constrained Riemannian Optimizer), a provably convergent single-loop optimization framework that applies manifold constraints to bound forward activation scales and enforce rotational equilibrium.
AI.result: Manifold constraints independently bound forward activation scales and enforce stable rotational equilibrium, subsuming the roles of heuristic mechanisms like RMS normalization and decoupled weight decay. MACRO achieves competitive performance on large-scale LLM architectures.
AI.conclusion: Explicit manifold constraints can systematically replace heuristic stabilization techniques in LLM pre-training while providing theoretical guarantees of exact Riemannian optimization.

title: ICPR 2026 Competition on Privacy-Preserving Person Re-Identification from Top-View RGB-Depth Camera (TVRID)
summary: This companion paper reports the ICPR 2026 TVRID competition on privacy-aware top-view person re-identification. We present the competition setting, the released RGB-Depth dataset, and a summary of final results with descriptions of the top entries. TVRID contains 86 identities captured by four synchronized overhead Intel RealSense D455 cameras, with paired RGB/Depth streams and structured geometric variation across flat, ascent, descent, and oblique viewpoints. The evaluation protocol includes three tracks: RGB Re-ID, Depth Re-ID, and RGB$\leftrightarrow$Depth cross-modal retrieval. Submissions are ranked using mAP and CMC-1 under a unified server-side evaluation. The final results show a clear difficulty ordering (RGB $>$ Depth $>$ Cross-Modal), highlighting both the challenge of modality-constrained retrieval and the feasibility of strong performance with modality-invariant learning. By releasing the dataset at https://zenodo.org/records/17909410, the evaluation scripts at https://github.com/RaphaelDel/ICPR-TVRID, and the accompanying documentation, TVRID establishes a reproducible benchmark for top-view, depth-based, and cross-modal person re-id.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the ICPR 2026 TVRID competition on privacy-aware top-view person re-identification, introducing a new RGB-Depth dataset with 86 identities from four overhead cameras. The competition includes three tracks (RGB Re-ID, Depth Re-ID, and cross-modal retrieval), with results showing RGB > Depth > Cross-Modal in difficulty. The dataset and tools are publicly released as a reproducible benchmark.
AI.motivation: To address the need for privacy-aware person re-identification in top-view surveillance scenarios, where traditional frontal/rear-view Re-ID methods are insufficient and privacy concerns limit RGB-only approaches.
AI.method: The authors organized the TVRID competition using a new RGB-Depth dataset captured by four synchronized overhead Intel RealSense D455 cameras with varying geometric viewpoints (flat, ascent, descent, oblique). The evaluation followed three tracks: RGB Re-ID, Depth Re-ID, and RGB↔Depth cross-modal retrieval, ranked by mAP and CMC-1 through unified server-side evaluation.
AI.result: The difficulty ordering across tracks was RGB > Depth > Cross-Modal retrieval. The results demonstrate that modality-constrained retrieval remains challenging, while modality-invariant learning approaches can achieve strong performance.
AI.conclusion: TVRID establishes a reproducible benchmark for top-view, depth-based, and cross-modal person re-identification, with public dataset, evaluation scripts, and documentation available for future research.

title: FLUID: Continuous-Time Hyperconnected Sparse Transformer for Sink-Free Learning
summary: Continuous-time (CT) Transformers improve irregular and long-range modeling over CT-RNNs by exploiting inputs or outputs embeddings with continuous dynamics. However, the core scaled-dot-product-attention (SDPA) mechanism remains inherently discrete. We propose FLUID (Flexible Unified Information Dynamics), a CT Transformer that incorporates continuous dynamics directly into the attention computation by replacing it with Liquid Attention Network (LAN). LAN reinterprets attention logits as continuous dynamical system and reformulates them as the solution to a linear ODE modulated by input-dependent nonlinear recurrent gates. Theoretically, we establish stability guarantees for LAN dynamics and show that it serves as an interpolating middle ground between SDPA and CT-RNNs, recovering each as special case under well-defined parameterization of its gating functions. LAN also introduces an explicit attention-sink gate to eliminate disproportionate attention mass on uninformative nodes. FLUID replaces standard residual connections with input-dependent Liquid Hyper-Connections to adaptively regulate interlayer information flow. Empirically, we evaluate FLUID on a broad set of learning tasks, including (i) irregular time-series, (ii) long-range modeling, (iii) lane-keeping control of autonomous vehicles, and (iv) learning physical dynamics under a scarce data regime. Across all the tasks, FLUID consistently matches or outperforms CT baselines, achieving improvements of up to 47% in certain scenarios and enhancing generalization under distributional shifts. Additionally, FLUID demonstrates superior noise robustness and a self-correcting inductive bias in autonomous vehicle control. We also provide a detailed analysis of key hyperparameters to guide tuning and show that FLUID occupies an intermediate position among competing approaches in terms of runtime and memory efficiency.

AI.tldr: FLUID is a Continuous-Time Transformer that replaces standard attention with Liquid Attention Networks (LAN)—solving an ODE to compute attention logits—and uses Liquid Hyper-Connections for adaptive residual connections. It outperforms CT baselines on irregular time-series, long-range modeling, autonomous driving, and physics modeling, with up to 47% improvement and better noise robustness.
AI.motivation: Standard CT Transformers still use discrete Scaled-Dot-Product Attention (SDPA), creating a mismatch with otherwise continuous dynamics. The authors aim to unify the architecture by making attention itself continuous via ODE-based computation.
AI.method: Replaces SDPA with Liquid Attention Network (LAN), which reinterprets attention logits as a linear ODE solution modulated by input-dependent nonlinear recurrent gates. Also replaces residual connections with Liquid Hyper-Connections for adaptive interlayer information flow. Includes an attention-sink gate to reduce uninformative attention.
AI.result: FLUID matches or outperforms CT baselines across all tasks (irregular time-series, long-range modeling, lane-keeping control, physical dynamics), achieving up to 47% improvement. Shows superior noise robustness, self-correcting inductive bias for autonomous vehicles, and intermediate runtime/memory efficiency.
AI.conclusion: FLUID bridges the gap between SDPA and CT-RNNs by recovering each as a special case, demonstrating that continuous attention dynamics improve performance, generalization under distribution shifts, and robustness.

title: Attention-Based Chaotic Self-Supervision for Medical Image Classification
summary: Deep learning models for medical image classification usually achieve promising results but typically rely on large, annotated datasets or standard transfer learning from ImageNet. Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) has emerged as a powerful alternative, yet common methods like masked autoencoders (MAEs) may inadvertently destroy fine-grained diagnostic features by using random masking. In this paper, we propose a novel SSL pre-training strategy, the Chaotic Denoising Autoencoder (CDAE). Instead of masking, we apply a chaotic transformation to the input image, tasking an autoencoder to reconstruct the original. We hypothesize this forces the encoder to learn robust, domain-specific features by "inverting the chaos". Furthermore, we propose an attentive fusion mechanism that combines features from our CDAE-trained encoder with a standard encoder, leveraging the strengths of both general and domain-specific representations. Our method is evaluated on two public medical datasets: ISIC 2018 (skin lesions) and APTOS 2019 (diabetic retinopathy). The proposed model achieves high performance, with an accuracy of 0.9221 and an F1-macro of 0.8530 on ISIC 2018, and an accuracy of 0.8644 and F1-macro of 0.7433 on APTOS 2019, demonstrating the efficacy of our approach.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Chaotic Denoising Autoencoder (CDAE), an SSL pre-training method that uses chaotic transformations instead of masking to reconstruct images, combined with an attentive fusion mechanism to blend domain-specific and general features, achieving strong results on ISIC 2018 and APTOS 2019.
AI.motivation: Self-supervised learning methods like MAEs use random masking that may destroy fine-grained diagnostic features in medical images, motivating the need for a pre-training strategy that preserves critical details.
AI.method: CDAE applies chaotic transformation to input images and tasks an autoencoder to reconstruct the original, forcing the encoder to learn robust features by inverting the chaos. An attentive fusion mechanism combines CDAE-trained encoder features with a standard encoder.
AI.result: On ISIC 2018: accuracy 0.9221, F1-macro 0.8530. On APTOS 2019: accuracy 0.8644, F1-macro 0.7433.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CDAE approach with attentive fusion effectively leverages domain-specific and general representations, outperforming standard SSL methods for medical image classification.

title: $p$-adic Manifold Learning and Benchmark Tasks from Impartial Games
summary: We introduce $p$-adic manifold learning, propose an algorithm to solve it, and propose benchmark tasks from impartial games.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces p-adic manifold learning, proposes an algorithm to solve it, and creates benchmark tasks from impartial games.
AI.motivation: To explore manifold learning in the p-adic metric space, moving beyond traditional Euclidean settings.
AI.method: The authors develop an algorithm designed for p-adic manifold learning, along with benchmark tasks based on impartial games.
AI.result: A new algorithm for p-adic manifold learning and novel benchmark tasks from impartial games are presented.
AI.conclusion: P-adic manifold learning is a viable area with algorithmic solutions and suitable benchmarks from impartial games.

title: Low-Rank Adaptation of Geospatial Foundation Models for Wildfire Mapping Using Sentinel-2 Data
summary: Wildfire burned-area mapping is essential for damage assessment, emissions modeling, and understanding fire-climate interactions across diverse ecological regions. Recent geospatial foundation models provide strong general-purpose representations for satellite imagery, yet there is still no clear understanding of how to efficiently adapt these models for downstream Earth observation tasks, particularly under geographic and temporal domain shift. This study evaluates three state-of-the-art Geospatial Foundation Models (GFMs) - Terramind, DINOv3, and Prithvi-v2 - for burned-area mapping across the United States and Canada using Sentinel-2 data. Leveraging 3,820 wildfire events from 2017-2023, we conduct spatial and temporal generalization tests across diverse biomes. We systematically compare full fine-tuning, decoder-only fine-tuning, and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for adapting each model. Across all experiments, LoRA provides the strongest cross-domain generalization while updating less than 1% of parameters, demonstrating a favorable trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. Prithvi-v2 with LoRA achieves the highest overall accuracy and the largest improvement compared to full fine-tuning. These findings indicate that geospatial foundation models, when adapted using lightweight parameter-efficient methods such as LoRA, offer a robust and scalable solution for large-scale burned-area mapping. Code is available at https://github.com/alishibli97/wildfire-lora-gfm.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates three Geospatial Foundation Models (Terramind, DINOv3, Prithvi-v2) for burned-area mapping across the US and Canada using Sentinel-2 data, finding that LoRA (updating <1% parameters) provides the best cross-domain generalization, with Prithvi-v2 + LoRA achieving the highest accuracy.
AI.motivation: Burned-area mapping is critical for damage assessment, emissions modeling, and understanding fire-climate interactions. However, there is a lack of clarity on how to efficiently adapt geospatial foundation models for downstream tasks under geographic and temporal domain shifts.
AI.method: The study evaluates three GFMs (Terramind, DINOv3, Prithvi-v2) on 3,820 wildfire events (2017-2023) using Sentinel-2 data. It systematically compares full fine-tuning, decoder-only fine-tuning, and LoRA across spatial and temporal generalization tests in diverse biomes across the US and Canada.
AI.result: LoRA provides the strongest cross-domain generalization while updating less than 1% of parameters. Prithvi-v2 with LoRA achieves the highest overall accuracy and the largest improvement compared to full fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Geospatial foundation models adapted with lightweight parameter-efficient methods like LoRA offer a robust and scalable solution for large-scale burned-area mapping.

title: GraphPI: Efficient Protein Inference with Graph Neural Networks
summary: The integration of deep learning approaches in biomedical research has been transformative, enabling breakthroughs in various applications. Despite these strides, its application in protein inference is impeded by the scarcity of extensively labeled datasets, a challenge compounded by the high costs and complexities of accurate protein annotation. In this study, we introduce GraphPI, a novel framework that treats protein inference as a node classification problem. We treat proteins as interconnected nodes within a protein-peptide-PSM graph, utilizing a Graph Neural Network-based architecture to elucidate their interrelations. To address label scarcity, we train the model on a set of unlabeled public protein datasets with pseudo-labels derived from an existing protein inference algorithm, enhanced by self-training to iteratively refine labels based on confidence scores. Contrary to prevalent methodologies necessitating dataset-specific training, our research illustrates that GraphPI, due to the well normalized nature of Percolator features, exhibits universal applicability without dataset-specific fine-tuning, a feature that not only mitigates the risk of overfitting but also enhances computational efficiency. Our empirical experiments reveal notable performance on various test datasets and deliver significantly reduced computation times compared to common protein inference algorithms.

AI.tldr: GraphPI is a Graph Neural Network-based framework for protein inference that uses pseudo-labeling and self-training to overcome label scarcity, achieving universal applicability across datasets without fine-tuning while reducing computation time.
AI.motivation: Deep learning in protein inference is hindered by scarce labeled data due to high costs and complexity of accurate protein annotation; existing methods require dataset-specific training which is inefficient.
AI.method: GraphPI treats protein inference as node classification on a protein-peptide-PSM graph using GNNs, trained on unlabeled public datasets with pseudo-labels from existing algorithms and refined via self-training with confidence scores.
AI.result: Notable performance across various test datasets with significantly reduced computation times compared to common protein inference algorithms, without requiring dataset-specific fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: GraphPI demonstrates universal applicability, mitigates overfitting risk, and enhances computational efficiency by leveraging normalized Percolator features and self-training.

title: Stabilizing LLM Supervised Fine-Tuning via Explicit Distributional Control
summary: Post-training large language models (LLMs) often suffers from catastrophic forgetting, where improvements on a target objective degrade previously acquired capabilities. Recent evidence suggests that this phenomenon is primarily driven by excessive distributional drift during optimization. Motivated by this perspective, we propose Anchored Learning, a simple framework that explicitly controls distributional updates during offline fine-tuning via a dynamically evolving moving anchor. Instead of matching a fixed reference distribution, the anchor interpolates between the current model and a frozen reference to construct an intermediate target that the model distills toward, transforming global fine-tuning into a sequence of local trust-region updates in distribution space. Theoretically, we prove this anchor-based update admits a linear KL-divergence upper bound per iteration, ensuring a stable transition between model distributions. Extensive experiments on iGSM, MedCalc, and IFEval show that Anchored Learning consistently lies on the Pareto frontier of gain-stability trade-offs, achieving near-optimal performance improvements while substantially reducing degradation compared to strong baselines. For example, while standard SFT suffers from over 53% performance degradation on iGSM and MedCalc, Anchored Learning slashes this drop to under 5% while maintaining near-optimal gains (e.g., 75.2% on iGSM).

AI.tldr: Anchored Learning is a fine-tuning framework that prevents catastrophic forgetting in LLMs by using a dynamically moving anchor to control distributional drift, achieving near-optimal performance gains with under 5% degradation (vs. 53% in standard SFT).
AI.motivation: Post-training LLMs suffers from catastrophic forgetting, where optimizing for a new target degrades previously learned capabilities, primarily due to excessive distributional drift.
AI.method: Proposes Anchored Learning, which uses a dynamically evolving moving anchor that interpolates between the current model and a frozen reference, transforming global fine-tuning into local trust-region updates in distribution space with a linear KL-divergence bound per iteration.
AI.result: On iGSM, MedCalc, and IFEval, Anchored Learning lies on the Pareto frontier of gain-stability trade-offs. It reduces performance degradation from 53% (standard SFT) to under 5% while maintaining near-optimal gains (e.g., 75.2% on iGSM).
AI.conclusion: Explicitly controlling distributional updates via moving anchors effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting while preserving fine-tuning gains, outperforming strong baselines.

title: Chaotic Contrastive Learning for Robust Texture Classification
summary: Texture classification is a pivotal task in computer vision, presenting unique challenges due to high inter-class similarity and the sensitivity of structural patterns to scale and illumination changes. While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and recent Vision Transformers have set performance benchmarks, they often require extensive labeled datasets or struggle to generalize across domains due to an over-reliance on color and shape features. This paper introduces a novel framework that synergizes Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) with deterministic chaotic dynamics. We propose a chaotic contrastive pre-training strategy, where pixel-wise chaotic maps, specifically Logistic, Tent, and Sine maps, act as non-linear data augmentation techniques. These chaotic perturbations, grounded in ergodic theory, force the network to learn topologically robust features by mimicking complex environmental noise and reflectance variations. Furthermore, we introduce an attention-based feature ensemble that fuses high-level semantic representations from a supervised large backbone with low-frequency structural features from a chaos-pretrained tiny encoder. Experimental results on six texture benchmarks (FMD, UMD, KTH-TIPS2-b, DTD, GTOS, and 1200Tex) demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches and achieving promising accuracies on all the analyzed datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a texture classification framework combining Self-Supervised Learning with chaotic dynamics (Logistic, Tent, Sine maps) for data augmentation, plus an attention-based feature ensemble that fuses a large supervised backbone with a chaos-pretrained tiny encoder, achieving SOTA on six texture benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Texture classification is challenging due to high inter-class similarity and sensitivity to scale/illumination changes. Existing CNNs and Vision Transformers require extensive labeled data or over-rely on color/shape features, failing to generalize across domains.
AI.method: (1) Chaotic contrastive pre-training using pixel-wise chaotic maps (Logistic, Tent, Sine) as non-linear data augmentation, grounded in ergodic theory to learn topologically robust features. (2) Attention-based feature ensemble fusing high-level semantics from a supervised large backbone with low-frequency structural features from a chaos-pretrained tiny encoder.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on six texture benchmarks: FMD, UMD, KTH-TIPS2-b, DTD, GTOS, and 1200Tex.
AI.conclusion: The proposed chaos-driven SSL framework effectively addresses texture classification challenges by learning robust, illumination-invariant features, and the hybrid ensemble design balances semantic richness with structural detail retrieval.

title: CARD: A Multi-Modal Automotive Dataset for Dense 3D Reconstruction in Challenging Road Topography
comment: Accepted at CVPR 2026 (Highlight). Project page: https://card.content.cariad.digital
summary: Autonomous driving must operate across diverse surfaces to enable safe mobility. However, most driving datasets are captured on well-paved flat roads. Moreover, recent driving datasets primarily provide sparse LiDAR ground truth for images, which is insufficient for assessing fine-grained geometry in depth estimation and completion. To address these gaps, we introduce CARD, a multi-modal driving dataset that delivers quasi-dense 3D ground truth across continuous sequences rich in speed bumps, potholes, irregular surfaces and off-road segments. Our sensor suite includes synchronized global-shutter stereo cameras, front and rear LiDARs, 6-DoF poses from LiDAR-inertial odometry, per-wheel motion traces, and full calibration. Notably, our multi-LiDAR fusion yields ~500K valid depth pixels per frame, about 6.5x more than KITTI Depth Completion and 10x more on average than other public driving datasets. The dataset spans ~110 km and 4.7 hours across Germany and Italy. In addition, CARD provides 2D bounding boxes targeting road-topography irregularities, enabling accurate benchmarking for both geometry and perception tasks. Furthermore, we establish a standardized evaluation protocol for road surface irregularities on CARD and benchmark state-of-the-art depth estimation models to provide strong baselines. The CARD dataset is hosted on https://huggingface.co/CARD-Data.

AI.tldr: CARD is a new multi-modal driving dataset with quasi-dense 3D ground truth (~500K valid depth pixels per frame, ~6.5x more than KITTI), covering ~110 km across Germany and Italy with speed bumps, potholes, and off-road surfaces, plus 2D boxes for road irregularities and benchmarks for depth estimation.
AI.motivation: Existing driving datasets are mostly captured on flat, well-paved roads and provide only sparse LiDAR ground truth, which is insufficient for evaluating fine-grained geometry in depth estimation and completion, especially on irregular surfaces.
AI.method: CARD uses synchronized global-shutter stereo cameras, front/rear LiDARs, LiDAR-inertial odometry for 6-DoF poses, per-wheel motion traces, and multi-LiDAR fusion to generate quasi-dense depth (~500K valid pixels per frame). It covers ~110 km over 4.7 hours in Germany and Italy, with 2D bounding boxes for road-topography irregularities.
AI.result: The dataset yields ~500K valid depth pixels per frame — about 6.5x more than KITTI Depth Completion and 10x more than other public driving datasets on average. It provides standardized evaluation protocols and baselines for depth estimation on road surface irregularities.
AI.conclusion: CARD fills the gap in driving datasets for irregular surfaces and fine-grained geometry, enabling better benchmarking for depth estimation and perception tasks in autonomous driving on diverse road conditions.

title: Beyond Rigid Geometries: The Spline-Pullback Metric for Universal Diffeomorphic SPD Representation Learning
summary: The integration of Symmetric Positive Definite (SPD) matrices into deep learning has historically relied on fixed algebraic Riemannian metrics. Analogous to hand-crafted features in classical machine learning, these static formulations impose rigid geometries limiting network expressivity and adaptability. Recent attempts to parameterize these geometries often violate the axioms of primary matrix functions through unconstrained powers or rank-dependent scaling, inviting spatial folding, loss of global surjectivity, and gradient collapse at spectral singularities. In this paper, we introduce the Spline-Pullback Metric (SPM), instantiated as Spectral-SPM and Cholesky-SPM, marking a paradigm shift from static metric selection to universal geometric approximation. By parameterizing the global diffeomorphism via a rank-invariant, monotonically constrained B-spline, SPM acts as a dense universal approximator for strictly increasing $C^1$ diffeomorphisms and theoretically subsumes existing pullback metrics while enabling localized non-linear spectral modelling. Topologically, SPM provides a globally bijective pullback geometry precluding rank-swapping discontinuities and gradient instabilities. Empirically, SPM achieves a state-of-the-art performance across 3 datasets utilizing Linear Probes, SPDNets, and deep Riemannian ResNets.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Spline-Pullback Metric (SPM), a universal geometric approximation method for SPD matrices that uses rank-invariant B-spline parameterization, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing Riemannian metrics for SPD matrices are static and rigid, limiting deep learning expressivity. Previous attempts to parameterize these metrics violate mathematical axioms, causing issues like spatial folding, loss of surjectivity, and gradient collapse.
AI.method: SPM (Spectral-SPM & Cholesky-SPM) parameterizes a global diffeomorphism via rank-invariant, monotonically constrained B-spline, acting as a universal approximator for strictly increasing C¹ diffeomorphisms while maintaining topological bijectivity.
AI.result: SPM achieves state-of-the-art performance across 3 datasets using Linear Probes, SPDNets, and deep Riemannian ResNets, while preventing rank-swapping discontinuities and gradient instabilities.
AI.conclusion: SPM marks a paradigm shift from static metric selection to universal geometric approximation, theoretically subsuming existing pullback metrics with superior stability and expressivity.

title: Local Intrinsic Dimension Unveils Hallucinations in Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models are prone to generating structural hallucinations - samples that match the statistical properties of the training data yet defy underlying structural rules, resulting in anomalies like hands with more than five fingers. Recent research studied this failure mode from several viewpoints, offering partial explanations to their occurrence, such as mode interpolation. In this work, we propose a complementary perspective that treats hallucinations as instabilities on the model-induced manifold. We begin by showing that a hallucination filter based on such instabilities matches or exceeds the performance of the recently proposed temporal one. By tracing the source of these instabilities, we identify local intrinsic dimension (LID) as their primary driver and propose Intrinsic Quenching (IQ), a direct corrective mechanism that deflates it to alleviate hallucinations. IQ consistently outperforms standard hallucination reduction baselines across a wide array of benchmarks and offers a highly promising solution for enforcing anatomical consistency in downstream medical imaging tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies structural hallucinations in diffusion models as instabilities on the model-induced manifold, proposes local intrinsic dimension (LID) as the key driver, and introduces Intrinsic Quenching (IQ) to deflate LID and reduce hallucinations, outperforming existing baselines.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models produce structural hallucinations (e.g., hands with extra fingers) that match training data statistics but violate structural rules. Existing explanations (e.g., mode interpolation) are only partial, motivating a complementary perspective.
AI.method: The authors view hallucinations as instabilities on the model-induced manifold, show that an instability-based hallucination filter is competitive with temporal filtering, identify local intrinsic dimension (LID) as the primary instability source, and propose Intrinsic Quenching (IQ) to directly deflate LID.
AI.result: IQ consistently outperforms standard hallucination reduction baselines across a wide array of benchmarks and shows promise for enforcing anatomical consistency in downstream medical imaging tasks.
AI.conclusion: The instability-based perspective provides a complementary explanation for hallucinations in diffusion models, and IQ offers an effective corrective mechanism with potential applications in medical imaging.

title: Counterfactual identifiability beyond global monotonicity: non-monotone triangular structural causal models
summary: Structural causal models provide a unified semantics for interventions and counterfactuals, but most identifiability results rely on restrictive assumptions like global monotonicity, which are often violated in embodied interaction, where the same exogenous perturbation can induce opposite responses under different contact contexts. We ask what structure still suffices once global monotonicity is dropped. We introduce non-monotone triangular structural causal models (NM-TM-SCM), which retain triangular recursion but replace global monotonicity with mechanism-wise invertibility and context-independent inverse transport. We prove that these conditions are equivalent to exogenous isomorphism and imply complete counterfactual identifiability, and we give a counterexample showing that local invertibility alone is insufficient. We instantiate the theory in CausalInverter, with triangular invertible layers, orientation gates, and transport-stability regularization. On synthetic non-monotonic mechanisms, the structural bias yields systematic counterfactual gains as non-monotonicity increases. On MuJoCo Door, our model achieves perfect event-level counterfactual recovery, lowers continuous angle error relative to a Transformer baseline, and delivers substantially more stable recovery than Transformer and conditional-flow predictors. On MuJoCo Push, where non-monotonicity is weaker, the same low-data predictors remain competitive or better, consistent with a bias-variance boundary. These results identify a broader identifiable regime between globally monotone triangular models and unconstrained black-box world models.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces non-monotone triangular structural causal models (NM-TM-SCM) that replace global monotonicity with mechanism-wise invertibility and context-independent inverse transport, proving these conditions enable complete counterfactual identifiability. The authors implement CausalInverter and demonstrate superior counterfactual recovery on synthetic and robotic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Global monotonicity assumptions are often violated in embodied interaction where the same exogenous perturbation can cause opposite responses under different contact contexts. The authors ask what structure still suffices for identifiability once global monotonicity is dropped.
AI.method: The paper proposes non-monotone triangular structural causal models (NM-TM-SCM) with mechanism-wise invertibility and context-independent inverse transport, proving equivalence to exogenous isomorphism and complete counterfactual identifiability. They instantiate the theory in CausalInverter using triangular invertible layers, orientation gates, and transport-stability regularization.
AI.result: On synthetic data, structural bias yields systematic counterfactual gains as non-monotonicity increases. On MuJoCo Door, CausalInverter achieves perfect event-level counterfactual recovery, lower continuous angle error vs Transformer, and more stable recovery. On MuJoCo Push (weaker non-monotonicity), low-data predictors remain competitive, consistent with bias-variance tradeoff.
AI.conclusion: The results identify a broader identifiable regime between globally monotone triangular models and unconstrained black-box world models, showing that mechanism-wise invertibility with context-independent inverse transport is sufficient for counterfactual identifiability without global monotonicity.

title: Prompt-Anchored Vision-Text Distillation for Lifelong Person Re-identification
summary: Lifelong person re-identification (LReID) aims to train a generalizable model with sequentially collected data. However, such models often suffer from semantic drift, limited adaptability, and catastrophic forgetting as new domains emerge. Existing exemplar-free approaches largely rely on visual-only distillation or parameter regularization, while overlooking the potential of auxiliary modalities, such as text, to preserve semantic stability and enable incremental plasticity. We observe that the frozen text encoder in pretrained vision-language models can serve as a stable semantic anchor across domains. To decouple the roles of vision and text, we propose Prompt-Anchored vision-text Distillation (PAD), an asymmetric vision-text framework for semantic alignment and cross-domain generalization. On the textual side, we distill prompts to preserve vision-text alignment under a fixed semantic space, acting as a global semantic reference rather than a dominant learning signal. On the visual side, an EMA-based teacher with an adaptive prompt pool enables domain-wise adaptation by allocating new slots while freezing past ones. Extensive experiments show that PAD substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods across seen and unseen domains, achieving a strong balance between stability and plasticity. Project page is available at https://github.com/zu-zi/PAD.

AI.tldr: PAD proposes an asymmetric vision-text distillation framework using frozen text encoders as semantic anchors to address catastrophic forgetting and domain adaptation in lifelong person re-identification.
AI.motivation: Existing exemplar-free LReID methods suffer from semantic drift, limited adaptability, and catastrophic forgetting because they rely only on visual cues, ignoring the potential of auxiliary modalities like text to preserve semantic stability.
AI.method: PAD uses a frozen text encoder from pretrained VLMs as a stable semantic anchor. It distills prompts on the textual side for vision-text alignment under a fixed semantic space, and uses an EMA-based teacher with an adaptive prompt pool on the visual side for domain-wise adaptation.
AI.result: PAD substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods across both seen and unseen domains, achieving a strong balance between stability (avoiding forgetting) and plasticity (adapting to new domains).
AI.conclusion: Leveraging frozen text encoders as semantic anchors in a vision-text distillation framework effectively decouples stability and plasticity, providing a new paradigm for exemplar-free lifelong person re-identification.

title: Computer-Aided Design Generation by Cascaded Discrete Diffusion Model
summary: Recent deep learning approaches seek to automate CAD creation by representing a model as a sequence of discrete commands and parameters, and then generating them using autoregressive models or continuous diffusion operating in Euclidean embedding space. However, continuous diffusion perturbs representations in a continuous Euclidean domain that does not reflect the inherently discrete and heterogeneous nature of CAD tokens, often producing perturbed representations that map to semantically invalid symbols. To overcome this limitation, we propose a cascaded discrete diffusion framework for CAD generation, which consists of a command diffusion for generating CAD commands and a parameter diffusion conditioned on CAD commands. Unlike isotropic Gaussian perturbation, the forward process of our approach operates directly over categorical token distributions using delicate transition matrices. For commands, we adopt an absorbing-state transition matrix that progressively corrupts tokens to a designated symbol; for parameters, we introduce specific transition matrices tailored to heterogeneous attributes: a Gaussian kernel for coordinate continuity, a scale-invariant kernel for dimensional values, and a prior-preserving kernel for boolean attributes. The reverse process is achieved by two denoising networks: a Transformer-based encoder for command recovery, and a parameter network with extra local self-attention for command-level interaction and cross-attention for conditional injection. Experiments on the DeepCAD dataset show that the proposed approach surpasses existing autoregressive and continuous diffusion models on unconditional generation metrics, while qualitative results validate effective controllability in conditional generation tasks. Source codes will be released.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a cascaded discrete diffusion framework for CAD generation that operates directly over categorical token distributions using specialized transition matrices, outperforming existing autoregressive and continuous diffusion models on the DeepCAD dataset.
AI.motivation: Continuous diffusion models perturb CAD representations in a continuous Euclidean domain that does not reflect the discrete and heterogeneous nature of CAD tokens, often producing semantically invalid symbols.
AI.method: A cascaded discrete diffusion framework with (1) command diffusion using absorbing-state transition matrices, and (2) parameter diffusion with tailored transition matrices (Gaussian kernel for coordinates, scale-invariant kernel for dimensions, prior-preserving kernel for booleans). Reverse process uses Transformer-based encoder for commands and parameter network with local self-attention + cross-attention.
AI.result: Surpasses autoregressive and continuous diffusion models on unconditional generation metrics; effective controllability in conditional generation tasks on DeepCAD dataset.
AI.conclusion: Discrete diffusion tailored to CAD's discrete and heterogeneous nature is more effective than continuous diffusion for CAD generation.

title: Few-Shot Learning Pipeline for Monkeypox Skin Disease Classification Using CNN Feature Extractors
summary: Despite the strong performance of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in disease classification, their effectiveness often depends on access to large annotated datasets, which is an impractical requirement for emerging or rare conditions such as Monkeypox. To overcome this limitation, we propose a few-shot learning (FSL) framework that employs SimpleShot, a lightweight, non-parametric, inductive classifier, for Monkeypox and pox-like skin disease recognition from limited labeled examples. The proposed pipeline passes the skin lesion images through a frozen, pretrained CNN backbone to obtain feature embeddings, which are then classified via SimpleShot using nearest-centroid comparisons in a normalized embedding space. We systematically benchmark six widely used CNN backbones as feature extractors under consistent experimental settings, enabling fair comparison. Experiments on three publicly available datasets (MSLD v1.0, MSID, and MSLD v2.0) are conducted across 2-way, 4-way, and 6-way tasks with 1-shot, 5-shot, and 10-shot configurations. Among all models, MobileNetV2_100 consistently achieves the highest accuracy. In addition, we present a cross-dataset evaluation for Monkeypox classification, revealing that binary Mpox-vs-Others transfer remains comparatively stable while multi-class performance degrades significantly under domain shift. Together, these results demonstrate the practical utility of combining inductive FSL methods with lightweight CNN backbones and highlight the importance of domain robustness for reliable real-world clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a few-shot learning framework using SimpleShot with frozen pretrained CNN backbones for Monkeypox and pox-like skin disease recognition, achieving best results with MobileNetV2_100 across multiple datasets and showing that binary classification transfers better than multi-class under domain shift.
AI.motivation: CNNs require large annotated datasets, which are impractical for rare conditions like Monkeypox. The authors aim to overcome this limitation by developing a few-shot learning approach that works with limited labeled examples.
AI.method: The pipeline uses frozen, pretrained CNN backbones to extract feature embeddings from skin lesion images, then applies SimpleShot (a non-parametric inductive classifier) using nearest-centroid comparisons in normalized embedding space. Six CNN backbones are benchmarked across 2/4/6-way tasks with 1/5/10-shot configurations on three datasets.
AI.result: MobileNetV2_100 consistently achieves the highest accuracy across all tasks. Cross-dataset evaluation shows binary Mpox-vs-Others transfer remains stable, while multi-class performance degrades significantly under domain shift.
AI.conclusion: Combining inductive FSL methods with lightweight CNN backbones is practical for rare disease classification, but domain robustness remains crucial for reliable real-world clinical deployment.

title: Discovering Sparse Counterfactual Factors via Latent Adjustment for Survey-based Community Intervention
summary: Transportation surveys are widely used to understand travel preferences and adoption barriers, yet most survey-based analyses remain descriptive or predictive and rarely provide sparse, policy-feasible intervention strategies. We study sparse counterfactual community intervention from survey responses, where the goal is to shift a target respondent group toward a desired reference group through controllable survey-variable adjustments. We formulate this task as a policy-feasible distributional alignment problem using a fixed-basis nonnegative latent representation that preserves pre/post comparability and provides a stable map from latent factors to original variables. To make latent movement actionable, target-relevant latent factors are identified through Shapley-guided attribution and transferred to controllable variables as intervention priorities. Feasible group-level adjustments are then learned by minimizing an entropy-regularized optimal-transport discrepancy between the post-intervention target distribution and the reference distribution, together with a weighted $\ell_{2,1}$ penalty that promotes shared policy-lever sparsity. Experiments on real-world transportation survey datasets show that the proposed framework produces compact and interpretable policy-feasible interventions with explicit adjustment magnitudes, improves population-level conversion, and preserves intervention sparsity. Code and datasets are publicly available at: https://github.com/pangjunbiao/latent-group-alignment.git

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework for sparse counterfactual community intervention from transportation survey data, using latent representation learning, Shapley-guided attribution, and entropy-regularized optimal transport to generate policy-feasible, interpretable group-level adjustments that shift a target group toward a reference group.
AI.motivation: Transportation surveys are widely used but remain mostly descriptive/predictive; they rarely provide sparse, policy-feasible intervention strategies to shift travel behavior.
AI.method: 1) Fixed-basis nonnegative latent representation for pre/post comparability. 2) Shapley-guided attribution to identify target-relevant latent factors. 3) Entropy-regularized optimal transport with weighted ℓ2,1 penalty for group-level adjustments.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world transportation survey datasets show the framework produces compact, interpretable interventions with explicit magnitudes, improves population-level conversion, and preserves intervention sparsity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables actionable, policy-feasible interventions from survey data, balancing interpretability, sparsity, and alignment effectiveness.

title: When Relations Break: Analyzing Relation Hallucination in Vision-Language Model Under Rotation and Noise
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong multimodal performance but remain prone to relation hallucination, which requires accurate reasoning over inter-object interactions. We study the impact of visual perturbations, specifically rotation and noise, and show that even mild distortions significantly degrade relational reasoning across models and datasets. We further evaluate prompt-based augmentation and preprocessing strategies (orientation correction and denoising), finding that while they offer partial improvements, they do not fully resolve hallucinations. Our results reveal a gap between perceptual robustness and relational understanding, highlighting the need for more robust, geometry-aware VLMs.

AI.tldr: Vision-language models struggle with relation hallucination under mild visual perturbations like rotation and noise; prompt-based preprocessing offers limited help, revealing a need for more robust, geometry-aware models.
AI.motivation: VLMs frequently hallucinate inter-object relations despite strong multimodal performance, and the robustness of their relational reasoning under visual perturbations is under-explored.
AI.method: Systematically evaluate VLM relational reasoning under rotation and noise perturbations, and test prompt-based preprocessing strategies (orientation correction and denoising).
AI.result: Mild visual distortions significantly degrade relational reasoning across models and datasets; preprocessing only partially mitigates hallucinations.
AI.conclusion: There is a gap between perceptual robustness and relational understanding, motivating the development of more robust, geometry-aware VLMs.

title: Direct Product Flow Matching: Decoupling Radial and Angular Dynamics for Few-Shot Adaptation
summary: Recent flow matching (FM) methods improve the few-shot adaptation of vision-language models, by modeling cross-modal alignment as a continuous multi-step flow. In this paper, we argue that existing FM methods are inherently constrained by incompatible geometric priors on pre-trained cross-modal features, resulting in suboptimal adaptation performance. We first analyze these methods from a polar decomposition perspective (i.e., radial and angular sub-manifolds). Under this new geometric view, we identify three overlooked limitations in them: 1) Angular dynamics distortion: The radial-angular coupling induces non-uniform speed on the angular sub-manifold, leading to regression training difficulty and extra truncation errors. 2) Radial dynamics neglect: Feature normalization discards modality confidence, failing to distinguish out-of-distribution and in-distribution data, and abandoning crucial radial dynamics. 3) Context-agnostic unconditional flow: Dataset-specific information loss during pre-trained cross-modal feature extraction remains unrecovered. To resolve these issues, we propose warped product flow matching (WP-FM), a unified Riemannian framework that reformulates alignment on a warped product manifold. Within this framework, we derive direct product flow matching (DP-FM) by introducing a constant-warping metric, which yields a decoupled cylindrical manifold (i.e., direct product manifold). DP-FM enables independent radial evolution and constant-speed angular geodesic transport, effectively eliminating angular dynamics distortion while preserving radial consistency. Meanwhile, we incorporate classifier-free guidance by conditioning the flow on the pre-trained VLMs' hidden states to inject missing dataset-specific information. Extensive results across 11 benchmarks have demonstrated that DP-FM achieves a new state-of-the-art for multi-step few-shot adaptation.

AI.tldr: WP-FM introduces a warped product manifold framework to fix geometric issues (angular distortion, radial neglect, context-agnostic flow) in flow matching methods for few-shot adaptation of vision-language models, achieving SOTA on 11 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing flow matching methods for few-shot VL adaptation suffer from incompatible geometric priors: angular dynamics distortion due to radial-angular coupling, radial dynamics neglect from feature normalization discarding modality confidence, and context-agnostic unconditional flow losing dataset-specific information.
AI.method: Proposes warped product flow matching (WP-FM), a unified Riemannian framework. Derives direct product flow matching (DP-FM) with constant-warping metric on a cylindrical (direct product) manifold, decoupling radial evolution and constant-speed angular geodesic transport. Also incorporates classifier-free guidance using pre-trained VLM hidden states to inject dataset-specific info.
AI.result: Achieves new state-of-the-art across 11 benchmarks for multi-step few-shot adaptation of vision-language models.
AI.conclusion: The geometric reformulation (decoupled radial & angular dynamics) and classifier-free guidance effectively resolve the three identified limitations, leading to superior few-shot adaptation performance.

title: HeterSEED: Semantics-Structure Decoupling for Heterogeneous Graph Learning under Heterophily
summary: Many real-world heterogeneous graphs exhibit pronounced heterophily, where connected nodes often have dissimilar labels or play different semantic roles. In such settings, standard heterogeneous graph neural networks that aggregate messages along metapaths or meta-relations primarily based on feature similarity can propagate misleading information, since feature similarity may be misaligned with underlying relational semantics. In this paper, we propose HeterSEED, a semantics-structure decoupling framework for heterogeneous graph learning under heterophily. HeterSEED decouples representation learning into a heterogeneous semantic channel that captures type- and relation-aware local semantics and a structure-aware heterophily channel that separates homophilic and heterophilic neighborhoods via pseudo-label-guided partitioning and aggregates them using metapath-based structural weights. A node-level adaptive fusion mechanism then combines the two channels to produce context-dependent node representations. Theoretically, we establish that, on heterogeneous graphs under heterophily, HeterSEED is strictly more expressive than standard heterogeneous graph neural networks that rely primarily on feature similarity and provably reduces the prediction bias introduced by heterophilic neighbors. Experiments on five real-world heterogeneous graphs, including two large-scale networks at the million-node and hundred-million-edge scale, demonstrate that HeterSEED consistently outperforms representative heterogeneous graph neural networks and recent heterophily-aware baselines, especially in strongly heterophilic regimes.

AI.tldr: HeterSEED is a semantics-structure decoupling framework for heterogeneous graph learning under heterophily, which separates representation learning into a semantic channel and a structure-aware heterophily channel, then fuses them adaptively. It outperforms existing methods on five real-world heterogeneous graphs.
AI.motivation: Standard heterogeneous GNNs aggregate messages based on feature similarity, which fails under heterophily (where connected nodes have dissimilar labels/roles). This misalignment between feature similarity and relational semantics leads to misleading information propagation.
AI.method: HeterSEED decouples learning into: (1) a heterogeneous semantic channel for type- and relation-aware local semantics, and (2) a structure-aware heterophily channel that separates homophilic/heterophilic neighborhoods via pseudo-label-guided partitioning and aggregates them with metapath-based structural weights. A node-level adaptive fusion combines both channels.
AI.result: Experiments on five real-world heterogeneous graphs (including million-node and hundred-million-edge scale) show HeterSEED consistently outperforms representative heterogeneous GNNs and heterophily-aware baselines, especially under strong heterophily.
AI.conclusion: HeterSEED is theoretically more expressive than standard heterogeneous GNNs under heterophily and provably reduces prediction bias from heterophilic neighbors, offering a robust solution for heterophilious heterogeneous graph learning.

title: ScriptHOI: Learning Scripted State Transitions for Open-Vocabulary Human-Object Interaction Detection
summary: Open-vocabulary human-object interaction (HOI) detection requires recognizing interaction phrases that may not appear as annotated categories during training. Recent vision-language HOI detectors improve semantic transfer by matching human-object features with text embeddings, but their predictions are often dominated by object affordance and phrase-level co-occurrence. As a result, a model may predict \textit{cut cake} from the presence of a knife and a cake without verifying whether the hand, tool, target, contact pattern, and object state jointly support the action. We propose \textbf{ScriptHOI}, a structured framework that represents each interaction phrase as a soft scripted state transition. Rather than treating a phrase as a single class token, ScriptHOI decomposes it into body-role, contact, geometry, affordance, motion, and object-state slots. A visual state tokenizer parses each detected human-object pair into corresponding state tokens, and a slot-wise matcher estimates both script coverage and script conflict. These two quantities calibrate HOI logits, expose missing visual evidence, and provide training constraints for incomplete annotations. To avoid suppressing valid but unannotated interactions, we further introduce interval partial-label learning, which constrains unannotated candidates with script-derived lower and upper probability bounds instead of assigning closed-world negatives. A counterfactual script contrast loss swaps individual script slots to discourage object-only shortcuts. Experiments on HICO-DET, V-COCO, and open-vocabulary HOI splits show that ScriptHOI improves rare and unseen interaction recognition while substantially reducing affordance-conflict false positives.

AI.tldr: ScriptHOI decomposes HOI phrases into structured script slots (body-role, contact, geometry, affordance, motion, object-state) with a visual state tokenizer and slot-wise matcher, using interval partial-label learning and counterfactual contrast loss to improve rare/unseen interaction recognition and reduce affordance-conflict false positives.
AI.motivation: Existing open-vocabulary HOI detectors rely on object affordance and phrase co-occurrence, causing them to predict actions (e.g., "cut cake") without verifying all supporting visual evidence (hand, tool, contact pattern, object state, etc.), leading to affordance-conflict false positives.
AI.method: ScriptHOI represents interaction phrases as soft scripted state transitions decomposed into six slots (body-role, contact, geometry, affordance, motion, object-state). A visual state tokenizer parses HOI pairs into state tokens, and a slot-wise matcher computes script coverage and conflict to calibrate HOI logits. Interval partial-label learning constrains unannotated candidates with probability bounds, and a counterfactual script contrast loss swaps script slots to reduce object-only shortcuts.
AI.result: Experiments on HICO-DET, V-COCO, and open-vocabulary HOI splits show improved rare and unseen interaction recognition and substantially reduced affordance-conflict false positives.
AI.conclusion: Structuring HOI phrases as decomposed script states with explicit slot-wise verification and learning constraints effectively addresses the object affordance shortcut problem in open-vocabulary HOI detection.

title: Automated Formal Proofs of Combinatorial Identities via Wilf-Zeilberger Guidance and LLMs
summary: Automating formal proofs of combinatorial identities is challenging for LLM-based provers, as long-horizon proof planning is required and unconstrained search quickly explodes. Symbolic methods such as the Wilf-Zeilberger (WZ) method can achieve a mechanized proof of combinatorial identities by constructing special auxiliary functions and demonstrating that they satisfy specific recurrence relations. We propose WZ-LLM, a neuro-symbolic framework that turns WZ proof plans into executable proof sketches in Lean 4 and uses an LLM-based prover to discharge the resulting machine-checkable subgoals. We also train a dedicated WZ-Prover via a Lean-kernel-verified bootstrapping loop with expert-verified iteration, followed by DAPO-based refinement. Experiments show that WZ-LLM achieves a 34% proof success rate on LCI-Test (100 classic combinatorial identities), outperforming strong baselines such as DeepSeek-V3 and Goedel-Prover-V2, and delivering consistent gains on CombiBench and PutnamBench-Comb. These results indicate that our framework provides two complementary strengths: improved direct proving for identities beyond the scope of WZ, and substantially higher end-to-end success when WZ sketches guide a specialized prover.

AI.tldr: WZ-LLM is a neuro-symbolic framework that combines the Wilf-Zeilberger method with an LLM-based prover in Lean 4, achieving 34% proof success on combinatorial identities, outperforming strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Automating formal proofs of combinatorial identities is challenging for LLM-based provers due to long-horizon proof planning and unconstrained search explosion. Symbolic methods like WZ exist but need integration with modern proof assistants.
AI.method: WZ-LLM turns WZ proof plans into executable Lean 4 proof sketches and uses an LLM-based prover to discharge subgoals. It trains a dedicated WZ-Prover via a Lean-kernel-verified bootstrapping loop with expert-verified iteration and DAPO-based refinement.
AI.result: 34% proof success rate on LCI-Test (100 classic combinatorial identities), outperforming DeepSeek-V3 and Goedel-Prover-V2, with consistent gains on CombiBench and PutnamBench-Comb.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides two complementary strengths: improved direct proving for identities beyond WZ's scope, and higher end-to-end success when WZ sketches guide a specialized prover.

title: A Queueing-Theoretic Framework for Stability Analysis of LLM Inference with KV Cache Memory Constraints
summary: The rapid adoption of large language models (LLMs) has created significant challenges for efficient inference at scale. Unlike traditional workloads, LLM inference is constrained by both computation and the memory overhead of key-value (KV) caching, which accelerates decoding but quickly exhausts GPU memory. In this paper, we introduce the first queueing-theoretic framework that explicitly incorporates both computation and GPU memory constraints into the analysis of LLM inference. Based on this framework, we derive rigorous stability and instability conditions that determine whether an LLM inference service can sustain incoming demand without unbounded queue growth. This result offers a powerful tool for system deployment, potentially addressing the core challenge of GPU provisioning. By combining an estimated request arrival rate with our derived stable service rate, operators can calculate the necessary cluster size to avoid both costly over-purchasing and performance-violating under-provisioning. We further validate our theoretical predictions through extensive experiments in real GPU production environments. Our results show that the predicted stability conditions are highly accurate, with deviations typically within 10%.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a queueing-theoretic framework that jointly considers computation and GPU memory constraints for LLM inference, deriving stability conditions to guide optimal GPU provisioning, validated with <10% error in production.
AI.motivation: LLM inference at scale faces dual bottlenecks: computation and KV-cache memory pressure. Current approaches lack a unified theoretical framework to determine whether a system can sustain demand without queue growth, leading to either over-provisioning (waste) or under-provisioning (performance violations).
AI.method: The authors propose a queueing-theoretic framework that explicitly models both computation and GPU memory constraints. They derive rigorous stability and instability conditions connecting request arrival rate to service rate under memory constraints. Validation uses real GPU production environments.
AI.result: Theoretical stability conditions are highly accurate in practice, with deviations typically within 10% from real-world GPU production experiments.
AI.conclusion: This framework enables operators to calculate the necessary cluster size from arrival rates and stable service rates, avoiding both over-purchasing and under-provisioning.

title: Height-Guided Projection Reparameterization for Camera-LiDAR Occupancy
summary: 3D occupancy prediction aims to infer dense, voxel-wise scene semantics from sensor observations, where the 2D-to-3D view transformation serves as a crucial step in bridging image features and volumetric representations. Most previous methods rely on a fixed projection space, where 3D reference points are uniformly sampled along pillars. However, such sampling struggles to capture the sparsity and height variations of real-world scenes, leading to ambiguous correspondences and unreliable feature aggregation. To address these challenges, we propose HiPR, a camera-LiDAR occupancy framework with Height-Guided Projection Reparameterization. HiPR first encodes LiDAR into a BEV height map to capture the maximum height of the point cloud. HiPR then adjusts the sampling range of each pillar using the height prior, enabling adaptive reparameterization of the projection space. As a result, the projected points are redistributed into geometrically meaningful regions rather than fixed ranges. Meanwhile, we mask out the invalid parts of the height map to avoid misleading the feature aggregation. In addition, to alleviate the training instability caused by noisy LiDAR-derived heights, we introduce a training-time Progressive Height Conditioning strategy, which gradually transitions the conditioning signal from ground-truth heights to LiDAR heights. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HiPR consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods while maintaining real-time inference. The code and pretrained models can be found at https://github.com/Rayn-Wu/HiPR.

AI.tldr: HiPR is a camera-LiDAR 3D occupancy prediction framework that uses height-guided projection reparameterization to adaptively adjust sampling ranges based on LiDAR-derived height priors, outperforming state-of-the-art methods while maintaining real-time inference.
AI.motivation: Existing 2D-to-3D view transformation methods rely on fixed projection spaces with uniformly sampled pillars, which fail to capture the sparsity and height variations of real-world scenes, causing ambiguous correspondences and unreliable feature aggregation.
AI.method: HiPR encodes LiDAR into a BEV height map to capture point cloud maximum heights, then adjusts each pillar's sampling range using this height prior for adaptive reparameterization. Invalid height map regions are masked out. A Progressive Height Conditioning strategy gradually transitions from ground-truth to LiDAR heights during training to alleviate instability.
AI.result: HiPR consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on 3D occupancy prediction while maintaining real-time inference performance.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, height-guided reparameterization of the projection space effectively resolves the limitations of fixed-pillar sampling, achieving superior occupancy prediction with practical real-time capabilities.

title: Geometry-Aware Neural Optimizer for Shape Optimization and Inversion
summary: Geometry is central to PDE-governed systems, motivating shape optimization and inversion. Classical pipelines conduct costly forward simulation with geometry processing, requiring substantial expert effort. Neural surrogates accelerate forward analysis but do not close the loop because gradients from objectives to geometry are often unavailable. Existing differentiable methods either rely on restrictive parameterizations or unstable latent optimization driven by scalar objectives, limiting interpretability and part-wise control. To address these challenges, we propose Geometry-Aware Neural Optimizer (GANO), an end-to-end differentiable framework that unifies geometry representation, field-level prediction, and automated optimization/inversion in a single latent-space loop. GANO encodes shapes with an auto-decoder and stabilizes latent updates via a denoising mechanism, and a geometry-injected surrogate provides a reliable gradient pathway for geometry updates. Moreover, GANO supports part-wise control through null-space projection and uses remeshing-free projection to accelerate geometry processing. We further prove that denoising induces an implicit Jacobian regularization that reduces decoder sensitivity, yielding controlled deformations. Experiments on three benchmarks spanning 2D Helmholtz, 2D airfoil, and 3D vehicles show state-of-the-art accuracy and stable, controllable updates, achieving up to +55.9% lift-to-drag improvement for airfoils and ~7% drag reduction for vehicles.

AI.tldr: GANO is an end-to-end differentiable framework unifying geometry representation, field prediction, and optimization/inversion for PDE-governed systems, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with stable, controllable geometry updates and significant performance gains on 2D/3D benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Classical shape optimization pipelines are costly and require expert effort; neural surrogates accelerate forward analysis but lack differentiable feedback from objectives to geometry; existing differentiable methods rely on restrictive parameterizations or unstable latent optimization with limited interpretability and part-wise control.
AI.method: GANO encodes shapes via an auto-decoder with a denoising mechanism for stable latent updates, uses a geometry-injected surrogate for reliable gradient pathways, supports part-wise control via null-space projection, and applies remeshing-free projection for efficient geometry processing.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on 2D Helmholtz, 2D airfoil, and 3D vehicle benchmarks; up to +55.9% lift-to-drag improvement for airfoils and ~7% drag reduction for vehicles; denoising provides implicit Jacobian regularization for controlled deformations.
AI.conclusion: GANO provides a unified, stable, and interpretable differentiable optimization framework for PDE-governed geometry optimization, with provable regularization and superior performance compared to existing methods.

title: FlowDIS: Language-Guided Dichotomous Image Segmentation with Flow Matching
summary: Accurate image segmentation is essential for modern computer vision applications such as image editing, autonomous driving, and medical image analysis. In recent years, Dichotomous Image Segmentation (DIS) has become a standard task for training and evaluating highly accurate segmentation models. Existing DIS approaches often fail to preserve fine-grained details or fully capture the semantic structure of the foreground. To address these challenges, we present FlowDIS, a novel dichotomous image segmentation method built on the flow matching framework, which learns a time-dependent vector field to transport the image distribution to the corresponding mask distribution, optionally conditioned on a text prompt. Moreover, with our Position-Aware Instance Pairing (PAIP) training strategy, FlowDIS offers strong controllability through text prompts, enabling precise, pixel-level object segmentation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches both with and without language guidance. Compared with the best prior DIS method, FlowDIS achieves a 5.5% higher $F_β^ω$ measure and 43% lower MAE ($\mathcal{M}$) on the DIS-TE test set. The code is available at: https://github.com/Picsart-AI-Research/FlowDIS

AI.tldr: FlowDIS is a new dichotomous image segmentation method using flow matching and a Position-Aware Instance Pairing (PAIP) strategy that outperforms state-of-the-art by 5.5% higher Fβω and 43% lower MAE on DIS-TE.
AI.motivation: Existing DIS approaches fail to preserve fine-grained details and fully capture the semantic structure of the foreground.
AI.method: FlowDIS builds on the flow matching framework, learning a time-dependent vector field to transport image distribution to mask distribution, with optional text prompt conditioning, and uses a Position-Aware Instance Pairing (PAIP) training strategy.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods with 5.5% higher Fβω measure and 43% lower MAE on the DIS-TE test set.
AI.conclusion: FlowDIS offers strong controllability through text prompts and achieves precise, pixel-level object segmentation.

title: Data-dependent Exploration for Online Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
summary: Online reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) has emerged as a promising paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs) by continuously collecting new preference feedback during training. A foundational challenge in this setting is exploration, which requires algorithms that enable the LLMs to generate informative comparisons that improve sample-efficiency in online RLHF. Existing exploration strategies often derive bonuses via on-policy expectations, which are difficult to estimate reliably from the limited historical preference data available during training; as a result, the policy can prematurely down-weight under-explored regions that may contain high-value behaviors. In this paper, we propose data-dependent exploration for preference optimization (DEPO), a simple and scalable method that leverages historical data to construct an extra uncertainty bonus for high-uncertainty regions, encouraging exploration toward potentially high-value data. Theoretically, we provide a data-dependent regret bound for the proposed algorithm, showing that it adapts to the hardness of the learning task itself and can be tighter than worst-case bounds in practice. Empirically, the proposed method consistently outperforms strong baselines across benchmarks, demonstrating improved sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: DEPO proposes a data-dependent exploration bonus for online RLHF that leverages historical preference data to encourage exploration in high-uncertainty regions, improving sample efficiency with a tighter regret bound.
AI.motivation: Existing exploration strategies in online RLHF rely on on-policy expectations that are hard to estimate from limited historical preference data, causing policies to prematurely down-weight under-explored but potentially high-value regions.
AI.method: DEPO constructs an extra uncertainty bonus from historical data to guide exploration toward high-uncertainty regions, with a data-dependent regret bound that adapts to task hardness.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms strong baselines across benchmarks with improved sample efficiency; the data-dependent regret bound can be tighter than worst-case bounds in practice.
AI.conclusion: DEPO provides a simple, scalable, and theoretically grounded exploration strategy for online RLHF that effectively balances exploration and exploitation.

title: Library learning with e-graphs on jazz harmony
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures, 2 listings, 1 table, no conference
summary: Humans can acquire a highly structured intuitive understanding of musical patterns, yet these patterns often require multiple iterations of reflection and re-listening to internalize fully. To capture such an internalization process, we present a computational model for the learning of jazz harmonic patterns based on library learning. Given a corpus of harmonic progressions, our model searches over a space of programs composed of primitive harmonic relations in order to discover concise generative explanations of the corpus. The model first enumerates possible programs for each piece, and then jointly learns a library of harmonic patterns and refactored programs. To efficiently navigate the vast joint space of programs and libraries, we integrate deductive parsing with library learning on e-graphs. We explore how well our model captures aspects of human musical pattern learning by evaluating the intuitiveness of both programs and libraries, as well as similarities to human-written harmonic derivations.

AI.tldr: A computational model that learns jazz harmonic patterns by discovering concise generative explanations through library learning on e-graphs, mimicking how humans internalize musical patterns.
AI.motivation: Humans internalize musical patterns through iterative reflection, but existing computational models don't capture this process of gradually acquiring structured understanding of harmonic patterns.
AI.method: The model searches over programs composed of primitive harmonic relations, enumerates possible programs per piece, then jointly learns a library of harmonic patterns and refactored programs using deductive parsing with library learning on e-graphs.
AI.result: The model discovers concise generative explanations of harmonic progressions and produces patterns that align with human intuitiveness and human-written harmonic derivations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed library learning approach effectively captures aspects of human musical pattern learning, offering a computational framework for how structured musical knowledge is internalized.

title: A unified Benchmark for Multi-Frame Image Restoration under Severe Refractive Warping
summary: Video sequence capturing through refractive dynamic media, such as a turbulent air or water surface, often suffer from severe geometric distortions and temporal instability. While recent advances address mild atmospheric turbulence, no existing benchmarks systematically evaluate restoration methods under strong and highly nonuniform refractive conditions. We present a comprehensive benchmark for geometric distortion removal in video, covering a range from turbulence-like mild warping to strong discontinuous refractive deformations. The benchmark includes both laboratory-captured real data and synthetic sequences generated for static scenes via physics-based light refraction modeling across four distortion levels and multiple surface wave types. We evaluate a spectrum of methods from simple baselines and classical registration algorithms to advanced learning-based approaches including DATUM and our proposed diffusion based V-cache for high and extreme distortions regimes. Evaluation uses both pixel-level (PSNR, SSIM), and perceptual (LPIPS, DINO, CLIP) metrics providing the first large scale analysis of geometric distortion removal. Our benchmark establishes a new foundation for developing and evaluating algorithms capable of reconstructing video from highly distorted optical environments. Our code and datasets are available at https://github.com/iafoss/refractive-mfir-benchmark.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive benchmark for evaluating video restoration methods under strong and nonuniform refractive geometric distortions, including real and synthetic data, multiple distortion levels, and various evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks only address mild atmospheric turbulence; there is no systematic evaluation of restoration methods under strong, highly nonuniform refractive distortions in video.
