Our work is the first to unify the two notions of phase transitions in practice: it provides a concrete diagnostic for when and why diffusion models rely on conditioning and global denoising, enabling principled evaluation of model efficiency and guiding the design of architectures and sampling schemes that avoid unnecessary computation.

AI.tldr: Diffusion models exhibit two types of phase transitions (symmetry breaking and nonlocality) during generation, and this study shows they occur nearly simultaneously in modern diffusion transformers, unifying these perspectives for the first time.
AI.motivation: To unify two previously separate notions of phase transitions in diffusion models—symmetry breaking and nonlocality—and understand when and why diffusion models rely on conditioning and global denoising.
AI.method: Evaluating the dynamics and outcomes of generation trajectories in modern diffusion transformers to compare the timing of non-locality and symmetry breaking critical windows.
AI.result: Near-simultaneous occurrence of non-locality and symmetry breaking critical times during generation.
AI.conclusion: Provides a concrete diagnostic for critical windows in diffusion models, enabling principled evaluation of model efficiency and guiding architecture design and sampling schemes to avoid unnecessary computation.

title: Replay-Based Continual Learning for Physics-Informed Neural Operators
summary: Neural operators generally demonstrate strong predictive performance on in-distribution (ID) problems. However, a critical limitation of existing methods is their significant performance degradation when encountering out-of-distribution (OOD) data. To address this issue, this work introduces continual learning into physics-informed neural operators, with particular emphasis on neural operators built upon the Transolver architecture, and proposes a simple yet effective replay-based continual learning strategy. The proposed method is fully physics-informed and does not require labeled data, relying solely on input fields together with physical constraints for training. When new OOD data become available, a small number of past data are incorporated through a distillation-based constraint to preserve previously acquired knowledge and alleviate catastrophic forgetting. Meanwhile, a transfer learning LoRA is employed to enable rapid adaptation to the new data. The proposed framework is systematically validated on three representative physical problems, including the Darcy flow problem in fluid mechanics, a two-dimensional hyperelastic brain tumor problem in biomechanics, and a three-dimensional linear elastic Triply Periodic Minimal Surfaces problem in solid mechanics. The results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting on previously learned data while maintaining fast adaptability to new data. Compared with conventional joint training strategies, the proposed method significantly improves training efficiency while reducing additional memory usage and computational cost.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a replay-based continual learning strategy for physics-informed neural operators (Transolver) to address performance degradation on out-of-distribution data. The method uses distillation-based constraints with past data and LoRA transfer learning, achieving efficient adaptation while preventing catastrophic forgetting, validated on Darcy flow, brain tumor, and TPMS problems.
AI.motivation: Existing neural operators perform well on in-distribution data but suffer significant performance degradation on out-of-distribution data, limiting their applicability in real-world scenarios where data distributions shift.
AI.method: A replay-based continual learning strategy combining: (1) physics-informed training with no labeled data (only input fields + physical constraints), (2) distillation-based replay of past data to prevent catastrophic forgetting, (3) LoRA transfer learning for rapid adaptation to new OOD data.
AI.result: Validated on three problems (Darcy flow, hyperelastic brain tumor, 3D TPMS), the method effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting, maintains fast adaptability to new data, and improves training efficiency with reduced memory and computational cost compared to joint training.
AI.conclusion: The proposed replay-based continual learning framework successfully enables neural operators to handle OOD scenarios efficiently, offering a practical path toward more robust and adaptive physics-informed machine learning models.

title: Bridging Input Feature Spaces Towards Graph Foundation Models
summary: Unlike vision and language domains, graph learning lacks a shared input space, as input features differ across graph datasets not only in semantics, but also in value ranges and dimensionality. This misalignment prevents graph models from generalizing across datasets, limiting their use as foundation models. In this work, we propose ALL-IN, a simple and theoretically grounded method that enables transferability across datasets with different input features. Our approach projects node features into a shared random space and constructs representations via covariance-based statistics, thus eliminating dependence on the original feature space. We show that the computed node-covariance operators and the resulting node representations are invariant in distribution to permutations of the input features. We further demonstrate that the expected operator exhibits invariance to general orthogonal transformations of the input features. Empirically, ALL-IN achieves strong performance across diverse node- and graph-level tasks on unseen datasets with new input features, without requiring architecture changes or retraining. These results point to a promising direction for input-agnostic, transferable graph models.

AI.tldr: ALL-IN proposes a method that projects node features into a shared random space and uses covariance-based statistics to enable graph models to transfer across datasets with different input features, without retraining or architecture changes.
AI.motivation: Graph learning lacks a shared input space across datasets due to differences in semantics, value ranges, and dimensionality, preventing graph models from generalizing across datasets and limiting their use as foundation models.
AI.method: Projects node features into a shared random space, then constructs representations via covariance-based statistics. Uses node-covariance operators that are distribution-invariant to permutations and expected-operator invariant to orthogonal transformations of input features.
AI.result: ALL-IN achieves strong performance across diverse node- and graph-level tasks on unseen datasets with new input features, without requiring architecture changes or retraining.
AI.conclusion: The results point to a promising direction for input-agnostic, transferable graph models that can serve as foundation models.

title: Hybrid Iterative Neural Low-Regularity Integrator for Nonlinear Dispersive Equations
summary: We propose HIN-LRI, a hybrid framework that augments a classical numerical solver with a neural operator trained to correct the solver's structured truncation error. A base low-regularity integrator provides a consistent first-order approximation to nonlinear dispersive PDEs, while a lightweight neural network, operating on a low-dimensional latent manifold, learns the residual defect that analytical methods cannot close. An explicit time-step scaling on the neural correction ensures that its Lipschitz contribution remains $\mathcal{O}(τ)$, yielding a Gronwall stability factor bounded uniformly in the step size and independent of the spatial resolution. The network is trained end-to-end through a solver-in-the-loop objective that unrolls the full iteration and penalises trajectory error in a Bourgain-type norm, aligning learning with multi-step solver dynamics rather than isolated one-step targets. Under stated assumptions, the global error satisfies $C(\varepsilon_{net}+δ)\,τ^γ\ln(1/τ)$, where $\varepsilon_{net}$ measures the network approximation quality and $δ$ the training shortfall. Experiments on three dispersive benchmarks with rough data show that HIN-LRI improves accuracy over analytical integrators, splitting methods, and neural PDE surrogates, with stable spatial refinement, effective out-of-distribution transfer, and modest online overhead.

AI.tldr: HIN-LRI combines a classical low-regularity integrator with a neural operator that corrects truncation errors, achieving provably stable and accurate solutions for nonlinear dispersive PDEs with rough data.
AI.motivation: Classical numerical solvers for nonlinear dispersive PDEs suffer from structured truncation errors that analytical methods cannot close, especially for rough data. There is a need for a hybrid approach that preserves stability guarantees while improving accuracy.
AI.method: The framework augments a base low-regularity integrator (first-order) with a lightweight neural network operating on a low-dimensional latent manifold. The neural correction is scaled by the time-step to ensure Lipschitz contribution is O(τ), yielding a Gronwall stability factor independent of spatial resolution. Training uses a solver-in-the-loop objective with unrolled iterations and trajectory error in Bourgain-type norm.
AI.result: Global error satisfies C(ε_net+δ) τ^γ ln(1/τ). Experiments on three dispersive benchmarks with rough data show improved accuracy over analytical integrators, splitting methods, and neural PDE surrogates, with stable spatial refinement, effective out-of-distribution transfer, and modest online overhead.
AI.conclusion: HIN-LRI effectively bridges classical numerical analysis and neural operators, providing both theoretical stability guarantees and practical accuracy gains for challenging dispersive PDE problems.

title: Self-Attention as Transport: Limits of Symmetric Spectral Diagnostics
comment: 42 pages, 6 figures, 3 tables; 82-page online supplement (proofs, additional experiments, dataset statistics) as an ancillary file
summary:
Large language models hallucinate in predictable ways: attention routing fails by over-concentrating on a narrow set of positions, or by spreading so diffusely that relevance is diluted, and the shape of the failure carries diagnostic signal. A widely used family of spectral methods analyzes the symmetric component of the degree-normalized attention operator, which governs transport capacity; we prove that every transpose-invariant spectral diagnostic of this operator is structurally orientation-blind (it cannot distinguish an operator from its transpose, and therefore cannot detect information-flow direction), with a quantitative converse establishing the asymmetry coefficient $G$ as the unique control parameter for direction.
  Pairing this with a closed-form bipartite-Cheeger landscape for canonical causal architectures, we show that uniform causal attention satisfies an $n$-independent floor $φ\ge 1/5$ with worst cut at $t^\ast/n \approx 0.32$, while window attention pierces the floor as $O(w/n)$; failure modes are shape-different, not just value-different. The resulting two-axis diagnostic ($φ$ for capacity, $G$ for direction) yields a falsifiable polarity prediction: bottleneck- and diffuse-dominated benchmarks should exhibit opposite polarity. Under length-controlled evaluation, transport features retain interpretable signal (LC-AUROC from 0.62 to 0.84) on tested models up to 8B parameters, with polarity reversing as predicted between HaluEval and MedHallu.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that spectral diagnostics of attention operators are orientation-blind (cannot detect direction), introduces the asymmetry coefficient G as the unique direction control parameter, derives a bipartite-Cheeger floor for causal attention (φ ≥ 1/5), and shows that failure modes (bottleneck vs. diffuse) have distinct shapes. A two-axis diagnostic (φ for capacity, G for direction) predicts opposite polarity between bottleneck- and diffuse-dominated benchmarks, validated on models up to 8B parameters with LC-AUROC 0.62–0.84.
AI.motivation: To understand and predict hallucinations in large language models by analyzing how attention routing fails — either over-concentrating on narrow positions or spreading too diffusely — and to develop interpretable diagnostics that distinguish these failure types.
AI.method: The paper proves theoretically that transpose-invariant spectral diagnostics of the degree-normalized attention operator are orientation-blind, establishes the asymmetry coefficient G as the unique direction measure, derives a bipartite-Cheeger landscape for causal attention architectures, and proposes a two-axis diagnostic (φ for capacity, G for direction). Empirical validation uses length-controlled evaluation on models up to 8B parameters.
AI.result: Uniform causal attention satisfies an n-independent floor φ ≥ 1/5 with worst cut at t*/n ≈ 0.32; window attention pierces the floor as O(w/n). The two-axis diagnostic yields polarity reversal between HaluEval (bottleneck-dominated) and MedHallu (diffuse-dominated), with LC-AUROC from 0.62 to 0.84.
AI.conclusion: Attention failure modes are shape-different (not just value-different), and the proposed polarity prediction is falsifiable and validated empirically. The two-axis diagnostic provides interpretable signal for detecting hallucination tendencies across different benchmarks and model scales.

title: Regime-Conditioned Evaluation in Multi-Context Bayesian Optimization
summary: Published transfer-BO comparisons often estimate an average treatment effect of acquisition choice over hidden regime variables, while practitioners need the conditional effect for their specific prior quality, budget ratio, and metric. An audit of 40 transfer-BO papers from NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, AISTATS, UAI, TMLR, JMLR, and AutoML-Conf (2022-2025) finds that 98% never vary B/|A| as a controlled axis. On the same GDSC2 benchmark, changing only the budget reverses the ranking: at B=50, Greedy outperforms UCB by 0.050 Hit@1, while at B=100, UCB outperforms Greedy by 0.035. We capture this transition with the Portable Regime Score PRS=(B/|A|)(1-rho), where rho is the prior rank correlation and can be estimated from pilot contexts before the main comparison. Across 79 conditions spanning chemistry, drug-response biology, and HPO, a hierarchical model gives beta=0.50 (p=1.1e-9), and 19% of conditions fall in an equivalence zone where |advantage|<0.01 Hit@1. In five published reversal cases, PRS predicts the winner from pre-comparison observables. A No-Free-Leaderboard proposition explains why unconditional rankings are unstable: when CATE changes sign across regimes, the reported ATE becomes a function of benchmark mixture. RegimePlanner, which estimates rho online and switches acquisition accordingly, wins all 16 HPO-B search spaces at B=100 and exceeds the matched {Greedy,UCB} per-context oracle on GDSC2 by 18%. Pre-registered predictions achieve 27/40=67.5% overall accuracy and above 90% within EMA prior families. The practical protocol is simple: report B/|A|, rho, K, and metric alongside any claimed acquisition advantage.

AI.tldr: This paper audits 40 transfer-BO papers, finding that 98% fail to vary budget ratio (B/|A|) as a controlled axis. The authors introduce the Portable Regime Score (PRS) to predict when acquisition strategy rankings reverse based on budget and prior quality, demonstrate empirical reversals, and propose RegimePlanner—an adaptive acquisition method that outperforms fixed strategies.
AI.motivation: Existing transfer-BO evaluations report average treatment effects that hide conditional effects dependent on prior quality, budget ratio, and metric. Practitioners cannot determine which acquisition works for their specific setting, leading to unreliable and potentially reversed rankings.
AI.method: The authors audit 40 transfer-BO papers (2022-2025), introduce the Portable Regime Score PRS=(B/|A|)(1-ρ), fit a hierarchical model across 79 conditions, propose RegimePlanner (online ρ estimation with dynamic acquisition switching), and conduct pre-registered predictions.
AI.result: 98% of audited papers never vary budget ratio. Budget reversal demonstrated: at B=50 Greedy beats UCB by 0.050, at B=100 UCB beats Greedy by 0.035. Hierarchical model shows β=0.50 (p=1.1e-9). RegimePlanner wins all 16 HPO-B spaces at B=100 and exceeds matched oracle by 18% on GDSC2. Pre-registered accuracy: 67.5% overall, >90% within EMA families.
AI.conclusion: Unconditional rankings are unstable—a "No-Free-Leaderboard" proposition applies. Practical protocol: report B/|A|, ρ, K, and metric alongside any acquisition advantage claim. Regime-aware evaluation is essential for reproducible transfer-BO comparisons.

title: A geometric relation of the error introduced by sampling a language model's output distribution to its internal state
comment: 12 Pages, 10 Figures, 2 Appendices. To appear in Proceedings of ICML 2026
summary: GPT-style language models are sensitive to single-token changes at generation points where the predicted probability distribution is spread across multiple tokens. Viewing this sensitivity as a geometric property, we derive an $\mathfrak{so}(n)$-valued 1-form that depends only on the geometry of the token embeddings. Despite this purely geometric origin, we show that its curvature is semantically meaningful: On chess reasoning tasks, the curvature couples to the world model of an off-the-shelf instruction-tuned model, with transformations clustering by board region and respecting piece importance. Our findings suggest that token space geometry directly reflects how models internally represent problems.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that GPT-style language models have geometric sensitivity to single-token changes at uncertain generation points, deriving a mathematical 1-form from token embedding geometry whose curvature reveals semantic structure in how models represent problems internally.
AI.motivation: To understand why language models are sensitive to small input changes at points of high prediction uncertainty, and whether this sensitivity has meaningful geometric structure beyond randomness.
AI.method: Derives an so(n)-valued 1-form from token embedding geometry and analyzes its curvature properties, then tests on chess reasoning tasks with instruction-tuned models.
AI.result: The curvature of this purely geometric quantity is semantically meaningful - on chess tasks it couples to the model's world model, with transformations clustering by board region and respecting piece importance.
AI.conclusion: Token space geometry directly reflects how models internally represent problems, providing a geometric lens into model internals.

title: Cross-Model Consistency of Feature Importance in Electrospinning: Separating Robust from Model-Dependent Features
summary: Electrospinning is a highly sensitive fabrication process in which small variations in operating parameters can significantly influence fiber morphology and material performance. Machine learning (ML) methods are increasingly employed to model these process-structure relationships and to identify the relative importance of processing variables. However, most existing studies rely on a single ML model, implicitly assuming that the resulting feature importance is robust and reproducible. In this study, the consistency of feature importance across multiple ML model families was systematically evaluated using a curated dataset of 96 polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) electrospinning experiments. Twenty-one ML models representing linear, tree-based, kernel-based, neural network, and instance-based approaches were trained and compared. To provide a unified interpretability framework, SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values were used to calculate feature importance consistently across all models. A rank-based statistical analysis was then performed to quantify inter-model agreement and assess the robustness of parameter rankings. The results demonstrate that predictive performance and interpretive reliability are fundamentally distinct properties. Although several models achieved comparable predictive accuracy, substantial differences were observed in their feature importance rankings. Solution concentration emerged as the most robust and consistently influential parameter (variability = 0), whereas flow rate and applied voltage exhibited high ranking variability (variability > 0.9), indicating strong model dependence. These findings suggest that feature importance derived from a single ML model may be unreliable, particularly for small experimental datasets, and highlight the importance of cross-model validation for achieving trustworthy interpretation in ML-assisted electrospinning research.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates 21 ML models on 96 PVA electrospinning experiments, showing that feature importance rankings vary significantly across models even when predictive performance is similar, with solution concentration being the most robust parameter while flow rate and voltage show high variability.
AI.motivation: Existing electrospinning ML studies rely on single models, assuming feature importance is robust. This work questions that assumption and systematically evaluates cross-model consistency of interpretability.
AI.method: 96 PVA electrospinning experiments dataset; 21 ML models from 5 families (linear, tree, kernel, neural network, instance-based); SHAP values for unified feature importance; rank-based statistical analysis to quantify inter-model agreement.
AI.result: Predictive performance and interpretive reliability are distinct; models with similar accuracy produced different feature rankings; solution concentration had variability=0 (most robust), while flow rate and voltage had variability>0.9 (highly model-dependent).
AI.conclusion: Single-model feature importance may be unreliable, especially for small datasets; cross-model validation is essential for trustworthy ML-assisted electrospinning research.

title: Breaking the Quality-Privacy Tradeoff in Tabular Data Generation via In-Context Learning
summary: Tabular data synthesis aims to generate high-quality data while preserving privacy. However, we find that existing tabular generative models exhibit a clear tradeoff in the small-data regime: improving data quality typically comes at the cost of increased memorization of training samples, thereby weakening privacy protection. This tradeoff arises because small training sets make it difficult for dataset-specific generative models to distinguish generalizable structure from sample-specific patterns. To address this, we propose DiffICL, which formulates tabular data generation as an in-context learning problem. Instead of fitting each dataset from scratch,DiffICL leverages pretrained structural priors learned from a large collection of datasets, enabling it to infer data distributions from limited context rather than memorizing individual samples. We evaluate DiffICL on 14 real-world datasets. Results show that DiffICL improves both data quality and privacy, and generate synthetic data that provides effective data augmentation. Our findings suggest that the quality-privacy tradeoff can be improved through better training paradigms.

AI.tldr: DiffICL improves tabular data generation by using in-context learning with pretrained structural priors, breaking the quality-privacy tradeoff in small-data regimes.
AI.motivation: Existing tabular generative models face a quality-privacy tradeoff in small-data regimes — improving data quality increases memorization of training samples, weakening privacy protection.
AI.method: DiffICL formulates tabular data generation as an in-context learning problem, leveraging pretrained structural priors from a large collection of datasets to infer distributions from limited context rather than memorizing individual samples.
AI.result: Evaluated on 14 real-world datasets, DiffICL improves both data quality and privacy, and provides effective data augmentation.
AI.conclusion: The quality-privacy tradeoff can be improved through better training paradigms (specifically, in-context learning with pretrained priors).

title: Reinforcement Learning for Compositional Generalization with Outcome-Level Optimization
summary: Compositional generalization refers to correctly interpret novel combinations of known primitives, which remains a major challenge. Existing approaches often rely on supervised fine-tuning, which encourages models to imitate target outputs. This token-level training paradigm fails to capture the global compositional structure required for generalizing to unseen combinations. In this work, we investigate whether compositional generalization can instead be improved through outcome-level reinforcement learning. We adopt Group Relative Policy Optimization to optimize models based on feedback on their final outputs. Within this framework, we explore both a simple binary outcome reward and a composite reward that provides additional composition feedback. Experiments on multiple compositional benchmarks show that reinforcement learning improves compositional generalization compared to supervised fine-tuning. Further analysis reveals that supervised models tend to overfit frequent training compositions, whereas reinforcement learning improves compositional generalization by reshaping the output distribution, particularly for more complex composition types.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that reinforcement learning (using GRPO) improves compositional generalization over supervised fine-tuning by reshaping output distributions and reducing overfitting to frequent training compositions.
AI.motivation: Existing supervised fine-tuning approaches fail to capture global compositional structures needed for generalizing to unseen combinations of known primitives, motivating the need for alternative training paradigms.
AI.method: The authors adopt Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with outcome-level reinforcement learning, exploring both a simple binary outcome reward and a composite reward with additional composition feedback.
AI.result: RL improves compositional generalization across multiple benchmarks compared to supervised fine-tuning; supervised models overfit frequent training compositions while RL reshapes output distributions, especially for complex composition types.
AI.conclusion: Outcome-level reinforcement learning is a promising alternative to token-level supervised fine-tuning for improving compositional generalization.

title: Training-Time Batch Normalization Reshapes Local Partition Geometry in Piecewise-Affine Networks
summary: Batch normalization (BN) is central to modern deep networks, but its effect on the realized function during training remains less understood than its optimization benefits. We study training-time BN in continuous piecewise-affine (CPA) networks through the geometry of switching hyperplanes and the induced affine-region partition. Conditioned on a mini-batch, we show that BN defines for each neuron a reference hyperplane through the batch centroid, and that breakpoint-switching hyperplanes are parallel translates whose offsets are expressed in batch-standardized coordinates and are independent of the raw bias. This yields an exact criterion for when a switching hyperplane intersects a local $\ell_\infty$ window and motivates a local region-density functional based on exact affine-region counts. Under explicit sufficient conditions, we show that BN increases expected local partition refinement in ReLU and more general piecewise-affine networks, and that this mechanism transfers locally through depth inside parent affine regions where the upstream representation map is an affine embedding. These results provide a function-level geometric account of training-time BN as a batch-conditional recentering mechanism near the data.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a geometric analysis of how batch normalization affects the learned function in piecewise-affine networks during training, showing it acts as a batch-conditional recentering mechanism that locally refines the affine-region partition.
AI.motivation: While batch normalization's optimization benefits are well-studied, its effect on the realized function during training is less understood. The authors aim to bridge this gap by analyzing BN through the geometry of switching hyperplanes and region partitions.
AI.method: The authors analyze BN in continuous piecewise-affine (CPA) networks by studying switching hyperplanes and affine-region partitions conditioned on mini-batches. They derive exact criteria for hyperplane intersection with local windows and propose a region-density functional based on exact affine-region counts.
AI.result: BN defines reference hyperplanes through batch centroids; switching hyperplanes are parallel translates with offsets in batch-standardized coordinates. Under sufficient conditions, BN increases expected local partition refinement in ReLU and general CPA networks, and this mechanism transfers locally through depth via affine embeddings.
AI.conclusion: Training-time BN acts as a batch-conditional recentering mechanism near the data, geometrically refining the function's affine-region partition locally—providing a function-level geometric explanation beyond its known optimization benefits.

title: Adaptive Inverted-Index Routing for Granular Mixtures-of-Experts
summary: Mixture-of-experts (MoE) models enable scalable transformer architectures by activating only a subset of experts per token. Recent evidence suggests that performance improves with increasingly granular experts, i.e., many small experts instead of a few large ones. However, this regime substantially increases routing cost, which can dominate computation. We introduce adaptive inverted-index routing for MoE (AIR-MoE), an inverted-index-inspired routing architecture based on vector quantization (VQ). In a first stage, AIR-MoE performs coarse shortlisting by assigning tokens to VQ codewords to construct a candidate set of experts. In a second stage, fine scoring computes exact routing scores restricted to this shortlist. This two-stage procedure approximates true top-k routing while avoiding full expert scoring and, in contrast to prior work, imposing no structural constraints on expert parameters. AIR-MoE serves as a drop-in replacement for standard routers and requires no modifications to the model architecture or loss function. We further provide a lower bound on the mass recall achieved by AIR-MoE that yields insights into its inner workings. Empirically, we demonstrate that AIR-MoE achieves improved performance compared to existing routing approaches in granular MoE settings.

AI.tldr: AIR-MoE introduces a two-stage inverted-index routing mechanism using vector quantization to efficiently approximate top-k expert selection in granular MoE models, reducing routing costs while improving performance without architectural constraints.
AI.motivation: Granular MoE (many small experts) improves performance but substantially increases routing costs, which can dominate computation. Prior routing approaches impose structural constraints on expert parameters or require full expert scoring.
AI.method: Two-stage routing: (1) Coarse shortlisting assigns tokens to VQ codewords to construct a candidate expert set via an inverted index. (2) Fine scoring computes exact routing scores only for the shortlisted experts. This approximates top-k routing without full expert scoring or structural constraints.
AI.result: AIR-MoE achieves improved performance compared to existing routing approaches in granular MoE settings. The paper also provides a theoretical lower bound on mass recall, offering insights into the method's inner workings.
AI.conclusion: AIR-MoE serves as an effective drop-in replacement for standard routers, requiring no modifications to model architecture or loss function, and is particularly beneficial for granular MoE regimes where routing cost is a bottleneck.

title: Order-based Rehearsal Learning
summary: When a machine learning (ML) model forecasts an undesired event, one often seeks a decision to avoid it, known as the avoiding undesired future (AUF) problem. Many rehearsal learning methods have been proposed for AUF, but they rely on an underlying graph structure; learning such a graph from observational data is challenging and can incur substantial estimation error. In this work, we demonstrate that the order structure can be sufficient for AUF decision-making, and propose the first order-based rehearsal learning method. Although an order is less informative than a graph, it can be sufficient to identify the influence of decisions from observational data, suggesting that learning the entire graph is not always necessary. To learn the order, we develop an information-theoretic method that imposes no restrictions on the form of structural functions or the type of noise distributions. For AUF decision-making, we construct an order-based sampler to approximate the influence of decisions and, combined with a surrogate objective for maximizing the post-decision success probability, reduce the AUF task to a differentiable optimization problem. Experiments show that our order learning method outperforms existing methods, and that our AUF approach not only surpasses methods relying on learned graphs or learned orders, but also matches or even exceeds oracle baselines that are given the true graph.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel order-based rehearsal learning method for the AUF problem, showing that learning an order (rather than a full graph) is sufficient for decision-making, and develops an information-theoretic order learning approach that achieves competitive results.
AI.motivation: Existing rehearsal learning methods for AUF rely on graph structures, which are difficult to learn from observational data and prone to estimation errors. The authors aim to show that a simpler order structure suffices for this task.
AI.method: The authors propose an information-theoretic method for learning order structures (without restrictions on function forms or noise distributions), develop an order-based sampler to approximate decision influence, and reduce AUF to a differentiable optimization problem using a surrogate objective.
AI.result: The order learning method outperforms existing methods. The AUF approach surpasses methods relying on learned graphs or learned orders, and matches/exceeds oracle baselines with the true graph.
AI.conclusion: Learning the entire causal graph is not always necessary for AUF; an order structure can be sufficient, offering a simpler and more robust alternative for decision-making under uncertainty.

title: KernelBench-X: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating LLM-Generated GPU Kernels
summary: LLM-based Triton kernel generation has attracted significant interest, yet a fundamental empirical question remains unanswered: where does this capability break down, and why? We present KernelBench-X, a benchmark designed to answer this question through category-aware evaluation of correctness and hardware efficiency across 176 tasks in 15 categories. Our systematic comparison of five representative methods yields three main findings. First, task structure determines correctness more than method design. Category explains nearly three times more variance in semantic correctness than method (9.4% vs 3.3% explained deviance), and 72% of Fusion tasks fail across all five methods while Math tasks are solved consistently. Second, iterative refinement improves correctness, but not performance. Across GEAK iterations, compile rate rises from 52.3% to 68.8% while average speedup declines from $1.58\times$ to $1.44\times$; newly rescued kernels consistently underperform persistently correct ones ($1.16\times$ vs $1.58\times$ speedup in round~0$\to$1). Third, correctness does not imply efficiency. 46.6% of correct kernels are slower than the PyTorch eager baseline, and cross-hardware speedup variance reaches $21.4\times$. Besides, quantization remains completely unsolved (0/30 successes) despite non-trivial compilation rates, revealing systematic misunderstanding of numerical computation contracts rather than surface-level syntax errors. These findings suggest that future progress depends on handling global coordination, explicitly modeling numerical precision, and incorporating hardware efficiency into generation. The code is available at https://github.com/BonnieW05/KernelBenchX

AI.tldr: KernelBench-X evaluates LLM-based Triton kernel generation across 176 tasks, finding that task structure affects correctness more than method design, iterative refinement improves compilation but degrades speed, and correctness often fails to guarantee hardware efficiency.
AI.motivation: To understand where and why LLM-based Triton kernel generation breaks down, as a fundamental empirical question remains unanswered despite growing interest.
AI.method: They built KernelBench-X, a benchmark of 176 tasks in 15 categories, and systematically compared five representative generation methods using category-aware evaluation of correctness and hardware efficiency.
AI.result: (1) Task structure explains 9.4% of correctness variance vs 3.3% for method; (2) Iterative refinement raises compile rate from 52.3% to 68.8% but average speedup drops from 1.58x to 1.44x; (3) 46.6% of correct kernels are slower than PyTorch eager baseline; quantization has 0/30 successes.
AI.conclusion: Future progress depends on handling global coordination, explicitly modeling numerical precision, and incorporating hardware efficiency into generation.

title: Delving into Non-Exchangeability for Conformal Prediction in Graph-Structured Multivariate Time Series
summary: Point forecasting for graph-structured multivariate time series is a fundamental problem, but rigorous uncertainty quantification for such predictions is still underexplored. Conformal prediction (CP) offers uncertainty estimation with a solid coverage guarantee under the exchangeability assumption, which requires the joint data distribution to be unchanged under permutation. However, in graph-structured time series, inherent cross-node coupling can violate the exchangeability condition, making direct application of CP unreliable. Inspired by the spectral graph theory, such coupling resides in global trends and can be characterized by the low-frequency components, while high-frequency components are nearly exchangeable. Therefore, we propose a novel concept named Spectral Graph Conditional Exchangeability (SGCE), which conditions exchangeable high-frequency components on low-frequency ones to preserve global trends and enable effective CP in the spectral domain. Based on SGCE, we further propose Spectral Conformal prediction via wAveLEt transform (SCALE). SCALE uses graph wavelets to decompose low/high-frequency components and conformalizes high-frequency residuals via adaptive gating over a low-frequency embedding. Experimental results on real-world traffic datasets show that SCALE not only achieves valid coverage but also consistently improves the coverage-efficiency trade-off over the state-of-the-art CP methods.

AI.tldr: SCALE is a novel conformal prediction method for graph-structured time series that uses spectral decomposition via graph wavelets to achieve valid uncertainty quantification despite cross-node coupling violating exchangeability.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty quantification for graph-structured multivariate time series is underexplored. Standard conformal prediction fails because cross-node coupling violates the exchangeability assumption.
AI.method: Introduces Spectral Graph Conditional Exchangeability (SGCE) to condition exchangeable high-frequency components on low-frequency ones. SCALE uses graph wavelets to decompose frequencies and conformalizes high-frequency residuals via adaptive gating over low-frequency embedding.
AI.result: On real-world traffic datasets, SCALE achieves valid coverage and consistently improves coverage-efficiency trade-off over state-of-the-art CP methods.
AI.conclusion: The spectral-domain approach effectively handles non-exchangeability in graph-structured time series, enabling reliable conformal prediction with tighter intervals.

title: Conceptors for Semantic Steering
summary: Activation-based steering provides control of LLM behavior at inference time, but the dominant paradigm reduces each concept to a single direction whose geometry is left largely unexamined. Rather than selecting a single steering direction, we use conceptors: soft projection matrices estimated from activations pooled across both poles of a bipolar concept, which preserve the concept's full multidimensional subspace. A geometric analysis shows the bipolar subspace strictly subsumes the single-vector baseline. We further show that the conceptor quota provides a parameter-free layer-selection diagnostic, predicting concept separability with Pearson correlations up to r=0.96 across three instruction-tuned models and three semantic dimensions. Beyond selection, conceptors admit a closed-form Boolean algebra (AND, OR, NOT): we evaluate conceptor compositionality on thematically related sub-concepts. Across a systematic five-axis design-space evaluation, conceptors match or outperform additive baselines at layers where concept subspaces are multi-dimensional while producing substantially fewer degenerate outputs. Conceptor steering is a geometrically principled, compositional, and practically safer alternative to single-direction steering from a limited number of contrastive pairs.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces conceptors (soft projection matrices) as an alternative to single-direction steering for controlling LLM behavior, showing they better capture multidimensional concept subspaces, enable Boolean composition, and outperform baselines with fewer degenerate outputs.
AI.motivation: The dominant paradigm for activation-based steering reduces each concept to a single direction whose geometry is largely unexamined, limiting expressivity and safety.
AI.method: The authors use conceptors (soft projection matrices) estimated from activations pooled across both poles of a bipolar concept, preserving the full multidimensional subspace. They also introduce a parameter-free layer-selection diagnostic (conceptor quota) and a closed-form Boolean algebra for composition.
AI.result: The bipolar subspace subsumes single-vector baselines; the conceptor quota predicts concept separability with Pearson correlations up to r=0.96; conceptors match/outperform additive baselines at multi-dimensional layers while producing substantially fewer degenerate outputs.
AI.conclusion: Conceptor steering is a geometrically principled, compositional, and practically safer alternative to single-direction steering from limited contrastive pairs.

title: Self-Induced Outcome Potential: Turn-Level Credit Assignment for Agents without Verifiers
summary: Long-horizon LLM agents depend on intermediate information-gathering turns, yet training feedback is usually observed only at the final answer, because process-level rewards require high-quality human annotation. Existing turn-level shaping methods reward turns that increase the likelihood of a gold answer, but they require answer supervision or stable task-specific verifiers. Conversely, label-free RL methods extract self-signals from output distributions, but mainly at the answer or trajectory level and therefore cannot assign credit to intermediate turns. We propose Self-Induced Outcome Potential (SIOP), which treats semantic clusters of final answers as latent future outcome states for potential-based turn-level credit assignment. For each query, SIOP samples multiple rollouts, clusters final answers into semantic outcome modes, and builds a reliability-aware target distribution over these states. It then rewards turns for increasing posterior support for reliable future states using a tractable cluster-level approximation. The objective generalizes information-potential shaping from gold-answer supervision to settings without task-specific gold verifiers while avoiding the broadcasted rollout-level advantages used by standard GRPO. We formalize the framework, characterize its supervised gold-answer limit, and show that SIOP improves average performance over verifier-free outcome-level baselines on seven search-augmented agentic reasoning benchmarks while approaching a gold-supervised outcome baseline. Code is available at https://github.com/dl-m9/SIOP.git.

AI.tldr: SIOP is a verifier-free RL method that assigns turn-level credit to LLM agents by clustering final answers into semantic outcome modes and rewarding turns that increase posterior support for reliable future states, improving performance on search-augmented agentic reasoning benchmarks without requiring human-annotated process rewards or gold answer supervision.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents need intermediate feedback but process-level rewards require costly human annotation, while existing turn-level shaping methods need gold answer supervision or task-specific verifiers, and label-free RL methods only provide answer/trajectory-level signals that cannot credit intermediate turns.
AI.method: SIOP samples multiple rollouts per query, clusters final answers into semantic outcome modes, builds a reliability-aware target distribution over these states, and rewards turns for increasing posterior support for reliable future states via tractable cluster-level approximation, generalizing information-potential shaping from gold-answer supervision to verifier-free settings.
AI.result: SIOP improves average performance over verifier-free outcome-level baselines on seven search-augmented agentic reasoning benchmarks and approaches a gold-supervised outcome baseline.
AI.conclusion: The framework formalizes turn-level credit assignment without requiring gold-answer supervision or task-specific verifiers, showing that semantic clustering of final answers can serve as latent outcome states for effective potential-based reward shaping.

title: Adaptivity Under Realizability Constraints: Comparing In-Context and Agentic Learning
summary: We compare in-context learning with fixed queries and agentic learning with adaptive queries for uniform approximation of task families. We consider two settings: an unrestricted regime, where querying and approximation are arbitrary functions, and a realizable regime, where we require these operations to be implemented by ReLU neural networks. In both settings, adaptivity never hinders approximation performance. However, this advantage can change when one passes from the unrestricted regime to the realizable regime. We identify four distinct approximation scenarios, each witnessed by an explicit task family: (a) no advantage of adaptivity; (b) an advantage in the unrestricted regime that persists under ReLU realizability; (c) an advantage that arises only under realizability; and (d) an advantage that disappears under realizability. This demonstrates that representational constraints interact profoundly with the effect of adaptivity.

AI.tldr: This paper compares in-context learning (fixed queries) with agentic learning (adaptive queries) for approximating task families, examining both unrestricted and realizable (ReLU neural network) regimes. It identifies four distinct scenarios regarding whether adaptivity provides an advantage, showing that representational constraints significantly impact the benefits of adaptivity.
AI.motivation: To understand how adaptivity (adaptive queries versus fixed queries) affects approximation performance in learning, and whether this advantage holds under real-world neural network constraints.
AI.method: The paper theoretically compares in-context learning (fixed queries) and agentic learning (adaptive queries) in two settings: unrestricted (arbitrary functions) and realizable (ReLU neural network implementations). It constructs explicit task families to demonstrate four distinct approximation scenarios.
AI.result: Four scenarios identified: (a) no advantage of adaptivity; (b) advantage in unrestricted regime persists under realizability; (c) advantage arises only under realizability; (d) advantage disappears under realizability.
AI.conclusion: Adaptivity never hurts approximation, but its benefits depend critically on representational constraints. The transition from unrestricted to realizable regimes can create, eliminate, or preserve adaptivity advantages.

title: DualTCN: A Physics-Constrained Temporal Convolutional Network for 2 Time-Domain Marine CSEM Inversion
summary:
DualTCN is the first deep-learning framework for inverting time-domain marine controlled-source electromagnetic (MCSEM) transient data. Moving away from traditional subsurface discretization, the framework regresses four earth-model parameters -- $σ_1$, $σ_2$, $d_1$, $d_2$ -- and reconstructs conductivity-depth profiles using a differentiable soft-step decoder. The optimized architecture (379K parameters) features a Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) encoder paired with a late-time branch and an auxiliary seafloor-depth head. This design achieves a 25.3\% loss reduction over baseline models, with high predictive accuracy ($R^2 = 0.898$ for $σ_2$) and an inversion speed of 3.5~ms per sample on an A100 GPU.
  The framework demonstrates high robustness to noise through curriculum-based amplitude augmentation, maintaining a mean $\bar{R}^2$ of 0.858 at $\pm2\%$ random amplitude error, compared to $0.363$ without augmentation. DualTCN generalizes effectively to three-layer extensions (seawater/resistive layer/basement), accurately resolving basement conductivity ($R^2 \approx 0.88$), though thin-layer resolution remains a physical limitation ($R^2 \approx 0.23$).
  In comparative benchmarks, DualTCN significantly outperforms traditional local optimization methods like Levenberg-Marquardt and L-BFGS-B, yielding a mean $\bar{R}^2 = 0.877$ versus 0.129-0.439 for multi-start baselines, while operating at up to 21,000$\times$ lower computational cost. Finally, the framework incorporates uncertainty quantification via Monte Carlo (MC) Dropout. While well-calibrated for $σ_1$ (PICP90 = 0.944), inherent signal limitations at short offsets (200m) lead to under-coverage for $d_2$ (PICP90 = 0.572), which can be mitigated through post-hoc temperature scaling or split conformal prediction.

AI.tldr: DualTCN is a deep-learning framework for inverting time-domain MCSEM data, using a TCN encoder with late-time branch and seafloor-depth head to regress four earth-model parameters. It achieves 25.3% loss reduction, high accuracy (R²=0.898), 3.5ms/sample speed, noise robustness via augmentation, generalization to three-layer models, and significantly outperforms traditional optimization methods (R²=0.877 vs 0.129-0.439) at 21,000× lower cost, with uncertainty quantification via MC Dropout.
AI.motivation: To develop a fast, accurate, and robust deep-learning alternative to traditional local optimization methods for inverting time-domain MCSEM transient data, overcoming computational cost and resolution limitations.
AI.method: DualTCN uses a Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) encoder with a late-time branch and auxiliary seafloor-depth head, regressing four parameters (σ₁, σ₂, d₁, d₂) and reconstructing conductivity-depth profiles via a differentiable soft-step decoder. Curriculum-based amplitude augmentation is used for noise robustness, and MC Dropout for uncertainty quantification.
AI.result: 25.3% loss reduction over baselines; R²=0.898 for σ₂; inversion speed 3.5ms/sample; mean R²=0.858 at ±2% noise with augmentation vs 0.363 without; generalizes to three-layer models (R²≈0.88 for basement conductivity); outperforms Levenberg-Marquardt/L-BFGS-B (R²=0.877 vs 0.129-0.439) at 21,000× lower cost; well-calibrated uncertainty for σ₁ (PICP90=0.944) but under-coverage for d₂ (PICP90=0.572).
AI.conclusion: DualTCN provides a highly efficient, accurate, and robust deep-learning solution for MCSEM inversion, with orders-of-magnitude speedup over traditional methods and built-in uncertainty quantification, though thin-layer resolution remains a physical limitation.

title: Learned Neighbor Trust for Collaborative Deployment in Model-Agnostic Decentralized Learning
summary: Many decentralized distillation methods are designed around training-time coordination, yet deploy each node in isolation even when more capable neighbors remain available at inference time. This is an incomplete objective for settings such as IoT, where devices are heterogeneous, data is scarce and skewed, and a node's strongest neighbors may far exceed its own local capacity. We study how nodes should train so that their predictions compose well at deployment, and how each node should learn whom to trust. Under a server-free, model-agnostic protocol where nodes exchange only queries and soft predictions, we propose Learned Neighbor Trust (LNTrust) wherein each node learns a compact trust function over its neighborhood from local validation evidence. This trust function gates auxiliary distillation during training and defines a deployment ensemble at inference, so that collaboration learned during training transfers directly to deployment. Across datasets and topologies, LNTrust improves deployed accuracy over the strongest output-only baseline by large margins while using significantly less communication than previous methods.

AI.tldr: LNTrust enables decentralized nodes to learn trust-based collaboration during training that transfers directly to inference, improving accuracy with less communication.
AI.motivation: Existing decentralized distillation methods focus on training-time coordination but ignore inference-time collaboration, which is suboptimal for heterogeneous IoT settings where strong neighbors exist.
AI.method: Each node learns a compact trust function over its neighborhood from local validation data, which gates auxiliary distillation during training and defines an ensemble at deployment.
AI.result: LNTrust improves deployed accuracy over the strongest output-only baseline by large margins while using significantly less communication than previous methods.
AI.conclusion: Training nodes to learn whom to trust enables effective composition of predictions at inference time in heterogeneous, server-free settings.

title: Graph-SND: Sparse Aggregation for Behavioral Diversity in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: System Neural Diversity (SND) measures behavioral heterogeneity in multi-agent reinforcement learning by averaging pairwise distances over all $\binom{n}{2}$ agent pairs, making each call quadratic in team size. We introduce Graph-SND, which replaces this complete-graph average with a weighted average over the edges of an arbitrary graph $G$. Three regimes follow: $G=K_n$ recovers SND exactly; a fixed sparse $G$ defines a localized diversity measure at $O(|E|)$ cost; and random edge samples yield an unbiased Horvitz-Thompson estimator and a normalized sample mean with $O(1/\sqrt{m})$ concentration in the sampled edge count $m$. For fixed sparse graphs we prove forwarding-index distortion bounds for expanders and a spectral refinement under low-rank distance structure; for random $d$-regular graphs we prove an unconditional probabilistic $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(D_{\max}/\sqrt{n})$ bound. On VMAS we verify recovery, unbiasedness, concentration, and wall-clock scaling, with a PettingZoo TVD panel checking non-Gaussian transfer. In a 500-iteration $n=100$ PPO run, Bernoulli-$0.1$ Graph-SND tracks full SND while reducing per-call metric time by about $10\times$, and frozen-policy GPU timing up to $n=500$ follows the predicted $\binom{n}{2}/|E|$ speedup. Random $d$-regular expanders empirically achieve $\mathrm{SND}_{G}^{\mathrm{u}}/\mathrm{SND} \in [0.9987, 1.0013]$ at $Θ(n \log n)$ edges. In DiCo diversity control at $n=50$, Bernoulli-$0.1$ Graph-SND preserves set-point tracking with paired reward differences indistinguishable from zero across nine matched cells while cutting per-call metric cost by ${\sim}9.5\times$. Together, these results show that the SND aggregation bottleneck can be removed without changing the metric's semantics, yielding a drop-in sparse alternative that scales beyond complete-graph SND and supports both passive measurement and closed-loop diversity control.

AI.tldr: Graph-SND replaces the quadratic-complexity complete-graph averaging in System Neural Diversity (SND) with weighted averaging over an arbitrary graph, achieving O(|E|) cost. It offers three regimes: exact recovery (complete graph), localized diversity (fixed sparse graph), and unbiased estimation (random edges). Theoretical guarantees include distortion bounds for expanders, spectral refinement, and O(1/√m) concentration. Experiments show ~10× speedup with near-identical values, and scaling up to n=500 agents.
AI.motivation: SND's quadratic O(n²) cost in team size creates a scalability bottleneck for multi-agent RL with many agents. The authors aim to remove this bottleneck without altering the metric's semantics, enabling SND to scale to large agent teams for both passive measurement and closed-loop diversity control.
AI.method: Graph-SND replaces the complete-graph average (over all n(n-1)/2 agent pairs) with a weighted average over edges of an arbitrary graph G. Three regimes: (1) G=K_n recovers original SND exactly; (2) fixed sparse G yields localized diversity at O(|E|) cost; (3) random edge sampling produces unbiased Horvitz-Thompson estimator with O(1/√m) concentration. Theoretical analysis covers forwarding-index distortion bounds for expanders and spectral refinement. Implementation uses Bernoulli sampling or random d-regular graphs.
AI.result: On VMAS: recovery, unbiasedness, concentration, and wall-clock scaling verified. In n=100 PPO run, Bernoulli-0.1 Graph-SND tracks full SND with ~10× per-call speedup. GPU timing up to n=500 confirms predicted O(n²/|E|) speedup. Random d-regular expanders achieve SND_G_u/SND ∈ [0.9987, 1.0013] at Θ(n log n) edges. In DiCo diversity control at n=50, Bernoulli-0.1 preserves set-point tracking with ~9.5× cost reduction, with reward differences indistinguishable from zero.
AI.conclusion: The SND aggregation bottleneck can be removed without changing the metric's semantics. Graph-SND provides a drop-in sparse alternative that scales beyond complete-graph SND, supporting both passive measurement and closed-loop diversity control in multi-agent RL.

title: CuBridge: An LLM-Based Framework for Understanding and Reconstructing High-Performance Attention Kernels
summary:
Efficient CUDA implementations of attention mechanisms are critical to modern deep learning systems, yet supporting diverse and evolving attention variants remains challenging. Existing frameworks and compilers trade performance for flexibility, while expert-written kernels achieve high efficiency but are difficult to adapt. Recent work explores large language models (LLMs) for GPU kernel generation, but prior studies report unstable correctness and significant performance gaps for complex operators such as attention.
  We present CuBridge, an LLM-based framework that adapts expert-written attention kernels through a structured lift-transfer-lower workflow. CuBridge starts from expert-written CUDA attention kernels and lifts them into an executable intermediate representation that makes execution orchestration explicit while abstracting low-level CUDA syntax. Given a user-provided PyTorch specification, CuBridge generates and verifies a target IR program, then reconstructs optimized CUDA code via reference-guided lowering. Across diverse attention variants and GPU platforms, CuBridge consistently produces correct kernels and substantially outperforms general frameworks, compiler-based approaches, and prior LLM-based methods.

AI.tldr: CuBridge is an LLM-based framework that adapts expert-written CUDA attention kernels using a lift-transfer-lower workflow, achieving correct and highly efficient kernels across diverse attention variants and GPU platforms.
AI.motivation: Efficient CUDA implementations of attention mechanisms are critical but challenging due to the trade-off between flexibility (existing frameworks/compilers) and performance (expert-written kernels). LLM-based kernel generation has shown unstable correctness and performance gaps for complex operators like attention.
AI.method: CuBridge uses a structured lift-transfer-lower workflow: (1) lifts expert-written CUDA kernels into an executable intermediate representation (IR), (2) generates and verifies a target IR program from a user-provided PyTorch specification, and (3) reconstructs optimized CUDA code via reference-guided lowering.
AI.result: Across diverse attention variants and GPU platforms, CuBridge consistently produces correct kernels and substantially outperforms general frameworks, compiler-based approaches, and prior LLM-based methods.
AI.conclusion: CuBridge demonstrates that combining LLMs with structured intermediate representations can bridge the gap between flexibility and performance for attention kernel generation, enabling automated adaptation of expert-written kernels.

title: The Predictive-Causal Gap: An Impossibility Theorem and Large-Scale Neural Evidence
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures, 3 tables. Supplemental Material included (Sections S1-S10)
summary: We report a systematic failure mode in predictive representation learning. Across 2695 neural network configurations trained to predict linear-Gaussian dynamics, the optimal encoder tracks the environment rather than the system it is meant to model. The mean causal fidelity -- the fraction of encoder sensitivity allocated to system degrees of freedom -- is 0.49, and only 2.5% of configurations exceed 0.70. The failure intensifies with dimension: at N=100, the optimal encoder becomes causally blind (fidelity ~10^{-8}) while achieving 92% lower prediction error than the causal representation. We prove this is not an optimization artifact but a structural property of the predictive objective: when environment modes are slower or less noisy than system modes, every minimizer of the population risk encodes the former. The set of dynamics exhibiting this predictive-causal gap is open and of positive measure in parameter space. In a nonlinear Duffing-GRU sweep, unconstrained predictors learn environment-dominant representations in 55% of tasks (95% CI 41--68%) versus 24% under operational grounding (p=2.3e-3); the median out-of-distribution MSE inflation under environment shift is 1.82x versus 1.00x. Operational grounding -- restricting the loss to system observables -- partially suppresses the gap, but causal fidelity is never recovered without an explicit system-environment boundary. The results identify the predictive-causal gap as a structural limit of learning, with implications for self-supervised representation learning, world models, and the scaling paradigm.

AI.tldr: Predictive representation learning systematically learns environment dynamics instead of system dynamics, causing a "predictive-causal gap" where optimal encoders track the environment rather than the system they should model, and this failure is structural (not an optimization artifact) and worsens with dimensionality.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether predictive representation learning (a widely used paradigm in self-supervised learning and world models) actually learns causal representations of the system of interest, or whether it systematically defaults to modeling environmental dynamics instead.
AI.method: The authors train 2695 neural network configurations on linear-Gaussian dynamics, measuring "causal fidelity" (fraction of encoder sensitivity to system degrees of freedom). They prove theoretically that when environment modes are slower/less noisy, all population risk minimizers encode the environment. They also test nonlinear Duffing-GRU tasks with/without operational grounding.
AI.result: Mean causal fidelity is 0.49; only 2.5% exceed 0.70. At N=100, causal fidelity drops to ~10^-8 while prediction error is 92% lower than causal representation. In nonlinear tasks, 55% learn environment-dominant representations vs 24% with operational grounding. Median OOD MSE inflation is 1.82x vs 1.00x.
AI.conclusion: The predictive-causal gap is a structural limit of predictive learning, not an optimization artifact. Simply restricting to a predictive loss cannot recover causal representations without explicitly enforcing a system-environment boundary. This has serious implications for self-supervised learning, world models, and the scaling paradigm.

title: Full-chip CMP modelling based on Fully Convolutional Network leveraging White Light Interferometry
comment: Presented at the International Conference on Planarization Technology 2025 in Hong Kong
summary: As time-to-market is crucial in the Integrated Circuit (IC) industry, speeding up layout manufacturability verifi-cation is essential. Chemical-Mechanical Polishing (CMP) plays a vital role in IC fabrication but is significantly influenced by Layout-Dependent Effects (LDE). An accurate and efficient CMP model enables design teams to correct surface unevenness before fabrication, reducing costs and accelerating the design phase. However, existing models often rely on Density Step Height (DSH) modeling, which is time-consuming for calibration and requires substantial hardware resources for fine-grained predictions. In this paper, we propose combining the advantages of two surface analysis techniques, White Light Interfer-ometry (WLI) and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), to train a deep learning model. This model aims to predict full-chip post-CMP nanotopography with nanometer-scale accuracy. Our deep learning model is based on a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and follows a two-step pipeline. The model is trained on each technique separately, resulting in a detailed full-chip CMP model.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a deep learning (CNN-based) model combining WLI and AFM surface analysis data to predict full-chip post-CMP nanotopography with nanometer accuracy, overcoming the slowness and resource demands of traditional DSH-based CMP models.
AI.motivation: Existing CMP models rely on Density Step Height (DSH) modeling, which is time-consuming to calibrate and requires substantial hardware resources for fine-grained predictions. Faster, more efficient CMP verification is needed to speed up IC layout manufacturability verification and reduce time-to-market.
AI.method: A CNN-based deep learning model with a two-step pipeline, trained separately on data from two surface analysis techniques: White Light Interferometry (WLI) and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM).
AI.result: The model achieves full-chip post-CMP nanotopography prediction with nanometer-scale accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Combining WLI and AFM data via a CNN-based deep learning approach provides an accurate and efficient alternative to traditional DSH-based CMP modeling, potentially reducing costs and accelerating the IC design phase.

title: Provable imitation learning for control of instability in partially-observed Vlasov--Poisson equations
summary: We consider the stabilization of Vlasov--Poisson plasma dynamics, a central control problem in nuclear fusion. Our focus is the gap between what an ideal controller would use and what experiments can actually observe: while optimal policy may rely on the full phase-space state, practical feedback is typically limited to sparse macroscopic diagnostics. We therefore study imitation learning methods that distill a fully observed expert policy into controllers operating only on macroscopic measurements. We show the stability guarantees of the learned policy, where the error floor depends on the minimal behavior cloning loss achievable under the observation constraints. We further characterize this minimal loss in terms of a notion of entropy that quantifies the complexity of the initial distribution. Our results demonstrates the theoretical feasibility of learning stabilizing feedback policies for kinetic plasma dynamics from macroscopic observations, and exhibits the adaptivity of the learning approach to low-complexity structures. Through extensive numerical experiments, we validate our theory and show that the learned policies can stabilize the system using only macroscopic observations, within a significantly longer time horizon than non-adaptive baseline controllers.

AI.tldr: This paper studies imitation learning for stabilizing Vlasov-Poisson plasma dynamics using only macroscopic observations, proving stability guarantees and characterizing the minimal achievable error via behavior cloning loss and entropy measures.
AI.motivation: There is a critical gap in nuclear fusion control: optimal stabilization of Vlasov-Poisson plasma dynamics requires full phase-space state information, but practical experiments are limited to sparse macroscopic diagnostics. The authors aim to bridge this gap.
AI.method: The authors employ imitation learning to distill a fully observed expert policy into controllers that rely only on macroscopic measurements. They derive stability guarantees, characterize minimal behavior cloning loss using entropy, and validate through numerical experiments.
AI.result: The learned policies provide stability guarantees where the error floor depends on the minimal behavior cloning loss achievable under observation constraints. The minimal loss is characterized by an entropy measure of initial distribution complexity. Numerical experiments show the learned policies stabilize the system longer than non-adaptive baselines.
AI.conclusion: Learning stabilizing feedback policies for kinetic plasma dynamics from macroscopic observations is theoretically feasible. The approach adapts to low-complexity structures and outperforms non-adaptive baselines in stabilization horizon.

title: Order Matters: Improving Domain Adaptation by Reordering Data
summary: Domain shift remains a key challenge in deploying machine learning models to the real world. Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) aims to address this by minimising domain discrepancy during training, but the discrepancy estimates suffer from high variance in stochastic settings, which can stifle the theoretical benefits of the method. This paper proposes Optimal Reordering of Data for Error-Reduced Estimation of Discrepancy (ORDERED), a novel unbiased stochastic variance reduction technique which reduces the discrepancy estimation error by optimising the order in which the training data are sampled. We consider two specific domain discrepancy losses (correlation alignment and the maximum mean discrepancy), formulate their stochastic estimation error as a function of the data sampling order, and propose a practical optimisation algorithm. Our simulations demonstrate reduced variance compared to related methods, and experiments on two domain shift image classification benchmarks show improved target domain accuracy.

AI.tldr: ORDERED is a variance reduction technique for unsupervised domain adaptation that reduces discrepancy estimation error by optimizing the sampling order of training data, demonstrating improved accuracy on domain shift benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Domain shift hinders real-world deployment of ML models; UDA addresses this via domain discrepancy minimization, but stochastic estimation suffers from high variance that undermines theoretical benefits.
AI.method: Proposes ORDERED, an unbiased stochastic variance reduction technique that optimizes the data sampling order to reduce estimation error for two discrepancy losses (correlation alignment and maximum mean discrepancy).
AI.result: Simulations show reduced variance compared to related methods; experiments on two domain shift image classification benchmarks show improved target domain accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Ordered data sampling effectively reduces discrepancy estimation variance and improves UDA performance, offering a practical optimization algorithm for domain adaptation.

title: Gated Multimodal Learning for Interpretable Property Energy Performance Prediction and Retrofit Scenario Analysis
summary: Achieving resilient and sustainable cities requires scalable approaches to decarbonising residential buildings, which account for about 20% of UK greenhouse gas emissions and 25% of energy-related emissions in the European Union. Energy Performance Certificates (EPCs) support regulation and retrofit planning, but their reliance on on-site inspections limits timely city-scale assessment. This study introduces a gated multimodal model to predict Standard Assessment Procedure (SAP) energy efficiency and Environmental Impact (EI) scores by integrating EPC tabular variables, assessor-written free text, and Geographic Information System (GIS)-derived spatial features describing footprint geometry, height, area, and orientation. Sample-wise gating learns property-specific modality weights, while an auxiliary band classification head stabilises training. In a Westminster, London case study, the model predicts SAP and EI scores with MAEs of 4.03 and 4.76 points and R2 values of 0.757 and 0.748, respectively, achieving a mean MAE of 4.39. Ablation results show that full multimodal fusion outperforms unimodal and bimodal baselines for both score prediction and band-level classification. Interpretability analyses provide decision-relevant evidence: gating weights indicate strong reliance on assessor text; SHAP highlights main fuel, built form, and construction age band; text occlusion prioritises roof and wall fields; and spatial attribution is dominated by height and footprint area, with sensitivity to footprint shape. The validated framework is further applied to retrofit scenarios for wall insulation, roof insulation, and window glazing upgrades, indicating projected improvements in SAP, EI, annual energy cost, and equivalent CO2 emissions. Overall, the framework provides scalable property-level evidence for retrofit screening, intervention prioritisation, and net-zero housing transitions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a gated multimodal model integrating EPC tabular data, assessor text, and GIS spatial features to predict building energy efficiency and environmental impact scores. Tested in Westminster, London, it achieves strong predictive performance (MAE ~4.39, R² ~0.75), outperforms unimodal/bimodal baselines, and is applied to retrofit scenario analysis.
AI.motivation: Decarbonising residential buildings is critical for sustainable cities, but current EPC-based assessment relies on costly on-site inspections, limiting scalability for city-level analysis. There is a need for a scalable, accurate method to predict energy performance without full inspections.
AI.method: A gated multimodal deep learning model that fuses three modalities: (1) EPC tabular variables, (2) assessor-written free text, and (3) GIS-derived spatial features (footprint, height, area, orientation). Sample-wise gating learns property-specific modality weights, and an auxiliary band classification head stabilises training.
AI.result: In a Westminster case study, the model achieves MAEs of 4.03 and 4.76 and R² of 0.757 and 0.748 for SAP and EI scores. Full multimodal fusion outperforms unimodal and bimodal baselines. Interpretability analyses reveal reliance on assessor text, main fuel, built form, age band, roof/wall fields, height, and footprint area.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides scalable property-level evidence for retrofit screening, intervention prioritisation (wall insulation, roof insulation, window glazing), and supports net-zero housing transitions.

title: Unified Framework of Distributional Regret in Multi-Armed Bandits and Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted at the Conference of Learning Theory (COLT) 2026
summary: We study the distribution of regret in stochastic multi-armed bandits and episodic reinforcement learning through a unified framework. We formalize a distributional regret bound as a probabilistic guarantee that holds uniformly over all confidence levels $δ\in (0,1]$, thereby characterizing the regret distribution across the full range of $δ$. We present a simple UCBVI-style algorithm with exploration bonus $\min\{c_{1,k}/N, c_{2,k}/\sqrt{N}\}$, where $N$ denotes the visit count and $(c_{1,k},c_{2,k})$ are user-specified parameters. For arbitrary parameter sequences, we derive general gap-independent and gap-dependent distributional regret bounds, yielding a principled characterization of how the parameters control the trade-off between expected performance, tail risk, and instance-dependent behavior. In particular, our bounds achieve optimal trade-offs between expected and distributional regret in both minimax and instance-dependent regimes. As a special case, for multi-armed bandits with $A$ arms and horizon $T$, we obtain a distributional regret bound of order $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{AT}\log(1/δ))$, confirming the conjecture of Lattimore & Szepesvári (2020, Section 17.1) for the first time.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a unified framework for distributional regret in stochastic multi-armed bandits and episodic RL, proposing a UCBVI-style algorithm with a novel exploration bonus and obtaining optimal distributional regret bounds, including confirming a long-standing conjecture of O(√(AT) log(1/δ)).
AI.motivation: The authors aim to formalize distributional regret bounds that hold uniformly over all confidence levels δ, characterizing the full regret distribution. They also seek to confirm the conjecture of Lattimore & Szepesvári (2020) regarding distributional regret in multi-armed bandits.
AI.method: A UCBVI-style algorithm is introduced with an exploration bonus of min{c₁ₖ/N, c₂ₖ/√N}. The authors derive general gap-independent and gap-dependent distributional regret bounds for arbitrary parameter sequences (c₁ₖ, c₂ₖ), analyzing how parameters control the trade-off between expected performance, tail risk, and instance-dependent behavior.
AI.result: The bounds achieve optimal trade-offs between expected and distributional regret in both minimax and instance-dependent regimes. For multi-armed bandits with A arms and horizon T, they obtain O(√(AT) log(1/δ)), confirming the conjecture of Lattimore & Szepesvári (2020).
AI.conclusion: The work provides a principled characterization of distributional regret, offering optimal trade-offs and confirming a long-standing open conjecture. The unified framework covers both bandits and episodic RL.

title: Rollout Pass-Rate Control: Steering Binary-Reward RL Toward Its Most Informative Regime
summary: SWE-bench-style agentic reinforcement learning relies on expensive stateful trajectories, yet substantial compute is wasted on sampled rollout groups with skewed pass rates, where binary rewards provide a weak contrastive signal. We frame this inefficiency as a pass-rate control problem and show that a 50% pass rate is the most informative operating point: it maximizes reward entropy, the probability of surviving group filtering, RLOO advantage energy under GRPO, and success--failure contrastive structure. Guided by this principle, we propose Prefix Sampling (PS), which replays trajectory prefixes to steer skewed groups toward this regime: successful prefixes serve as head starts for mostly failing groups, while failing prefixes serve as handicaps for mostly passing groups.
